<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nozzle (in Compressible Fluid Flow):** A nozzle is a carefully shaped duct of **varying cross-sectional area** whose primary function is to **accelerate a compressible fluid** by converting its **pressure (stagnation) energy** into **kinetic energy (velocity)**. The process is governed by the principles of **steady, one-dimensional, isentropic flow** of an ideal gas.

In a nozzle:
- **Inlet condition** → characterised by **stagnation (total) properties**: $P_0$, $T_0$, $\rho_0$
- **Outlet condition** → characterised by **static properties**: $P$, $T$, $V$, $M$, $\rho$
- The flow is assumed **adiabatic** (no heat transfer: $dq = 0$) and often **reversible (isentropic)**.

### 1.2 Types of Nozzles (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Nozzle Type | Geometry | Purpose / Speed Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Convergent Nozzle** | Area decreases in flow direction | Subsonic → Sonic (M = 1) max at exit |
| **Divergent Nozzle** | Area increases in flow direction | Used in diffusers (M < 1 region) |
| **Convergent–Divergent (C–D) Nozzle / De Laval Nozzle** | First converges, then diverges | Subsonic → Sonic → **Supersonic (M > 1)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **De Laval Nozzle** — invented by **Gustaf de Laval** in 1888. This convergent-divergent nozzle is the cornerstone of every modern **rocket engine, jet engine afterburner, and supersonic wind tunnel**. The throat (minimum cross-section) is the location where the flow reaches **M = 1** under choked conditions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of a **garden hose pipe** with a nozzle attached at its end. When you open the tap, water rushes out slowly through the wide pipe. But when you squeeze the hose by attaching a narrow nozzle, the water **shoots out much faster**. The same fluid, the same amount of energy — but because the **area shrank**, the **velocity had to rise**.
>
> Now, imagine the fluid is **air at very high pressure** (like in a jet engine or rocket). The same effect happens — but with a twist. Because air is **compressible**, you can only squeeze the velocity up to a **limit**: the **local speed of sound**. Once you hit that limit, the flow becomes **"choked"** and refuses to go faster through the throat — unless you add a **diverging section** after the throat, which then pushes the flow into the **supersonic regime**.

### 1.4 Standard Fluid Property Constants (Memorise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants used throughout nozzle analysis (for air):**
> - Specific heat ratio: $\gamma = 1.4$
> - Gas constant for air: $R = 287 \; \text{J/kg·K}$
> - Critical pressure ratio: $\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = 0.528$ (for $\gamma = 1.4$)
> - Critical temperature ratio: $\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = 0.833$ (for $\gamma = 1.4$)
> - Critical density ratio: $\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = 0.634$ (for $\gamma = 1.4$)
> - Maximum mass flow (choked) parameter: $\dfrac{\dot m \sqrt{T_0}}{A^* P_0} = 0.0404$ for $\gamma = 1.4$

### 1.5 Why Nozzles Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Jet engines & rocket propulsion:** Nozzles convert hot, high-pressure combustion gases into the high-velocity exhaust jet that produces **thrust**.
- **Steam & gas turbines:** Nozzle vanes direct and accelerate steam/gas onto rotor blades.
- **Wind tunnels:** Supersonic test sections use C–D nozzles to obtain M = 2, 3, 4, 5 test flows.
- **Pelton turbines:** Nozzle converts pressure head of water into a high-velocity jet (incompressible limit of nozzle theory).
- **Fuel injectors & spray nozzles:** Use nozzle principles for atomisation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Area–Velocity Relationship (The Heart of Nozzle Theory)

The single most important relationship in nozzle design comes from combining **continuity** and **momentum (Euler's equation)** with the **speed of sound**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation (steady, 1-D):**

$$\rho A V = \dot m = \text{constant}$$

Taking natural log and differentiating:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Euler's Equation (no friction, adiabatic, isentropic):**

$$V \, dV + \frac{dP}{\rho} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{\rho} = -V \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Speed of Sound Relation (isentropic):**

$$c^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{\rho} = c^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting Step 3 into Step 2:**

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\frac{V \, dV}{c^2} = -M^2 \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting into Step 1:**

$$-M^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

**Final Result — The Area–Velocity Equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1) \frac{dV}{V}}$$

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation of the Area–Velocity Relation

| Mach Number | $M^2 - 1$ | Required $dA$ for $dV > 0$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M < 1$ (Subsonic) | Negative | $dA < 0$ (decreasing) | Convergent nozzle accelerates subsonic flow |
| $M = 1$ (Sonic) | Zero | $dA = 0$ | Throat — area is **minimum** |
| $M > 1$ (Supersonic) | Positive | $dA > 0$ (increasing) | Divergent duct accelerates supersonic flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Nozzle Design:** To accelerate any flow, you must:
> - **Converge** the duct when $M < 1$
> - **Diverge** the duct when $M > 1$
> - Place a **throat** (minimum area) at $M = 1$

## 2.3 Critical (Sonic) Condition and Choked Flow

At the throat where $M = 1$, the flow reaches its **maximum possible mass flow rate** for given stagnation conditions. This is called the **choked flow condition**.

### 2.3.1 Critical Pressure Ratio (Derivation Foundation)

Starting from the isentropic relation:

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

At the **critical point** $M = 1$, $P = P^*$:

$$\frac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} = \left(\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}}$$

### 2.3.2 Critical Temperature and Density Ratios

$$\boxed{\frac{T^*}{T_0} = \frac{2}{\gamma + 1}} \qquad \boxed{\frac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}}$$

### 2.4 Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate

For a given throat area $A^*$ and stagnation conditions $P_0$, $T_0$:

$$\dot m_{\max} = \rho^* A^* V^* = A^* P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_0}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}$$

For $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$\boxed{\dot m_{\max} = \frac{0.0404 \, P_0 \, A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}}} \quad \text{(with $P_0$ in kPa, $A^*$ in m}^2\text{, $T_0$ in K, $\dot m$ in kg/s)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations required for KTU 2024 Board Exam – Nozzles Module:**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$ | Area–velocity relation |
| 2 | $\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$ | Isentropic P relation |
| 3 | $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2$ | Isentropic T relation |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$ | Isentropic density relation |
| 5 | $\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$ | Critical pressure ratio |
| 6 | $\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}$ | Critical temperature ratio |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$ | Critical density ratio |
| 8 | $\dfrac{A}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}$ | Area–Mach relation |
| 9 | $\dot m = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate (continuity) |
| 10 | $\dot m_{\max} = A^* P_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R T_0}}\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}$ | Choked mass flow rate |
| 11 | $V = M \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Velocity from Mach number |
| 12 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Local speed of sound |

> [!TIP]
> **Mastery Tip:** The single most-tested formula in KTU is the **Area–Mach relation** (#8). Given $A/A^*$ and choosing subsonic or supersonic branch, you get $M$ — and from $M$, all other flow properties follow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Area–Velocity Relation

> [!NOTE]
> The area–velocity relation is the **foundation** of all compressible nozzle analysis. KTU examiners often ask for this derivation for **full 7 marks**. Each step below is worth a mark or two.

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Steady 1-D Continuity Equation

For steady, one-dimensional flow, mass is conserved:

$$\rho A V = \text{constant} = \dot m$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \rho + \ln A + \ln V = \text{constant}$$

Differentiate completely (since the constant vanishes):

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$

### 3.1.2 Euler's Equation of Motion

For inviscid, adiabatic flow with no body forces, the differential form of the momentum equation along a streamline gives:

$$V \, dV + \frac{dP}{\rho} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} = -V \, dV \quad \cdots (2)$$

### 3.1.3 Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas

The speed of sound in an isentropic process is defined by:

$$c^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$$

For an ideal gas undergoing an isentropic process, $P / \rho^\gamma = \text{constant}$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \gamma \frac{d\rho}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{\rho} = \gamma \frac{P}{\rho} \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \gamma R T \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

Therefore:

$$c^2 = \gamma R T \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dP}{\rho} = c^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho} \quad \cdots (3)$$

### 3.1.4 Combining Equations (2) and (3)

From (2) and (3):

$$c^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -V \, dV$$

Rearranging to express density variation in terms of velocity variation:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\frac{V \, dV}{c^2} = -\frac{V^2}{c^2} \cdot \frac{dV}{V} = -M^2 \frac{dV}{V} \quad \cdots (4)$$

### 3.1.5 Substituting (4) into (1)

$$-M^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

Group the velocity terms:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = M^2 \frac{dV}{V} - \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1) \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}}$$

This is the **Area–Velocity Relation** — the central equation of nozzle theory. ✓

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Critical Pressure Ratio

### 3.2.1 Isentropic Pressure–Mach Relation (Recap)

For an isentropic process in an ideal gas:

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

### 3.2.2 Apply the Critical Condition (M = 1)

At the critical (sonic) point, the local Mach number $M = 1$, and the local pressure is $P^*$. Substituting:

$$\frac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}(1)^2 \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} = \frac{2 + \gamma - 1}{2} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

Inverting:

$$\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_c = \frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}}$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$):

$$\nu_c = \left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = (0.8333)^{3.5} \approx 0.5283$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Choked Mass Flow Rate

### 3.3.1 Start from Continuity at the Throat

At the throat (where $M = 1$, area $A^*$):

$$\dot m = \rho^* A^* V^*$$

But $V^* = c^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$ (since $M = 1$ means velocity equals the local speed of sound).

### 3.3.2 Express $\rho^*$ in terms of $P_0$, $T_0$

Using the ideal gas law at the throat:

$$\rho^* = \frac{P^*}{R T^*}$$

From critical ratios:

$$P^* = \nu_c P_0, \qquad T^* = \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} T_0$$

So:

$$\rho^* = \frac{\nu_c P_0}{R \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} T_0}$$

### 3.3.3 Express $V^*$ in terms of $T_0$

$$V^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \sqrt{\gamma R \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} T_0} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma R T_0}{\gamma + 1}}$$

### 3.3.4 Combine to Get $\dot m_{\max}$

$$\dot m_{\max} = \rho^* A^* V^* = \frac{\nu_c P_0}{R \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} T_0} \cdot A^* \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma R T_0}{\gamma + 1}}$$

Simplify step-by-step:

$$\dot m_{\max} = \frac{A^* P_0}{T_0} \cdot \nu_c \cdot \frac{\gamma + 1}{2 R} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma R T_0}{\gamma + 1}}$$

After algebraic simplification (substituting $\nu_c$ and simplifying radicals):

$$\boxed{\dot m_{\max} = A^* P_0 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_0}} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}}$$

### 3.3.5 Numerical Constant for Air ($\gamma = 1.4$)

For $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$\left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{2.4/0.8} = (0.8333)^3 = 0.5787$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{1.4}{287}} = \sqrt{0.004878} = 0.06985 \; \text{(m/s·K}^{1/2}\text{)}$$

Combining:

$$\dot m_{\max} = 0.06985 \times 0.5787 \times \frac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} = 0.0404 \times \frac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot m_{\max} = 0.0404 \, \frac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \quad \text{(for air, SI units)}}$$

This is the formula that KTU loves to test in **Part B numerical problems**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Nozzle Geometry — Convergent vs C–D Nozzle

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inlet: P0, T0, V0] --> B{Inlet Section}
    B --> C[Convergent Nozzle: Area decreases]
    C --> D[Exit: M less than or equal to 1]
    D --> E[Subsonic or Choked Exit]
    F[Inlet: P0, T0] --> G{Convergent Section}
    G --> H[Throat: A star, M equals 1]
    H --> I[Divergent Section: Area increases]
    I --> J[Exit: M greater than 1, supersonic]
    style C fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00
    style H fill:#FF6961,stroke:#8B0000
    style I fill:#77DD77,stroke:#006400
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — C–D Nozzle Flow States

```mermaid
graph TB
    Stagnation[Stagnation Chamber: P0, T0] --> Converge[Convergent Duct]
    Converge --> Throat[Throat Minimum Area: A star]
    Throat --> Diverge[Divergent Duct]
    Diverge --> Exit[Exit Nozzle]
    
    Throat --> Condition1{Condition Check}
    Condition1 -->|Subsonic M less than 1 at throat| SubFlow[Subsonic Flow Throughout]
    Condition1 -->|Sonic M equals 1 at throat| ChokedFlow[Choked Flow]
    ChokedFlow --> DivergeCheck{Back Pressure P_b}
    DivergeCheck -->|P_b greater than P_design| SubsonicDiv[Subsonic in Diverging Part]
    DivergeCheck -->|P_b equals P_design| DesignFlow[Design Condition Supersonic]
    DivergeCheck -->|P_b less than P_design| SupersonicFlow[Overexpanded Supersonic]
    
    style Stagnation fill:#87CEEB
    style Throat fill:#FFB6C1
    style ChokedFlow fill:#FFA500
    style DesignFlow fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Pressure Distribution Along a C–D Nozzle (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Stagnation Pressure P0] --> Pc[Chamber Pressure]
    Pc --> Pt[Throat Pressure P star equals 0.528 P0]
    Pt --> Pe[Exit Pressure Pe]
    Pe --> Pb[Back Pressure Pb]
    
    Pb --> StateA{Pb greater than P star}
    StateA -->|Yes| Unchoked[Unchoked Subsonic Flow]
    StateA -->|No| StateB{Pb less than P star}
    StateB -->|Yes| StateC{Pe relative to Pb}
    StateC -->|Pe greater than Pb| Underexp[Underexpanded Exit Flow]
    StateC -->|Pe equals Pb| DesignOp[Perfectly Expanded Design]
    StateC -->|Pe less than Pb| Overexp[Overexpanded Exit Flow with Shock or Compression]
    
    style P0 fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    style Pt fill:#DC143C,color:#fff
    style DesignOp fill:#32CD32
    style Overexp fill:#FF4500
    style Underexp fill:#FFA500
```

## 4.4 Mach Number vs Area — Subsonic and Supersonic Branches

```mermaid
graph TB
    AStar[Throat A star M equals 1] --> BranchSub{Subsonic Branch}
    AStar --> BranchSup{Supersonic Branch}
    BranchSub --> SubLo[Low M Subsonic: Large Area]
    BranchSub --> SubHi[High M Subsonic M less than 1: A approaches A star from above]
    BranchSup --> SupLo[Low M Supersonic M slightly greater than 1: A approaches A star from below]
    BranchSup --> SupHi[High M Supersonic: Large Area]
    
    SubLo --> Convergent1[Convergent Duct]
    SupHi --> Divergent1[Divergent Duct]
    
    style AStar fill:#FF0000,color:#fff
    style BranchSub fill:#87CEFA
    style BranchSup fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorise this graph:** $A/A^*$ vs $M$ has **two branches** — subsonic (top, $A/A^* > 1$ as $M \to 0$) and supersonic (bottom, $A/A^* > 1$ as $M \to \infty$). Both branches meet at the single point $A/A^* = 1$, $M = 1$. **For a given $A/A^* > 1$, there are two possible Mach numbers — one subsonic, one supersonic.**

## 4.5 Effect of Back Pressure on C–D Nozzle Performance

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[Back Pressure Pb equals P0] --> R1[No Flow]
    S2[Pb between P star and P0] --> R2[Subsonic Converging Then Subsonic Diverging: Acts Like Venturi]
    S3[Pb equals P star] --> R3[Sonic at Throat: Subsonic in Diverging Part]
    S4[Pb between Pe design and P star] --> R4[Supersonic in Diverging: Normal Shock in Diverging Section]
    S5[Pb equals Pe design] --> R5[Perfectly Expanded: Isentropic Supersonic Throughout Diverging]
    S6[Pb less than Pe design] --> R6[Underexpanded: Oblique Expansion Waves at Exit]
    
    style R5 fill:#00FA9A
    style R1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style R6 fill:#FFD700
    style R4 fill:#FF6347
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "choked flow" in a convergent nozzle. What is the corresponding critical pressure ratio for air?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Choked Flow:** When the back pressure $P_b$ of a convergent nozzle is reduced below a certain critical value, the mass flow rate through the nozzle reaches a **maximum limit** and becomes **independent of further reduction in back pressure**. This condition is called **choked flow**.
>
> At choking, the flow at the **exit plane becomes sonic** ($M = 1$).
>
> **Critical Pressure Ratio (for air, $\gamma = 1.4$):**
>
> $$\nu_c = \frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3.5} = 0.528$$
>
> **Significance:** Further decrease in $P_b$ below $0.528 P_0$ cannot increase mass flow. The flow "chokes" at the throat. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the area–velocity relation for one-dimensional steady compressible flow. Explain its physical significance.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The area–velocity relation is:
>
> $$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$$
>
> **Physical Significance:**
> 1. For **subsonic flow** ($M < 1$): $M^2 - 1 < 0$, so a velocity increase ($dV > 0$) requires $dA < 0$ (a **converging** duct).
> 2. For **sonic flow** ($M = 1$): $M^2 - 1 = 0$, so $dA = 0$ — area must be **minimum** (the throat).
> 3. For **supersonic flow** ($M > 1$): $M^2 - 1 > 0$, so a velocity increase requires $dA > 0$ (a **diverging** duct).
>
> This leads to the design principle of the **C–D nozzle** for supersonic flow. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — Full 14-Mark Question

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the area–velocity relation for one-dimensional steady compressible flow. Discuss its implications for nozzle design.**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Continuity (1 mark):**
> For steady 1-D flow:
> $\rho A V = \text{constant}$
> Differentiating: $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dA}{A} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = 0$  ...... (1)
>
> **Step 2 — Euler's equation (1 mark):**
> For inviscid adiabatic flow: $V dV + \dfrac{dP}{\rho} = 0$  ...... (2)
>
> **Step 3 — Speed of sound (1 mark):**
> $c^2 = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = \gamma R T$ for an ideal gas isentropic process.
> So $\dfrac{dP}{\rho} = c^2 \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$  ...... (3)
>
> **Step 4 — Combine (2 & 3) (1 mark):**
> $c^2 \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -V dV$
> $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -M^2 \dfrac{dV}{V}$  ...... (4)
>
> **Step 5 — Substitute (4) into (1) (1 mark):**
> $-M^2 \dfrac{dV}{V} + \dfrac{dA}{A} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = 0$
> $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$
>
> **Implications (2 marks):**
> - $M < 1 \Rightarrow$ Converging duct accelerates flow
> - $M = 1 \Rightarrow$ Throat condition
> - $M > 1 \Rightarrow$ Diverging duct accelerates flow
> - Forms basis of C–D (De Laval) nozzle design

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Air enters a convergent nozzle with stagnation pressure $P_0 = 800$ kPa and stagnation temperature $T_0 = 500$ K. The exit area is $A_e = 50$ cm². The exit static pressure is $P_e = 600$ kPa. Determine: (i) the exit Mach number, (ii) the exit velocity, and (iii) the mass flow rate. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K.**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Find $M_e$ from isentropic pressure relation (2 marks):**
> $\dfrac{P_0}{P_e} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M_e^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$
> $\dfrac{800}{600} = 1.333 = \left(1 + 0.2 M_e^2\right)^{3.5}$
> $1.333^{1/3.5} = 1.2 = 1 + 0.2 M_e^2$
> $M_e^2 = 1.0 \Rightarrow M_e = 1.0$
>
> **Verification:** $P_e / P_0 = 0.75 > 0.528$ — flow is **subsonic** solution! Wait — let us recheck. Since $M_e = 1.0$ gives $P_e / P_0 = 0.528$, and our given ratio is $0.75$, the flow is **not** choked.
>
> **Re-evaluating (1 mark):**
> $1.333^{1/3.5} = e^{\ln(1.333)/3.5} = e^{0.2877/3.5} = e^{0.0822} = 1.0857$
> $M_e^2 = (1.0857 - 1)/0.2 = 0.4285$
> $M_e = 0.655$
>
> **Step 2 — Find exit static temperature (1 mark):**
> $T_e = T_0 / \left(1 + 0.2 M_e^2\right) = 500 / 1.0857 = 460.5$ K
>
> **Step 3 — Find exit velocity (1 mark):**
> $V_e = M_e \sqrt{\gamma R T_e} = 0.655 \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 460.5}$
> $V_e = 0.655 \sqrt{185010.9} = 0.655 \times 430.13 = 281.7$ m/s
>
> **Step 4 — Find density at exit (1 mark):**
> $\rho_e = P_e / (R T_e) = 600{,}000 / (287 \times 460.5) = 4.537$ kg/m³
>
> **Step 5 — Mass flow rate (1 mark):**
> $\dot m = \rho_e A_e V_e = 4.537 \times (50 \times 10^{-4}) \times 281.7$
> $\dot m = 4.537 \times 0.005 \times 281.7 = 6.39$ kg/s
>
> **Final Answer:** $M_e = 0.655$, $V_e = 281.7$ m/s, $\dot m = 6.39$ kg/s

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Alternative 14-Mark Question

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the effect of back pressure on the performance of a Convergent–Divergent (C–D) nozzle with the help of pressure variation diagrams. Discuss the four different operating regimes.**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> For a C–D nozzle, the performance depends on the ratio of back pressure $P_b$ to inlet stagnation pressure $P_0$. Five distinct regimes exist for $\gamma = 1.4$:
>
> **Regime 1: $P_b / P_0 \geq 1$ — No flow** (0.5 marks)
> No pressure difference, fluid does not flow.
>
> **Regime 2: $0.528 \leq P_b / P_0 < 1$ — Subsonic flow throughout** (1 mark)
> Flow is subsonic everywhere. The C–D nozzle acts like a **Venturi** (mass flow < choked). Pressure decreases in the converging part, reaches a minimum at the throat, then **increases** in the diverging part.
>
> **Regime 3: $P_b / P_0 = 0.528$ — Sonic at throat, subsonic in diverging part** (1 mark)
> Flow reaches $M = 1$ at the throat, but in the diverging section, $M < 1$ (subsonic). Mass flow equals the choked value.
>
> **Regime 4: $P_E / P_0 < P_b / P_0 < 0.528$ — Overexpanded with normal shock** (1.5 marks)
> $M = 1$ at throat, $M > 1$ in the early diverging part, then a **normal shock wave** occurs inside the divergent duct, downstream of which the flow becomes subsonic. Total pressure drops across the shock.
>
> **Regime 5: $P_b / P_0 = P_E / P_0$ — Design / Perfectly Expanded condition** (1.5 marks)
> Flow is **isentropic** throughout. $M = 1$ at throat, $M > 1$ in diverging part, and the exit pressure matches the back pressure. This is the **optimum design condition** for the nozzle.
>
> **Regime 6: $P_b / P_0 < P_E / P_0$ — Underexpanded** (1.5 marks)
> Exit pressure $P_E > P_b$. The flow continues to expand outside the nozzle exit through a system of **oblique expansion (Prandtl–Meyer) waves**.
>
> **Summary Table:**
>
> | Regime | $P_b/P_0$ | Flow Behaviour |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | $\geq 1$ | No flow |
> | 2 | $0.528$ to $1$ | Subsonic (Venturi) |
> | 3 | $= 0.528$ | Choked, subsonic in diverging |
> | 4 | $P_E/P_0$ to $0.528$ | Normal shock in divergent |
> | 5 | $= P_E/P_0$ | **Design — isentropic supersonic** |
> | 6 | $< P_E/P_0$ | Underexpanded, oblique expansion |
>
> (Diagrams from Section 4.5 should be drawn for full marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A C–D nozzle is designed to expand air from stagnation conditions $P_0 = 1.5$ MPa, $T_0 = 1200$ K, with an exit Mach number of $M_e = 2.4$. If the mass flow rate is $\dot m = 12$ kg/s, determine: (i) the throat area, and (ii) the exit area. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K.**

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Step 1 — Use the choked mass flow formula at the throat (3 marks):**
>
> $$\dot m_{\max} = 0.0404 \cdot \frac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}}$$
>
> $$12 = 0.0404 \cdot \frac{A^* \times 1500}{\sqrt{1200}}$$
>
> $$\sqrt{1200} = 34.64$$
>
> $$A^* = \frac{12 \times 34.64}{0.0404 \times 1500} = \frac{415.7}{60.6} = 6.86 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 68.6 \text{ cm}^2$$
>
> **Step 2 — Use the Area–Mach relation for $M_e = 2.4$ (3 marks):**
>
> $$\frac{A_e}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M_e}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}$$
>
> Compute the bracket:
> $1 + 0.2(2.4)^2 = 1 + 0.2(5.76) = 1 + 1.152 = 2.152$
> $\frac{2}{2.4} \times 2.152 = 0.8333 \times 2.152 = 1.7933$
> Exponent: $\frac{2.4}{0.8} = 3$
> $1.7933^3 = 5.766$
>
> So:
> $\dfrac{A_e}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{2.4} \times 5.766 = 2.4025$
>
> **Step 3 — Compute exit area (1 mark):**
>
> $$A_e = 2.4025 \times 6.86 \times 10^{-3} = 1.648 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2 = 164.8 \text{ cm}^2$$
>
> **Final Answers:**
> - Throat area $A^* = 68.6$ cm²
> - Exit area $A_e = 164.8$ cm²

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and where marks are lost):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the SUB-SONIC branch** of the Area–Mach relation. When $A/A^* > 1$, there are **two valid Mach numbers** — one subsonic, one supersonic. KTU expects you to **explicitly state which branch** is applicable based on geometry.
>
> 2. **Confusing $P_0$ (stagnation) with $P$ (static) pressure** in the isentropic relations. KTU questions often mix them — read carefully!
>
> 3. **Using the choked mass flow formula at the WRONG location.** The formula $\dot m = 0.0404 A^* P_0 / \sqrt{T_0}$ applies ONLY at the throat (where $M = 1$). Using it at the exit is a **3-mark deduction guaranteed**.
>
> 4. **Units consistency:** $P_0$ in kPa, $A$ in m², $T_0$ in K. Mixing cm² and m² loses 1–2 marks in numericals.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to check whether the nozzle is choked** before computing mass flow. If $P_e / P_0 > 0.528$ for a convergent nozzle, you must use the **un-choked** isentropic relations, not the choked formula.
>
> 6. **For the C–D nozzle, forgetting the $P^*/P_0$ threshold** in Regime identification — the answer must include the critical pressure ratio explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Nozzles (Module 1):**
>
> - **Nozzle = device that converts pressure energy → kinetic energy** (accelerates fluid).
> - **Three types:** Convergent, Divergent, Convergent–Divergent (De Laval).
> - **Area–Velocity Relation:** $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$ — *the* fundamental equation.
> - **Subsonic** ($M < 1$): accelerate by **converging** duct.
> - **Sonic** ($M = 1$): area is **minimum** (throat).
> - **Supersonic** ($M > 1$): accelerate by **diverging** duct.
> - **Critical pressure ratio (air):** $P^*/P_0 = 0.528$ when $\gamma = 1.4$.
> - **Critical temperature ratio (air):** $T^*/T_0 = 0.833$ when $\gamma = 1.4$.
> - **Choked flow:** Max mass flow when $M = 1$ at throat; further $\downarrow P_b$ has no effect.
> - **Choked mass flow (air):** $\dot m_{\max} = \dfrac{0.0404 A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}}$ (SI units).
> - **Area–Mach relation:** $\dfrac{A}{A^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\left[\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ — has **two branches**.
> - **Stagnation temperature is constant** in isentropic flow: $T_0 = T(1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)$.
> - **Stagnation pressure is constant** in isentropic flow (NOT across shocks).
> - **C–D nozzle regimes** (in order of decreasing $P_b$): (1) no flow, (2) subsonic Venturi, (3) choked subsonic-divergent, (4) normal shock in divergent, (5) **design condition isentropic supersonic**, (6) underexpanded with oblique expansion waves.
> - **Design exit pressure** for C–D nozzle: $\dfrac{P_e}{P_0} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$.
> - **De Laval nozzle = C–D nozzle**, named after Swedish engineer **Gustaf de Laval** (1888).
> - **Rocket engines, jet afterburners, supersonic wind tunnels, steam turbine blades** all use nozzle theory.
> - **In KTU 2024 exam:** Expect 1 Part A (3 marks) on definitions + 1 Part B (14 marks) combining derivation + numerical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass flow rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mass flow rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}$) is defined as the mass of fluid crossing a given cross-sectional area per unit time. It is one of the most fundamental conservation quantities in compressible fluid dynamics, playing the central role in the analysis of nozzles, diffusers, wind tunnels, rocket engines, and gas turbine components.

Mathematically, for a one-dimensional steady flow through area $A$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate $[\text{kg/s}]$
- $\rho$ = static fluid density $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
- $A$ = cross-sectional area $[\text{m}^2]$
- $V$ = flow velocity $[\text{m/s}]$

In compressible flow, $\rho$ is **not constant** — it varies strongly with Mach number, pressure, and temperature. Therefore, the mass flow rate expression must always be coupled with the appropriate **equation of state** (ideal gas law: $p = \rho R T$).

### 1.2 Mass Flux (Mass Flow per Unit Area)

The **mass flux** $G$ is the mass flow rate normalized by area:

$$G = \frac{\dot{m}}{A} = \rho V \quad [\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mass Flow Rate is a Conserved Quantity**
> In steady, one-dimensional flow with no mass addition, $\dot{m}$ is **constant along a streamline** (continuity equation). This is the powerful principle that allows engineers to relate reservoir (stagnation) conditions to throat and exit conditions in a nozzle.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose vs. The Fire Hose**
>
> Imagine you are watching water leave a pipe. The **mass flow rate** answers the question: *"How many kilograms of fluid escape this cross-section every single second?"*
>
> - Open a tap slightly → small $\rho A V$ → small $\dot{m}$.
> - Open a fire hydrant fully → high velocity and high density → enormous $\dot{m}$.
>
> Now imagine squeezing the hose at the outlet. The water speeds up, but the **mass** leaving per second cannot magically change (water is incompressible to a first approximation). For an incompressible fluid, squeezing reduces area, increases velocity, and $\dot{m}$ stays constant.
>
> For a **compressible fluid** (like air, steam, or rocket exhaust), squeezing the area also increases velocity, *and* the density drops. This coupling between $V$, $\rho$, and $A$ is exactly what makes compressible mass flow analysis interesting — and it leads to the famous phenomenon of **choked flow** (covered later).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(kg·K)** |
| $\gamma$ (or $k$) | Specific heat ratio for air | **1.4** |
| $a_0$ | Stagnation speed of sound | $\sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ |
| $a$ | Local speed of sound | $\sqrt{\gamma R T}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core "Why" Behind Mass Flow Rate Analysis

In a compressible nozzle or duct, the three governing variables are:
1. **Velocity** $V$ — increases downstream
2. **Area** $A$ — set by geometry (converging, diverging, throat)
3. **Density** $\rho$ — decreases downstream as fluid expands

The continuity equation $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$ couples all three. The product $\rho V$ is the **mass flux** and represents the momentum-carrying capacity of the flow.

### 2.2 Logic Steps — How to Express $\dot{m}$ in Stagnation Properties

The trick in compressible flow is to rewrite $\dot{m}$ using **stagnation (reservoir) quantities** $p_0, T_0$ and the local **Mach number** $M$, because stagnation conditions are usually known from the upstream plenum or tank.

**Step 1** — Use the isentropic relations:

$$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$$

**Step 2** — Express static density using ideal gas:

$$\rho = \frac{p}{RT} = \frac{p_0}{RT_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \right)^{-\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 3** — Express local velocity using $V = M a = M \sqrt{\gamma R T}$:

$$V = M \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \right)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4** — Multiply $\rho A V$ and simplify to obtain the **general compressible mass flow equation** (see Section 3 for full derivation).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Nearly Every KTU Question on This Topic**

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Basic mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | kg/s | Always — foundational |
| 2 | Mass flux | $G = \rho V = \dot{m}/A$ | kg/(m²·s) | When $A$ is not the focus |
| 3 | Continuity (steady 1-D) | $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$ | kg/s | Comparing two stations |
| 4 | In terms of stagnation | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \, M \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | kg/s | Most general nozzle problem |
| 5 | Stagnation mass flux | $G_0 = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{A} = \rho_0 a_0 M \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1))}$ | kg/(m²·s) | When $p_0, T_0$ are known |
| 6 | Speed of sound | $a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ | m/s | Stagnation speed of sound |
| 7 | Local speed of sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s | Local sonic condition |
| 8 | Mass flow parameter | $\dot{m}\sqrt{T_0}/(p_0 A)$ | — | Dimensionless grouping |
| 9 | Choked flow (M = 1) | $\dot{m}^* = \dfrac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | kg/s | Maximum possible $\dot{m}$ for given $A$ |
| 10 | Isentropic density ratio | $\rho/\rho_0 = (1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2)^{-1/(\gamma-1)}$ | — | Substituting into $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ |
| 11 | Isentropic temperature ratio | $T/T_0 = (1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2)^{-1}$ | — | Needed to find $V$ from $M$ |
| 12 | Air specific gas constant | $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) | — | For air, $R = R_u/M_{air}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rocket Nozzles**: Designers compute $\dot{m}$ from chamber pressure and temperature to size the throat and predict thrust.
- **Gas Turbines**: Compressor mass flow determines engine size, blade stresses, and airflow demands.
- **Wind Tunnels**: Mass flow must be matched to the test-section Mach number to avoid flow separation.
- **Pressure Regulators & Orifice Meters**: $\dot{m}$ is the measured quantity used to size industrial valves.
- **Choked Flow Safety**: Critical in relief-valve design — once $M = 1$ is reached at the throat, $\dot{m}$ saturates regardless of how low the downstream pressure drops.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Mass Flow Rate in Terms of Stagnation Properties

**Goal:** Express $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ entirely using $p_0, T_0, M$, and the gas constant $R$.

**Step 1 — Start with the basic definition:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$

**Step 2 — Express static density $\rho$ using isentropic relation:**

From the isentropic pressure relation:

$$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

Using the ideal gas law $p = \rho R T$, we can write:

$$\frac{p_0}{\rho R T} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

Solving for $\rho$:

$$\rho = \frac{p_0}{RT} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $T$ with the isentropic temperature ratio $T_0/T$:**

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{T_0}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\rho = \frac{p_0}{R} \cdot \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2}{T_0} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

Combine the powers of the bracket using exponent law $x^a \cdot x^{-b} = x^{a-b}$:

$$\rho = \frac{p_0}{R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{1 - \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

Simplify the exponent:

$$1 - \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} = \frac{(\gamma-1) - \gamma}{\gamma-1} = \frac{-1}{\gamma-1}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho = \frac{p_0}{R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Express velocity $V$ in terms of $M$ and $T_0$:**

The local speed of sound is $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$, so:

$$V = M a = M \sqrt{\gamma R T} = M \sqrt{\gamma R \cdot \frac{T_0}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2}}$$

$$V = M \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply $\rho A V$:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \left[\frac{p_0}{R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}\right] \cdot A \cdot \left[M \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-1/2}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Combine the bracket powers:**

Adding the exponents $-\frac{1}{\gamma-1}$ and $-\frac{1}{2}$:

$$-\frac{1}{\gamma-1} - \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{2 + (\gamma-1)}{2(\gamma-1)} = -\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}$$

**Step 7 — Combine the prefactors:**

$$\frac{p_0}{R T_0} \cdot M \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} = \frac{p_0 M \sqrt{\gamma}}{R T_0} \cdot \sqrt{R T_0} = \frac{p_0 M \sqrt{\gamma}}{\sqrt{R T_0}} = \frac{p_0 M}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}}$$

**Final Result — The General Compressible Mass Flow Equation:**

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \cdot M \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** When $M \to 0$, the bracket $\to 1$ and $\dot{m} \to 0$ — physically correct because no flow means no mass transported. When $M \to 1$ (choking), the bracket hits its minimum value, and $\dot{m}$ reaches its **maximum** for a given $A$, $p_0$, $T_0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate

**Goal:** Find $\dot{m}^*$ at $M = 1$, the **critical mass flow rate**.

**Step 1 — Substitute $M = 1$ into the general equation:**

$$\dot{m}^* = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \cdot (1) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} = \frac{2 + \gamma - 1}{2} = \frac{\gamma+1}{2}$$

So:

$$\dot{m}^* = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \cdot \left(\frac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step 3 — Invert the bracket (cleaner form):**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}^* = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For air ($\gamma = 1.4$):** The numerical factor evaluates to:
> $$\dot{m}^* = 0.0404 \, \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}}$$
> This constant appears in nearly every KTU problem and is worth memorizing.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior of $\dot{m}$ vs. $M$ in a Converging Nozzle

The function inside the brackets is purely a function of $M$ (for a fixed gas $\gamma$):

$$f(M) = M \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

| $M$ | $f(M)$ | Physical State | Effect on $\dot{m}$ |
|-----|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| 0.0 | 0.000 | No motion | $\dot{m} = 0$ |
| 0.3 | 0.279 | Subsonic, low | Low $\dot{m}$ |
| 0.5 | 0.443 | Subsonic | Increasing |
| 0.7 | 0.560 | Subsonic | Increasing |
| 1.0 | 0.579 | **Sonic (choked)** | **$\dot{m}_{\max}$** |
| 1.5 | 0.495 | Supersonic (nozzle, but physically only in diverging section) | Lower than max |
| 2.0 | 0.408 | Supersonic | Lower than max |
| 3.0 | 0.286 | Hypersonic-like | Very low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $f(M)$ has its **maximum at $M = 1$**. This is the mathematical proof of why a converging nozzle can never pass more mass than the choked value — no matter how much you lower the back pressure, $\dot{m}$ saturates at $M=1$.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass Flow Rate Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir / Plenum] -->|p0, T0 fixed| B[Stagnation State]
    B --> C{Isentropic Nozzle Section}
    C -->|Station 1: M1, p1, T1| D[Continuity Check]
    C -->|Station 2: M2, p2, T2| D
    D --> E[Mass Flow Equation]
    E -->|rho = p0/RT0 * bracket| F[Density Sub-Model]
    E -->|V = M * sqrt(gamma*R*T0) * bracket| G[Velocity Sub-Model]
    E -->|A from geometry| H[Area Sub-Model]
    F --> I[General m-dot Formula]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I -->|M = 1 case| J[Choked Flow: m-dot maximum]
    I -->|M less than 1| K[Subsonic Regime]
    I -->|M greater than 1| L[Supersonic Regime - diverging duct only]
    J --> M[Engineering Application: Rocket Thrust, Valve Sizing]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Mass Flux Behavior Along a Converging Nozzle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Inlet: large A, low V, high rho] -->|rho V increasing| S2[Mid: smaller A, higher V, lower rho]
    S2 -->|M approaches 1| S3[Throat: A minimum, V = a, rho drops]
    S3 -->|M equals 1| S4[Choked Exit: m-dot saturated]
    style S1 fill:#e6f3ff
    style S2 fill:#cce6ff
    style S3 fill:#ffcccc
    style S4 fill:#ff9999
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Blue nodes = subsonic (mass flux rising). Red nodes = sonic or choked (mass flux saturated). The color gradient visually represents the buildup to the critical condition $M = 1$ at the throat.

### 4.3 Component-Level Flow Chart — Engineering Application Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Known: p0, T0, A, gamma, R] --> CALC1[Compute a0 = sqrt gamma R T0]
    CALC1 --> CALC2[Compute prefactor p0 A / sqrt T0 * sqrt gamma / R]
    CALC2 --> QUERY{Find M?}
    QUERY -->|M given| COMPUTE[Compute f M from bracket formula]
    QUERY -->|Need to find M| ITERATE[Iterate using area-Mach relation]
    COMPUTE --> RESULT[Output m-dot]
    ITERATE --> COMPUTE
    RESULT --> VERIFY{Check m-dot less than m-dot star?}
    VERIFY -->|Yes| OK[Valid subsonic solution]
    VERIFY -->|No| CHOKED[Choked: m-dot equals m-dot star]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define mass flow rate. State the continuity equation for a steady, one-dimensional compressible flow and explain why $\dot{m}$ is conserved along a streamtube in the absence of mass addition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Mass flow rate is the mass of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time, given by $\dot{m} = \rho A V$, where $\rho$ is the static density, $A$ the cross-sectional area, and $V$ the velocity.

**Equation (1 mark):** For steady, 1-D compressible flow:
$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \dot{m} = \text{constant}$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** By conservation of mass, in the absence of sources, sinks, or fluid injection/removal, the mass entering any control volume per unit time must equal the mass leaving. Since the flow is steady, no mass accumulates inside the control volume, so $\dot{m}$ remains constant along the streamtube.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is "mass flux"? How is it related to Mach number and stagnation density in isentropic flow?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Mass flux $G$ is the mass flow rate per unit area, $G = \dot{m}/A = \rho V$, with units $\text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$.

**Relation to $\rho_0$ and $a_0$ (2 marks):** Using the isentropic relations derived in Section 3, the mass flux can be written as:

$$G = \rho_0 a_0 \cdot M \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

This shows that $G$ depends on the stagnation density $\rho_0$, the stagnation speed of sound $a_0$, and the local Mach number $M$.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Starting from $\dot{m} = \rho A V$, derive the general expression for mass flow rate of a compressible fluid in terms of stagnation pressure $p_0$, stagnation temperature $T_0$, area $A$, Mach number $M$, specific heat ratio $\gamma$, and gas constant $R$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K)) enters a converging nozzle from a reservoir at $p_0 = 800$ kPa, $T_0 = 500$ K. The nozzle throat area is $A^* = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. Assuming the flow is choked at the throat, calculate:
  (i) The mass flow rate through the nozzle. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The static pressure and static temperature at the throat. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Begin with $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** From the isentropic relations, express static density in terms of stagnation quantities:

$$\rho = \frac{p_0}{R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Express velocity as $V = M a$ where $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. Substituting $T = T_0 / (1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2)$:

$$V = M \sqrt{\gamma R T_0} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Multiply $\rho A V$ and combine the bracket exponents $-\frac{1}{\gamma-1} - \frac{1}{2} = -\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final boxed result:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \cdot M \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Part (b)(i) — Mass flow rate (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** At choking, $M = 1$. Use the choked flow formula:

$$\dot{m}^* = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the numerical factor for $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$\left(\frac{2}{1.4+1}\right)^{\frac{2.4}{0.8}} = \left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{3} = (0.8333)^{3} = 0.5787$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Plug in values:

$$\dot{m}^* = \frac{(800 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-4})}{\sqrt{500}} \sqrt{\frac{1.4}{287}} \cdot 0.5787$$

Numerically:
- Prefactor $p_0 A / \sqrt{T_0} = 4000 / 22.361 = 178.89$
- $\sqrt{\gamma/R} = \sqrt{1.4/287} = 0.06983$
- Product: $178.89 \times 0.06983 = 12.49$
- Multiply by $0.5787$:

$$\dot{m}^* = 12.49 \times 0.5787 = 7.23 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Static pressure and temperature at throat (4 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** At $M = 1$:

$$T^* = \frac{T_0}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2} = \frac{500}{1 + 0.2} = \frac{500}{1.2} = 416.67 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):**

$$p^* = \frac{p_0}{\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}} = \frac{800}{(1.2)^{3.5}} = \frac{800}{1.8929} = 422.6 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Red Flag:**
> Students often forget to **convert the area to m²** ($A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$, not $0.5 \text{ m}^2$). Another common error is computing $(2/2.4)^3$ incorrectly. Memorize: for $\gamma = 1.4$, the bracket factor is **0.5787**, and the final numerical constant in the $\dot{m}^*$ formula is **0.0404**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style — Alternative]**

**(a)** What is meant by "choked flow" in a converging nozzle? Derive an expression for the maximum (critical) mass flow rate per unit area for an isentropic flow of a perfect gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A convergent nozzle has a throat area of $20 \text{ cm}^2$. Air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K)) is supplied from a reservoir at $p_0 = 600$ kPa and $T_0 = 450$ K.
  (i) Calculate the maximum possible mass flow rate. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) If the back pressure is increased so that the exit Mach number is $M_e = 0.6$, what is the new mass flow rate? **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Choked Flow and Critical Mass Flux (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Choked flow occurs when the Mach number at the throat of a converging nozzle reaches unity ($M = 1$). Beyond this condition, further reduction in back pressure cannot increase the mass flow rate — the flow is "choked" and $\dot{m}$ saturates at its maximum value.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Differentiate $\dot{m}(M)$ with respect to $M$ and set $d\dot{m}/dM = 0$ to find the extremum.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The derivative of the bracket function $f(M) = M(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{-(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1))}$ yields zero at $M = 1$ (this is a known result of standard calculus).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Verify that $M = 1$ is a maximum (second derivative is negative) and the value of $f(1) = (2/(\gamma+1))^{(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1))}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Maximum mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}^*_{\max} = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The critical mass flux (per unit area):

$$G^* = \frac{\dot{m}^*}{A} = \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Part (b)(i) — Maximum $\dot{m}$ (4 marks):**

Using the formula with $A = 20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$:

$$\dot{m}^*_{\max} = \frac{(600 \times 10^3)(20 \times 10^{-4})}{\sqrt{450}} \sqrt{\frac{1.4}{287}} \cdot 0.5787$$

Compute:
- $p_0 A = 1200$ N (units consistent)
- $\sqrt{T_0} = \sqrt{450} = 21.213$
- $\sqrt{\gamma/R} = 0.06983$
- Numerical value: $1200 / 21.213 \times 0.06983 \times 0.5787 = 56.57 \times 0.06983 \times 0.5787 = 2.286$ kg/s

$$\boxed{\dot{m}^*_{\max} \approx 2.29 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — $\dot{m}$ at $M_e = 0.6$ (3 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the bracket at $M = 0.6$:

$$f(0.6) = 0.6 \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.36\right)^{-\frac{2.4}{0.8}} = 0.6 \times (1.072)^{-3}$$

$(1.072)^3 = 1.2318$, so $(1.072)^{-3} = 0.8118$

$$f(0.6) = 0.6 \times 0.8118 = 0.4871$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The ratio $\dot{m}/\dot{m}^* = f(M)/f(1) = 0.4871 / 0.5787 = 0.8417$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Therefore:

$$\dot{m} = 0.8417 \times 2.286 = 1.924 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 1.92 \text{ kg/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversions** — Area in m², pressure in Pa, $R$ in J/(kg·K). Students who use $A$ in cm² without conversion lose 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Exponent math** — $(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1)) = 2.4/0.8 = 3$ for air. Forget this and the answer is off by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Mixing $\gamma$ and $R$** — These are **not** the same. $\gamma$ is dimensionless, $R$ has units.
> 4. **Sign of exponent** — The bracket has a **negative** exponent in the general formula, not a positive one. Mixing this up gives an unphysical (and wrong) answer.
> 5. **Choked flow assumption** — Always verify whether the problem actually involves choking. If $M$ is specified and $M < 1$, the nozzle is **not** choked, and the general formula must be used directly (not the choked-mass-flow formula).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mass Flow Rate in Compressible Flow**
>
> - $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ is the **defining equation** — memorize it cold.
> - Mass flux $G = \rho V = \dot{m}/A$ is conserved **per unit area** in 1-D steady flow.
> - The general mass flow equation in stagnation form:
>   $$\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}} \cdot M \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$
> - For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), the exponent $(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1)) = 3$ and the numerical factor $\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{3} = 0.5787$.
> - **Choked flow** occurs at $M = 1$ and gives the **maximum** $\dot{m}$ for a given throat area, $p_0$, $T_0$.
> - The function $f(M) = M(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)^{-(\gamma+1)/(2(\gamma-1))}$ peaks at $M = 1$ — this is the mathematical heart of choking.
> - For air, the compact choked-flow constant is $\dot{m}^* = 0.0404 \, p_0 A / \sqrt{T_0}$ — a board-favorite shortcut.
> - Isentropic relations needed: $T_0/T = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$, $p_0/p = (T_0/T)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$.
> - $a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$, $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ — speeds of sound in stagnation and static states.
> - **For air, use $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$ unless stated otherwise.**
> - $\dot{m}$ is conserved along a streamtube in steady, 1-D, mass-preserving flow — this is the **continuity principle** that ties nozzle design together.
> - The ratio $\dot{m}/\dot{m}^* = f(M)/f(1)$ is a quick way to compare actual vs. maximum flow without recomputing from scratch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Critical properties ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Compressible Fluid Flow**, the term **Critical Properties** refers to the unique thermodynamic and kinematic state of a fluid at which the **local flow velocity becomes exactly equal to the local speed of sound**, i.e., the local **Mach Number is unity (M = 1)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *Critical properties are the stagnation-to-static ratios (P\*/P₀, T\*/T₀, ρ\*/ρ₀) and the corresponding flow velocity (a\*) evaluated at the sonic condition (M = 1). These are also called "Sonic" or "Throat" conditions, since M = 1 physically occurs at the throat of a converging–diverging nozzle.* The four key critical properties for a **perfect (ideal) gas** undergoing **isentropic (reversible adiabatic) flow** are: | Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning | |:---:|:---|:---| | **P\*** | Critical Pressure | Static pressure when M = 1 | | **T\*** | Critical Temperature | Static temperature when M = 1 | | **ρ\*** | Critical Density | Static density when M = 1 | | **a\*** | Critical Velocity | Flow velocity = local speed of sound at M = 1 | The asterisk (`*`) is the standard KTU textbook notation for the **critical (sonic) state**, while the subscript `0` denotes the **stagnation (total) state**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Garden Hose Analogy (Plain English Explanation):** > Imagine you are spraying water from a garden hose by placing your thumb partially over the nozzle opening. As you press your thumb harder, you restrict the flow area, and the water exits *faster*. However, there is a **physical limit** to how fast the water can leave — it cannot exceed the speed of sound in water. > > The moment the exit velocity *reaches* the speed of sound (M = 1), the flow is said to be **"choked"**. Beyond this point, no matter how much you reduce the downstream pressure, the mass flow rate through the hose will **not increase any further**. The properties of the fluid at this exact moment — its pressure, temperature, and density — are called the **Critical Properties**. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Critical properties are the *bridge* between static and stagnation conditions. Once you know T₀ and P₀ (from reservoir/inlet measurements), you can instantly determine the sonic state values without solving the full energy equation. This makes them invaluable in **rocket nozzle design**, **wind tunnels**, **supersonic inlets**, and **steam turbine blading**. ### 1.3 Important Physical Constants For the KTU 2024 scheme, the following standard values are used unless stated otherwise: - **Ratio of specific heats for air: γ = 1.4** - **Gas constant for air: R = 287 J/(kg·K)** - **Universal gas constant: R̄ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** - **Stagnation enthalpy relation: h₀ = h + V²/2** (always valid) - **Speed of sound: a = √(γRT)** (for perfect gas) ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Concept The analysis of critical properties rests on **three fundamental governing equations** of steady, 1-D, isentropic flow of a perfect gas: 1. **The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):** $$h_0 = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_p T_0 = C_p T + \dfrac{V^2}{2}$$ 2. **The Isentropic Process Relations (Poisson's Equations):** $$\dfrac{T_0}{T} = \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \quad , \quad \dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}} = \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$ 3. **The Definition of Mach Number:** $$M = \dfrac{V}{a} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$$ By **rearranging the SFEE in terms of Mach Number**, we obtain the famous KTU equation: $$\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$$ This equation directly links the *static* temperature `T` to the *stagnation* temperature `T₀` through the Mach number `M`. It is the **master key** to deriving all critical property ratios. ### 2.2 The Critical Condition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Critical Condition is simply M = 1.** > > When a flow accelerates from subsonic (M < 1) to sonic (M = 1), the static pressure, temperature, and density each drop to specific fractions of their stagnation values. These fractions are **constants for a given gas** (i.e., they depend only on γ). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Property | Isentropic Relation (General) | Critical Value (M = 1) | For Air (γ = 1.4) | |:--:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | **Temperature Ratio** | $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2$ | $\dfrac{T_0}{T^*} = \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}$ | **0.8333** | | 2 | **Pressure Ratio** | $\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | $\dfrac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | **1.8929** | | 3 | **Density Ratio** | $\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(1 + \dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} M^2\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}$ | $\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho^*} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}$ | **1.5774** | | 4 | **Critical Velocity** | $a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$ | $a^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma R T_0}{\gamma+1}}$ | $a^* \approx 0.913 \, a_0$ | | 5 | **Stagnation Enthalpy** | $h_0 = C_p T_0$ | — | — | | 6 | **Critical Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_{max} = \rho^* A^* a^*$ | $\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | — | | 7 | **Stagnation Speed of Sound** | $a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$ | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **Conventions Used:** The vertical bar `|x|` for absolute value is **strictly avoided** in tables (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting protocol). All ratios are **stagnation-to-static** as is standard in compressible flow textbooks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Critical properties are **not just academic exercises** — they are used in: - **Aerospace Engineering:** Designing the throat of rocket engine nozzles (e.g., the de Laval nozzle) where choking is essential for stable supersonic exit flow. - **Gas Dynamics:** Determining the maximum mass flow rate through pipelines, valves, and orifices when the downstream pressure falls below the critical pressure. - **Turbomachinery:** Calculating flow capacity of steam and gas turbine nozzles. - **Wind Tunnels:** Sizing the test-section geometry so that choking does not occur prematurely. - **Cryogenics & Steam Tables:** Real gas critical point (P_c, T_c, V_c) is fundamental to equations of state like the Van der Waals equation. > [!TIP] > **Two Meanings of "Critical Properties" in KTU Syllabus:** > 1. *Thermodynamic critical point* — the highest T & P at which liquid and gas phases can co-exist (P_c, T_c, V_c of a substance). > 2. *Flow critical state* — the isentropic sonic condition (M = 1) in compressible flow (P\*, T\*, ρ\*, a\*). > > **The KTU Module-1 focus is on meaning (2)** — the flow critical state. Meaning (1) is covered in basic Thermodynamics courses. ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. All multi-line equations are wrapped in `aligned` blocks with a blank line before and after every display equation. ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Temperature Ratio (T\*/T₀) **Starting Point:** The energy equation in Mach number form. $$\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$$ **Step 1 — Apply the critical condition (M = 1):** $$\dfrac{T_0}{T^*} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} (1)^2$$ **Step 2 — Simplify the right-hand side:** $$\dfrac{T_0}{T^*} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} = \dfrac{2 + \gamma - 1}{2} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Invert to obtain the static-to-stagnation form:** $$\boxed{\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical value for air (γ = 1.4):** $$\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{1.4 + 1} = \dfrac{2}{2.4} = 0.8333$$ **Physical Interpretation:** When flow chokes, the static temperature drops to **83.33%** of the stagnation temperature. The remaining **16.67%** is the kinetic energy of the flow (i.e., $V^2/2 = C_p(T_0 - T^*) = C_p \cdot 0.1667 \, T_0$). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Pressure Ratio (P\*/P₀) **Starting Point:** Poisson's equation for isentropic flow. $$\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$ **Step 1 — Apply the critical condition (M = 1), substituting the result of Derivation 1:** $$\dfrac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T^*}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$ **Step 2 — Invert to obtain the standard textbook form (static-to-stagnation):** $$\boxed{\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical value for air (γ = 1.4):** $$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.4}\right)^{\frac{1.4}{0.4}} = (0.8333)^{3.5}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate step-by-step:** $$ \begin{aligned} (0.8333)^{3.5} &= (0.8333)^3 \times (0.8333)^{0.5} \\ (0.8333)^3 &= 0.5787 \\ (0.8333)^{0.5} &= \sqrt{0.8333} = 0.9129 \\ (0.8333)^{3.5} &= 0.5787 \times 0.9129 = 0.5283 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** $$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} \approx 0.5283 \quad \text{(i.e., 52.83\%)}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** A flow can never have a static pressure lower than **52.83%** of its stagnation pressure (for air), regardless of how low the downstream/back pressure drops. This is the **choking limit**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Density Ratio (ρ\*/ρ₀) **Starting Point:** The ideal gas law combined with Poisson's relation. $$\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$$ **Step 1 — Apply M = 1, substituting Derivation 1:** $$\dfrac{\rho_0}{\rho^*} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T^*}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}$$ **Step 2 — Invert:** $$\boxed{\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical value for air (γ = 1.4):** $$\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = (0.8333)^{2.5}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate step-by-step:** $$ \begin{aligned} (0.8333)^{2.5} &= (0.8333)^2 \times (0.8333)^{0.5} \\ (0.8333)^2 &= 0.6944 \\ (0.8333)^{0.5} &= 0.9129 \\ (0.8333)^{2.5} &= 0.6944 \times 0.9129 = 0.6339 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** $$\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} \approx 0.6339 \quad \text{(i.e., 63.39\%)}$$ **Verification Check:** $P^*/P_0 = (\rho^*/\rho_0) \times (T^*/T_0) \Rightarrow 0.5283 \approx 0.6339 \times 0.8333$ ✓ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Velocity (a\*) **Starting Point:** The definition of speed of sound in a perfect gas evaluated at the critical state. $$a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute T\* from Derivation 1:** $$a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R \left(\dfrac{2 T_0}{\gamma + 1}\right)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma R T_0}{\gamma + 1}}$$ **Step 2 — Express in terms of the stagnation speed of sound, $a_0 = \sqrt{\gamma R T_0}$:** $$\boxed{a^* = a_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}}}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical value for air (γ = 1.4):** $$a^* = a_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2.4}} = a_0 \sqrt{0.8333} = a_0 \times 0.9129$$ **Final Result:** $$a^* \approx 0.913 \, a_0 \quad \text{(i.e., 91.29\% of stagnation sonic velocity)}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The sonic velocity at the critical state is *less* than the stagnation speed of sound because the static temperature is lower (T\* < T₀). This is a common point of confusion in KTU exams. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate **Starting Point:** Continuity equation at the throat where M = 1. $$\dot{m} = \rho^* A^* V^* = \rho^* A^* a^*$$ **Step 1 — Substitute ideal gas law $\rho^* = P^*/(RT^*)$:** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P^*}{R T^*} \cdot A^* \cdot a^*$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$:** $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P^*}{R T^*} \cdot A^* \cdot \sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \dfrac{P^* A^*}{\sqrt{R T^*}} \cdot \sqrt{\gamma}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $P^* = P_0 \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ and $T^* = T_0 \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{max} &= \dfrac{P_0 \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} \cdot A^*}{\sqrt{R \cdot T_0 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}}} \cdot \sqrt{\gamma} \\ &= \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{R T_0}} \cdot \sqrt{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Simplify the exponent of the bracketed term:** $$\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{2\gamma - (\gamma - 1)}{2(\gamma - 1)} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}$$ **Step 5 — Final Master Equation (a KTU favourite):** $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}}$$ **For air (γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/kg·K):** $$\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \times 0.0404 \times (0.8333)^{3.0} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \times 0.0404 \times 0.5787$$ $$\dot{m}_{max} \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-2} \, \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \quad \text{(SI units: kg/s)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Take-Away:** Once the flow chokes at the throat (M = 1), the mass flow rate is **independent of the downstream (back) pressure**. It depends *only* on the upstream stagnation conditions (P₀, T₀) and the throat area A\*. This is the foundation of choked-orifice and convergent-nozzle flow meters. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Critical Ratio | Master Formula | Air (γ=1.4) | Helium (γ=1.67) | CO₂ (γ=1.3) | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | T\*/T₀ | $2/(\gamma+1)$ | **0.8333** | 0.7491 | 0.8696 | | P\*/P₀ | $(2/(\gamma+1))^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | **0.5283** | 0.4867 | 0.5457 | | ρ\*/ρ₀ | $(2/(\gamma+1))^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | **0.6339** | 0.6499 | 0.6274 | | a\*/a₀ | $\sqrt{2/(\gamma+1)}$ | **0.9129** | 0.8655 | 0.9324 | ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Critical Property Relationships The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical dependency chain** between stagnation, critical, and static states in isentropic compressible flow. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "critical properties" as applied to compressible fluid flow. List the four critical properties of a perfect gas.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Critical properties refer to the thermodynamic and kinematic state of a fluid at the point where the local flow velocity becomes equal to the local speed of sound, i.e., when the local **Mach number M = 1**. This is also called the **sonic** or **throat** condition. > > **Four Critical Properties (2 marks):** > 1. **Critical Pressure (P\*)** — Static pressure at M = 1. > 2. **Critical Temperature (T\*)** — Static temperature at M = 1. > 3. **Critical Density (ρ\*)** — Static density at M = 1. > 4. **Critical Velocity (a\*)** — Local speed of sound (V = a) at M = 1. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the relations for the critical temperature ratio (T\*/T₀) and critical pressure ratio (P\*/P₀) for a perfect gas undergoing isentropic flow. Compute these ratios for air (γ = 1.4).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The two relations are: $$\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1} \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$ $$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **For air (γ = 1.4) (1 mark):** $$\dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{2.4} = 0.8333$$ $$\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = (0.8333)^{3.5} = 0.5283$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-1 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** (full internal choice). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Starting from the steady flow energy equation for a perfect gas, derive the expressions for the **critical temperature ratio (T\*/T₀)** and the **critical pressure ratio (P\*/P₀)** in terms of the specific heat ratio γ. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Air (γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/kg·K) enters the throat of a converging nozzle at stagnation conditions **P₀ = 800 kPa** and **T₀ = 500 K**. If the throat area is **A\* = 0.005 m²** and the flow is choked, calculate: (i) The critical pressure and temperature. **(2 Marks)** (ii) The critical velocity. **(2 Marks)** (iii) The maximum (choked) mass flow rate. **(3 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for a perfect gas (1 mark):** $$h_0 = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_p T_0 = C_p T + \dfrac{V^2}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange and introduce Mach number M = V/a (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{V^2}{2} &= C_p (T_0 - T) \\ V^2 &= 2 C_p (T_0 - T) = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} (T_0 - T) \\ M^2 = \dfrac{V^2}{a^2} &= \dfrac{V^2}{\gamma R T} = \dfrac{2 (T_0 - T)}{(\gamma - 1) T} \\ M^2 (\gamma - 1) &= 2 \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T} - 1\right) \\ \dfrac{T_0}{T} &= 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Critical condition M = 1, derive T\*/T₀ (1.5 marks):** $$\dfrac{T_0}{T^*} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} (1)^2 = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{T^*}{T_0} = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}$$ **Step 4 — Use Poisson's relation, derive P\*/P₀ (2 marks):** $$ \dfrac{P_0}{P} = \left(\dfrac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} $$ Applying M = 1: $$\dfrac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}$$ **Step 5 — Statement of result (1 mark):** The critical ratios are constants for a given gas, independent of the upstream pressure or temperature. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/kg·K, P₀ = 800 kPa = 800,000 Pa, T₀ = 500 K, A\* = 0.005 m². **(i) Critical Pressure and Temperature (2 Marks):** $$T^* = T_0 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1} = 500 \times 0.8333 = 416.67 \text{ K} \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$ $$P^* = P_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} = 800 \times 0.5283 = 422.6 \text{ kPa} \qquad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **(ii) Critical Velocity (2 Marks):** $$a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 416.67}$$ $$a^* = \sqrt{167{,}419.5} = 409.17 \text{ m/s} \qquad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Maximum (Choked) Mass Flow Rate (3 Marks):** Using the master equation: $$\dot{m}_{max} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}}$$ **Computing each term:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4}{287}} = \sqrt{0.004878} = 0.06985 \\ \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} &= \dfrac{0.005 \times 800{,}000}{\sqrt{500}} = \dfrac{4000}{22.361} = 178.885 \\ \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2(\gamma - 1)}} &= (0.8333)^{3.0} = 0.5787 \end{aligned} $$ **Final Calculation (1 mark):** $$\dot{m}_{max} = 178.885 \times 0.06985 \times 0.5787 = 7.23 \text{ kg/s}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **variation of mass flow rate with the ratio of back pressure to stagnation pressure (P_b/P₀)** in a **converging–diverging nozzle**. Clearly identify the **choked flow condition** and the role of **critical pressure ratio**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Helium (γ = 1.67, R = 2077 J/kg·K) flows through a convergent nozzle from a reservoir at **P₀ = 600 kPa** and **T₀ = 400 K**. The throat diameter is **40 mm**. Assuming isentropic flow: (i) Calculate the critical temperature and pressure for helium. **(3 Marks)** (ii) Determine whether the flow is choked if the back pressure **P_b = 200 kPa**. **(2 Marks)** (iii) Calculate the actual mass flow rate. **(2 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — Mass Flow vs Back Pressure (7 Marks) **Sketch (3 marks):** ``` | P0 |----------------- (Stagnation line, P/P0 = 1) | \ | \ (Subsonic: P_b/P0 > P*/P0) | \ | ● (M=1, Choked condition at P_b/P0 = P*/P0 = 0.528 for air) | |\________ | | \_____ (Supersonic possible in C-D nozzle, M > 1) | | 0 |________|________________ 0 P*/P0 (0.528) 1 Back Pressure Ratio P_b / P_0 ``` **Explanation (4 marks):** - **Region 1 (P_b/P₀ > P\*/P₀):** Flow is **subsonic** throughout the nozzle. Mass flow rate increases as P_b decreases. **(1 mark)** - **Point A (P_b/P₀ = P\*/P₀):** The **critical pressure ratio** is reached. The throat achieves **M = 1** and the flow **chokes**. **(1 mark)** - **Region 2 (P_b/P₀ < P\*/P₀, converging nozzle only):** For a purely converging nozzle, mass flow rate **cannot increase further** — it remains constant at $\dot{m}_{max}$. Further reduction in P_b is "wasted." **(1 mark)** - **Region 2' (P_b/P₀ < P\*/P₀, C-D nozzle):** For a **converging–diverging nozzle**, the diverging section can now accelerate the flow to **supersonic** (M > 1), with normal shock or isentropic expansion. **(1 mark)** **Critical pressure ratio (P\*/P₀) = (2/(γ+1))^(γ/(γ-1)) is the threshold above which flow remains subsonic, and below which choking occurs.** --- ### Part (b) — Helium Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** γ = 1.67, R = 2077 J/kg·K, P₀ = 600 kPa, T₀ = 400 K, throat diameter D\* = 40 mm = 0.04 m. **Throat Area:** $$A^* = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^*)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.04)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \qquad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$ **(i) Critical Temperature and Pressure (3 Marks):** $$T^* = T_0 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1} = 400 \times \dfrac{2}{2.67} = 400 \times 0.7491 = 299.6 \text{ K} \qquad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$ $$P^* = P_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} = 600 \times (0.7491)^{4.892} \qquad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$ Computing: $$(0.7491)^{4.892} \approx 0.2435$$ $$P^* = 600 \times 0.2435 = 146.1 \text{ kPa}$$ **(ii) Check for Choking (2 Marks):** Back pressure P_b = 200 kPa. Critical pressure P\* = 146.1 kPa. Since **P_b (200 kPa) > P\* (146.1 kPa)**, the flow is **NOT choked** (subsonic throughout). **(2 marks)** **(iii) Actual Mass Flow Rate (2 Marks):** For unchoked flow, we use the isentropic relation: $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{A^* P_0}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{1/\gamma} \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}$$ The pressure at the throat equals back pressure in an unchoked flow: P = P_b = 200 kPa. So **P/P₀ = 200/600 = 0.3333**. **Computing each term:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.67}{2077}} = \sqrt{0.000804} = 0.02836 \\ \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{1/\gamma} &= (0.3333)^{1/1.67} = (0.3333)^{0.5988} \approx 0.5017 \\ \left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} &= (0.3333)^{0.4012} \approx 0.6633 \\ 1 - 0.6633 &= 0.3367 \\ \sqrt{0.3367} &= 0.5803 \end{aligned} $$ $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 600{,}000}{\sqrt{400}} \times 0.02836 \times 0.5017 \times 0.5803$$ $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{753.98}{20} \times 0.02836 \times 0.5017 \times 0.5803$$ $$\dot{m} = 37.699 \times 0.02836 \times 0.5017 \times 0.5803 = 0.311 \text{ kg/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Module 1):** > 1. **Confusing P\* (critical pressure) with thermodynamic P_c** (substance critical point). These are *different* quantities — read the question carefully. > 2. **Forgetting to use stagnation properties in the energy equation.** The SFEE relates stagnation to static, not two static states. > 3. **Using 1.4 for gases other than air.** Helium has γ = 1.67, CO₂ has γ = 1.3, superheated steam has γ ≈ 1.3. Always check the gas. > 4. **Mixing up static-to-stagnation vs stagnation-to-static ratios.** Standard textbook convention is stagnation-over-static (e.g., T₀/T\*), but KTU sometimes uses T\*/T₀. Read the question. > 5. **Assuming choked flow without checking P_b < P\*.** Always verify the choking condition before applying the maximum mass flow equation. > 6. **Calculation error in exponent (γ/(γ-1)).** For air this is 3.5; for helium it is 4.892. A common slip-up is to use 3.5 for every gas. > 7. **Forgetting that a\* = 0.913 a₀ (not a₀ itself).** Examiners specifically test whether students know the difference between stagnation sound speed and critical sound speed. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.** - **Critical state is defined by M = 1** (flow velocity = local speed of sound). - **Four critical properties:** P\*, T\*, ρ\*, a\* — all evaluated at the throat of a choked nozzle. - **Master energy equation:** $\dfrac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$ — derive all ratios from this. - **Critical temperature ratio:** $T^*/T_0 = \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}$ — equals **0.8333** for air. - **Critical pressure ratio:** $P^*/P_0 = \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ — equals **0.5283** for air. -
