For $\gamma = 1.4$ and $A_e/A^* = 1.6$: $$\frac{A_e}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M_e}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_e^2\right)\right]^{(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)]}$$ Trial-and-error or table lookup for $A/A^* = 1.6$ on the **supersonic branch** gives: $$M_e \approx 1.74 \quad \text{(to 2 decimal places — 1 mark)}$$ **Step 2:** Compute exit static pressure: $$\frac{p_e}{p_0} = \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(1.74)^2\right)^{-3.5} = (1 + 0.605)^{-3.5} = (1.605)^{-3.5} \approx 0.186$$ $$p_e = 0.186 \times 800 = 148.8 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{— (1 mark)}$$ **Step 3:** Compute exit temperature: $$T_e = T_0 \left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(1.74)^2\right)^{-1} = \frac{600}{1.605} = 373.8 \text{ K} \quad \text{— (1 mark)}$$ **Step 4:** Compute exit velocity: $$V_e = M_e \sqrt{\gamma R T_e} = 1.74 \times \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 373.8}$$ $$V_e = 1.74 \times \sqrt{150{,}196} = 1.74 \times 387.6 = 674.4 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{— (2 marks)}$$ **Step 5:** Note that at design condition, $p_e = p_b$, so back pressure is **148.8 kPa** — **(1 mark)**. **Part (b) — Back Pressure Changed to 200 kPa [7 marks]** **Step 1:** Compare $p_b = 200$ kPa with the design exit pressure $p_e = 148.8$ kPa. Since $p_b > p_e$ (i.e., 200 > 148.8), the nozzle is operating in the **over-expanded regime** — **(2 marks)**. **Step 2:** Critical pressure for choking: $p^* = 0.5283 \times 800 = 422.6$ kPa. Since $p_b = 200$ kPa $< p^*$, the throat is **choked** with M = 1, and mass flow is at maximum — **(2 marks)**. **Step 3:** Compute $\dot{m}_{max}$ (assuming $A^* = 1$ m² for the rate-per-unit-area calculation): $$\dot{m}_{max} = 0.0404 \times \frac{800{,}000 \times 1}{\sqrt{600}} = 0.0404 \times \frac{800{,}000}{24.49} = 0.0404 \times 32{,}666 = 1319.7 \text{ kg/s·m}^2 \quad \text{— (2 marks)}$$ The mass flow is **identical** in both design and over-expanded cases (because the throat is choked in both), since $p_0$, $T_0$, and $A^*$ are unchanged. **Step 4 — Thrust Implication:** In the over-expanded case, the nozzle exit pressure $p_e = 148.8$ kPa exceeds the back pressure $p_b = 200$ kPa. The flow adjusts via a **normal shock** in the divergent section, which causes a **loss in total pressure** and therefore a **reduction in thrust efficiency** compared to the design condition. The rocket/jet engine designer typically prefers operation at or near the design condition — **(1 mark)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Losers:** > 1. **Sign error in area-velocity relation:** Writing $\frac{dA}{A} = (1 - M^2)\frac{dV}{V}$ instead of the correct $(M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$ — costs **2 marks** on derivation questions. > 2. **Forgetting to take the supersonic branch:** When $A/A^* > 1$, two M values satisfy the equation. Always pick the **supersonic branch** for the divergent section of a C–D nozzle — wrong branch selection can cost **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Confusing $p^*$ with $p_b$:** $p^*$ is the throat critical pressure (≈ 0.528 $p_0$), whereas $p_b$ is the **ambient back pressure**, which can be anything the problem specifies — mixing these up costs **1 mark**. > 4. **Not stating isentropic assumption explicitly:** Most KTU 2024 valuation keys expect the words "**assuming isentropic flow**" or "**for an ideal gas with constant specific heats**" to appear in the answer. Omitting this assumption can cost **0.5–1 mark**. > 5. **Mass flow rate formula units mismatch:** When using $\dot{m}_{max} = 0.0404 \cdot p_0 A^* / \sqrt{T_0}$, ensure $p_0$ is in **Pa (not kPa)** and $T_0$ is in **K (not °C)** — this single unit slip can cost **1 full mark**. > 6. **Confusing over-expanded with under-expanded:** Over-expansion ⇒ $p_e > p_b$; under-expansion ⇒ $p_e < p_b$. A quick mnemonic: **"over-expanded = the gas has expanded too much, so its pressure is high relative to ambient"**. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Revise these 14 points before entering the KTU exam hall:** 1. **Nozzle** accelerates flow; **diffuser** decelerates it — both convert between kinetic and pressure energy isentropically. 2. The **area-velocity relation** $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$ is the **master equation** governing all variable-area duct flow. 3. **Convergent duct:** accelerates subsonic flow OR decelerates supersonic flow. 4. **Divergent duct:** decelerates subsonic flow OR accelerates supersonic flow. 5. The **throat** is the only location where M = 1 is geometrically possible (since $dA = 0$). 6. The **critical pressure ratio** for air is **0.5283**, independent of inlet pressure and temperature. 7. The **critical temperature ratio** for air is **0.8333**; the **critical density ratio** is **0.6339**. 8. The **choked mass flow rate** $\dot{m}_{max} = 0.0404 \cdot p_0 A^* / \sqrt{T_0}$ is a constant once $p_0$, $T_0$, and $A^*$ are fixed — it **cannot be increased** by lowering $p_b$. 9. **Convergent nozzle:** behaves subsonically for $p_b/p_0 > 0.528$; chokes for $p_b/p_0 \le 0.528$; cannot produce supersonic flow internally. 10. **Convergent–divergent nozzle:** required to produce **supersonic exit flow**; the divergent section acts as a supersonic accelerator only when the throat is choked. 11. **Seven operating regimes** of a C–D nozzle span from no-flow (regime 1) to severely under-expanded (regime 7). 12. **Design (perfectly expanded) condition:** $p_e = p_b$ ⇒ maximum propulsive efficiency. 13. **Over-expansion:** $p_e > p_b$ ⇒ normal/oblique shocks form, causing total pressure loss. 14. **Under-expansion:** $p_e < p_b$ ⇒ Prandtl–Meyer expansion fans form outside the nozzle exit; flow has residual kinetic energy that was not converted. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on this topic almost always carry 14 marks split into two 7-mark parts. Expect either: (i) a derivation + analysis, or (ii) a numerical + regime identification. **Always draw a labeled sketch** of the nozzle and indicate $p_0$, $p^*$, $p_e$, and $p_b$ on the diagram — examiners award **partial marks** for clear diagrams even when the calculation has an error.

topics.title: Use of gas tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Use of gas tables ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Compressible Fluid Flow, the **fundamental governing equations** (continuity, momentum, and energy) for one-dimensional, steady, isentropic flow of a **calorically perfect gas** (constant specific heats, $c_p$, $c_v$, and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$) yield closed-form analytical relations between flow properties (pressure $p$, temperature $T$, density $\rho$, velocity $V$, Mach number $M$) and reference/stagnation conditions ($p_0$, $T_0$, $\rho_0$, $a_0$) or critical/sonic conditions ($p^*$, $T^*$, $\rho^*$, $A^*$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas Tables** are pre-computed, tabulated numerical values of these non-dimensional compressible flow parameters as a function of the **Mach number $M$** (and $\gamma$). They exist for four standard processes: > 1. **Isentropic Flow Tables** (also called "Reference State Tables") > 2. **Normal Shock Wave Tables** (Rankine-Hugoniot relations) > 3. **Fanno Flow Tables** (adiabatic flow with friction, $4fL/D$ as parameter) > 4. **Rayleigh Flow Tables** (frictionless flow with heat addition, $T_0/T_0^*$ as parameter) > > Their **purpose** is to bypass the tedious, iterative solution of implicit transcendental equations (like area-Mach relation) and provide direct, accurate property look-up for any Mach number in the range $0 \le M \le 10$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Phone Book Analogy:** Imagine you need to find the phone number of "Rahul" in a city of 10 million people. You *could* use a complicated search algorithm or alphabetical hashing function. But a **telephone directory (phone book)** solves it instantly. > > Similarly, the relation between Mach number $M$ and the area ratio $A/A^*$ is given by an **implicit transcendental equation**: > $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$ > You *could* solve this iteratively (Newton-Raphson) for $M$ given $A/A^*$. But the **Isentropic Flow Gas Table** gives you $M$ immediately for any $A/A^*$. This is the essence of "using gas tables" — leveraging **pre-tabulated numerical data** instead of wrestling with implicit equations in an exam or design calculation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Ratio of specific heats for air:** $\gamma = \mathbf{1.4}$ (universally used unless stated otherwise) - **Gas constant for air:** $R = \mathbf{287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}}$ - **Universal gas constant:** $\bar{R} = \mathbf{8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}}$ - **Speed of sound reference:** $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ - **Stagnation temperature reference:** $T_0 = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)$ - **Standard tables cover Mach number range:** $M = 0$ to $M = 10$ (in steps of $0.01$ for high accuracy, often $0.05$ or $0.1$ in compact tables) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three "States" in Compressible Flow Tables Every gas table is indexed by one of three physical states: 1. **Stagnation State (subscript 0):** The state the gas *would reach* if brought to rest **isentropically** (no shocks, no friction). It is conserved across any adiabatic process (shocks, friction, area change) — a **fundamental invariant** in 1-D steady flow without heat addition or work. 2. **Critical / Sonic State (superscript \*, e.g., $A^*, T^*, p^*$):** The state at $M = 1$ (choked condition). The geometric throat area $A^*$ is the reference for area ratios. 3. **Static State (no subscript):** The actual local flow property ($p, T, \rho, V, a$). ### 2.2 Why Are Gas Tables Needed? (The Operational Logic) - The isentropic relations are **explicit in property ratios** given $M$ (e.g., $T_0/T = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$). - However, the **area-Mach relation** $A/A^*(M)$ is **transcendental and implicit** — you cannot isolate $M$ in closed form. Numerical solution is required. - Similarly, **Normal Shock** relations give $M_2$ as an implicit function of $M_1$ (the Prandtl-Meyer function is also implicit). - **Fanno** and **Rayleigh** flows involve implicit relations where the friction parameter $4fL_{\max}/D$ or heat addition $q$ is the index. - Gas tables are the **engineer's practical tool** to convert these implicit relations into simple **linear interpolation** in a tabulated array. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet: Isentropic Flow Table (γ = 1.4) | # | Parameter | Symbol | Functional Form | Unit | |:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | Stagnation-to-static pressure ratio | $p_0/p$ | $\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Stagnation-to-static density ratio | $\rho_0/\rho$ | $\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Stagnation-to-static temperature ratio | $T_0/T$ | $1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Critical pressure ratio (at $M=1$) | $p_0/p^*$ | $\left(\frac{\gamma+1}{2}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Critical temperature ratio (at $M=1$) | $T_0/T^*$ | $\frac{\gamma+1}{2}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Area-Mach ratio (supersonic branch $M>1$, subsonic $M<1$) | $A/A^*$ | $\frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Velocity ratio | $V/a^*$ | $M\sqrt{\frac{\gamma+1}{2 + (\gamma-1)M^2}}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Mach number from area ratio (implicit) | $M$ | Solve $A/A^*(M) = $ given value | dimensionless | | 9 | Mass flow rate (choked reference) | $\dot{m}$ | $\frac{p_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | kg/s | | 10 | Critical speed of sound | $a^*$ | $\sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \sqrt{\frac{2\gamma R T_0}{\gamma+1}}$ | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **Note on $A/A^*$ uniqueness:** For a given $A/A^* > 1$, there are **two** valid Mach numbers — one subsonic ($M < 1$) and one supersonic ($M > 1$). The gas table provides both values, and the physical context (e.g., converging or diverging nozzle) selects the correct branch. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet: Normal Shock Table (γ = 1.4) | # | Parameter (function of upstream $M_1$) | Symbol | |:-:|:-:|:-:| | 1 | Downstream Mach number | $M_2$ | | 2 | Static pressure ratio | $p_2/p_1$ | | 3 | Static temperature ratio | $T_2/T_1$ | | 4 | Static density ratio | $\rho_2/\rho_1$ | | 5 | Stagnation pressure ratio (loss metric) | $p_{02}/p_{01}$ | | 6 | Stagnation temperature ratio (always 1) | $T_{02}/T_{01} = 1$ | **Key implicit relations used to build the table:** - $M_2^2 = \frac{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}$ - $\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1)$ - $\frac{p_{02}}{p_{01}} = \left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{(\gamma-1)M_1^2 + 2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \left[\frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma M_1^2 - (\gamma-1)}\right]^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ ### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Aerospace:** Design of supersonic inlets, nozzles (CD nozzles), and wind tunnel test sections. - **Gas Dynamics Labs:** Determination of Mach number from pitot-static pressure readings in a supersonic test facility. - **Rocket Propulsion:** Throat area sizing using choked mass flow and exit conditions. - **Steam & Gas Turbines:** Evaluation of compressibility effects in high-Mach blade passages. - **Process Industry:** Safety relief valve sizing and blowdown calculations (choked flow through orifice). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Isentropic Property Ratios (Building Block of Table 1) **Starting equations (perfect gas, isentropic, calorically perfect):** Step 1 — Energy equation (steady, adiabatic, no work) integrated from local state to stagnation: $$h_0 = h + \frac{V^2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_p T_0 = c_p T + \frac{V^2}{2}$$ Step 2 — Substitute $c_p = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ and $V = Ma = M\sqrt{\gamma R T}$: $$\frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}T_0 = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}T + \frac{\gamma R T M^2}{2}$$ Step 3 — Cancel $\gamma R$ and divide by $T$: $$\frac{T_0}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{1}{\gamma - 1} + \frac{M^2}{2}$$ Step 4 — Rearrange (the **fundamental temperature-Mach relation**): $$\boxed{\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2}$$ Step 5 — Apply isentropic process law $p/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ and ideal gas $p = \rho R T$ to get pressure and density ratios: $$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$ $$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Area-Mach Relation (Foundation of $A/A^*$) **Step 1** — Continuity equation for compressible flow: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$$ **Step 2** — Apply between an arbitrary section (Mach $M$) and the sonic throat ($M^* = 1$): $$\rho A V = \rho^* A^* V^*$$ **Step 3** — Divide both sides and substitute $V = Ma$ and $V^* = a^*$: $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho}\cdot \frac{V^*}{V} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho}\cdot \frac{a^*}{M a}$$ **Step 4** — Use $a/a^* = \sqrt{T/T^*}$ (from $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$), and $\rho/\rho^* = (T/T^*)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ (from isentropic + ideal gas): $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{1/2} = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$ **Step 5** — Apply $T_0/T^* = (\gamma+1)/2$ (from Step 4 of §3.1 with $M^* = 1$), giving $T^*/T = [(\gamma+1)/2] / T_0/T$: $$\frac{T^*}{T} = \frac{\gamma+1}{2 + (\gamma-1)M^2}$$ **Step 6** — Final closed-form expression (this is what the gas table tabulates as a function of $M$): $$\boxed{\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight for KTU exam:** This equation cannot be solved analytically for $M$ in terms of $A/A^*$. The gas table converts this implicit equation into a simple look-up plus linear interpolation. ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Linear Interpolation in Isentropic Table **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):** Given $A/A^* = 2.1970$, find the Mach number. Use $\gamma = 1.4$ and the table snippet below. | $M$ | $A/A^*$ (subsonic) | $A/A^*$ (supersonic) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0.30 | 2.0351 | — | | 0.32 | 2.0000 (approx) | — | | 0.40 | 1.5901 | — | | 2.20 | — | 2.0050 | | 2.40 | — | 2.4030 | **Solution (Step-by-Step, with full arithmetic):** **Case 1: Subsonic branch.** Between $M=0.30$ ($A/A^*=2.0351$) and $M=0.32$ (approx $A/A^*=2.0000$ approx). Since $2.1970 > 2.0351$, our value lies **above** the maximum $A/A^*$ on the subsonic side (which occurs at $M=1$, $A/A^*=1$ decreasing as $M$ rises). Wait — actually the subsonic $A/A^*$ is **maximum at $M=1$** (equals 1) and **decreases as $M \to 0$** (goes to $\infty$). So $2.1970$ is at a low subsonic Mach. Re-corrected: At $M=0.30$, $A/A^* \approx 2.0351$. As $M$ decreases toward 0, $A/A^* \to \infty$. So 2.1970 lies between $M=0$ and $M=0.30$. But our snippet doesn't have that exact range. Use the typical table values: Between $M_1 = 0.28$ ($A/A^* = 2.1693$) and $M_2 = 0.30$ ($A/A^* = 2.0351$): $$\text{Slope} = \frac{2.0351 - 2.1693}{0.30 - 0.28} = \frac{-0.1342}{0.02} = -6.71 \text{ per unit Mach}$$ **Linear interpolation formula:** $$M = M_1 + \frac{(A/A^*)_{\text{given}} - (A/A^*)_1}{(A/A^*)_2 - (A/A^*)_1}(M_2 - M_1)$$ $$M = 0.28 + \frac{2.1970 - 2.1693}{2.0351 - 2.1693}(0.30 - 0.28) = 0.28 + \frac{0.0277}{-0.1342}(0.02)$$ $$M = 0.28 + (-0.2064)(0.02) = 0.28 - 0.00413 = 0.2759 \approx 0.276$$ **Case 2: Supersonic branch.** Between $M=2.20$ ($A/A^*=2.0050$) and $M=2.40$ ($A/A^*=2.4030$): $$M = 2.20 + \frac{2.1970 - 2.0050}{2.4030 - 2.0050}(2.40 - 2.20) = 2.20 + \frac{0.1920}{0.3980}(0.20)$$ $$M = 2.20 + (0.4824)(0.20) = 2.20 + 0.0965 = 2.2965 \approx 2.297$$ > [!TIP] > **The two valid answers are $M \approx 0.276$ (subsonic) and $M \approx 2.297$ (supersonic).** The physical context (e.g., a converging nozzle has only subsonic flow; a CD nozzle exit has supersonic flow) selects the correct one. This is the most common type of KTU exam question. ### 3.4 Analytical Strategy Matrix for Using Gas Tables | **Given** | **Required** | **Table to Use** | **Procedure** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | $M$ (known) | $p/p_0, T/T_0, A/A^*$ etc. | Isentropic | Direct look-up at row $M$ | | $A/A^*$ | $M$ (with $M<1$ or $M>1$ branch) | Isentropic | Search column $A/A^*$, find $M$, possibly interpolate | | $p_0, p$ (in supersonic flow) | $M$ | Isentropic | Compute $p_0/p$, find row in isentropic table, read $M$ | | $M_1$ (upstream of shock) | $M_2, p_2/p_1, p_{02}/p_{01}$ | Normal Shock | Direct look-up at row $M_1$ | | $M$ + duct geometry (Fanno) | Max length $L_{\max}$ or entropy change | Fanno | Look up $4fL^*/D$ at given $M$ | | $T_0/T_0^*$ (Rayleigh heat addition) | $M$ | Rayleigh | Look up temperature ratio column, find $M$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Gas-Table Lookup Workflow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **decision-flow** an engineer follows when using gas tables in a KTU exam or design problem. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Identify Given Quantities]) --> qA{What is the
known parameter?} qA -- Mach Number M --> dirA[Direct Read:
Look up row M in
Isentropic Table] qA -- Area Ratio A/A* --> interpA[Two-Branch Search:
Find nearest M<1 and M>1
Linear Interpolation] qA -- Pressure Ratio p0/p --> isoA[Isentropic Table:
Locate p0/p column
Read corresponding M] qA -- Upstream M1 across Shock --> nsA[Normal Shock Table:
Locate row M1
Read M2, p2/p1, p02/p01] qA -- Friction Parameter 4fL/D --> fanA[Fanno Table:
Locate friction column
Read M, T/T*, p/p*] qA -- Heat Parameter T0/T0* --> rayA[Rayleigh Table:
Locate T0/T0* column
Read M, T/T*, p/p*] dirA --> outA[Output: All isentropic
property ratios] interpA --> branchA{Physical Context?} branchA -- Converging Nozzle --> subA[Select Subsonic M<1] branchA -- CD Nozzle Exit --> supA[Select Supersonic M>1] subA --> outA supA --> outA isoA --> outA nsA --> outNS[Output: Downstream
shock state] fanA --> outFan[Output: Fanno
flow properties] rayA --> outRay[Output: Rayleigh
flow properties] outA --> endA([End: Tabulated Values Retrieved]) outNS --> endA outFan --> endA outRay --> endA ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: Reference States Used Across All Four Tables ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph refStates[Reference State Reference Card] sA[Stagnation State:
p0, T0, rho0
Conservation Law:
Adiabatic + No Work] sB[Critical State:
p*, T*, rho*, A*
Reference at M=1
Choked Condition] sC[Static State:
p, T, rho, V, a
Local Flow Property] end sA -->|Ratio p0/p, T0/T| sC sB -->|Ratio A/A*, p/p*, T/T*| sC sA -->|Constant across
adiabatic processes| sB ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading Tip:** The **stagnation state** is the universal "anchor" for isentropic and normal-shock processes. The **critical state** is the universal "anchor" for Fanno, Rayleigh, and choked-area problems. The **static state** is what you measure (with a pitot-static probe in a wind tunnel). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper Style]** Define the terms: (i) **stagnation state** and (ii) **critical (sonic) state** as used in isentropic compressible flow tables. Why are these states used as reference states? **Model Answer (3 marks allocation):** - **(i) Stagnation State (1 mark):** It is the state the gas would reach if it were brought to rest **isentropically** from its current state (no entropy change). Denoted by subscript "0" (e.g., $p_0, T_0, \rho_0$). It represents the total energy content of the flow. - **(ii) Critical (Sonic) State (1 mark):** It is the state of the gas when the local Mach number equals unity ($M = 1$). Denoted by superscript "*" (e.g., $p^*, T^*, A^*$). It represents the choked/saturated condition. - **(iii) Why used as reference (1 mark):** Stagnation state is **conserved** across any adiabatic, no-work process (area change, friction, normal shock), making it an ideal invariant to non-dimensionalize properties. The critical state provides a natural reference for mass-flow (choked) and area ratio $A/A^*$ relations. **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper Style]** What is the physical significance of the area ratio $A/A^*$ in compressible flow? Why does the gas table give **two** Mach numbers for a single value of $A/A^* > 1$? **Model Answer (3 marks allocation):** - **Physical significance (1 mark):** $A/A^*$ represents the ratio of the local cross-sectional area to the throat area (choked area). It is the parameter that controls whether flow accelerates, decelerates, or chokes in a variable-area duct. For $A/A^* = 1$, the flow is sonic (choked); for $A/A^* \neq 1$, the flow is either subsonic or supersonic. - **Mathematical reason for two values (1 mark):** The function $A/A^*(M)$ decreases from $\infty$ at $M \to 0$ to 1 at $M = 1$, then increases again to $\infty$ as $M \to \infty$. Therefore, for any $A/A^* > 1$, there are exactly two Mach numbers that satisfy the equation — one subsonic and one supersonic. - **Physical selection criterion (1 mark):** Selection between the two is based on duct geometry and process type: a **converging nozzle** has subsonic flow; a **converging-diverging (CD) nozzle** exit has supersonic flow; **friction in a constant-area duct** with subsonic inlet accelerates flow toward $M=1$, etc. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (Module Internal Choice). --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified] **(a)** Starting from the steady-flow energy equation for a calorically perfect gas, derive the isentropic flow relations: $$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2, \quad \frac{p_0}{p} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$ Show clearly every algebraic step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$), a converging-diverging nozzle has a throat area $A^* = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ and exit area $A_e = 0.10 \text{ m}^2$. The reservoir (stagnation) conditions are $p_0 = 600 \text{ kPa}$ and $T_0 = 500 \text{ K}$. Using the isentropic gas table for $\gamma = 1.4$: - (i) Determine the exit Mach number $M_e$. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Find the static pressure $p_e$, static temperature $T_e$, and exit velocity $V_e$. **(3 marks)** - (iii) Compute the mass flow rate through the nozzle. **(2 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Step 1** — Steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) for adiabatic, no-work, no-elevation-change flow: $$h_0 = h + \frac{V^2}{2}$$ **Step 2** — For a calorically perfect gas, $h_0 = c_p T_0$ and $h = c_p T$, so: $$c_p T_0 = c_p T + \frac{V^2}{2}$$ **Step 3** — Substitute $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$: $$\frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T_0 = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T + \frac{V^2}{2}$$ **Step 4** — Velocity of sound definition: $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$, and Mach number $M = V/a$, so $V = M\sqrt{\gamma R T}$, hence $V^2 = M^2 \gamma R T$: $$\frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T_0 = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} T + \frac{M^2 \gamma R T}{2}$$ **Step 5** — Divide throughout by $\gamma R T$: $$\frac{T_0}{T(\gamma-1)} = \frac{1}{\gamma-1} + \frac{M^2}{2}$$ **Step 6** — Multiply by $(\gamma-1)$ and add the temperature ratio: $$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2 \quad \blacksquare \quad (3 \text{ marks for derivation up to this point})$$ **Step 7** — For pressure: use isentropic relation $p/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ combined with ideal gas $p = \rho R T$: $$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right)^\gamma \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \frac{T_0}{T} \quad (\text{since } p/\rho = RT \Rightarrow p_0/\rho_0 = p/\rho = RT \text{ only if } T_0 = T, \text{ which is NOT the case})$$ **Correction:** For isentropic, $T/\rho^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$ (combined gas + isentropic law), so: $$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$$ **Step 8** — Substitute into $p_0/p = (\rho_0/\rho)^\gamma$: $$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(\frac{T_0}{T}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \quad \blacksquare \quad (4 \text{ marks for pressure derivation})$$ **Model Solution (b):** **Part (i) — Find $M_e$ from $A/A^* = 0.10 / 0.05 = 2.0$:** Since the nozzle is a CD nozzle with exit area larger than throat, exit flow is **supersonic** ($M_e > 1$). From the isentropic table ($\gamma = 1.4$), for $A/A^* = 2.0$: - Subsonic branch: $M \approx 0.30$ (rejected — would be in converging section, not exit of CD) - **Supersonic branch: $M_e \approx 2.197$** (direct read from standard tables) **$(2 \text{ marks})$** **Part (ii) — Static properties at $M_e = 2.197$:** From the isentropic table (linear interpolation if required): - $p_0/p_e = 9.765$ (approx, at $M = 2.197$) $$p_e = \frac{p_0}{9.765} = \frac{600 \text{ kPa}}{9.765} = 61.44 \text{ kPa} \approx 61.4 \text{ kPa} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ - $T_0/T_e = 1 + \frac{0.4}{2}(2.197)^2 = 1 + 0.2 \times 4.827 = 1 + 0.9654 = 1.9654$ $$T_e = \frac{500 \text{ K}}{1.9654} = 254.4 \text{ K} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ - Speed of sound at exit: $a_e = \sqrt{\gamma R T_e} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 254.4} = \sqrt{102{,}233} = 319.7 \text{ m/s}$ - Exit velocity: $V_e = M_e \cdot a_e = 2.197 \times 319.7 = 702.4 \text{ m/s} \approx 702 \text{ m/s} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$ **Part (iii) — Mass flow rate (use choked mass flow equation):** At the throat ($A^* = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$), $M = 1$, $T^* = T_0 / [(\gamma+1)/2] = 500 / 1.2 = 416.67 \text{ K}$: $$\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$ Compute the constant: $\sqrt{\frac{1.4}{287}\left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.4}{287} \times (0.8333)^6}$ $(0.8333)^6 = 0.3349$ $\frac{1.4}{287} \times 0.3349 = 0.001634$ $\sqrt{0.001634} = 0.04042$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 \times 0.05}{\sqrt{500}} \times 0.04042 = \frac{30{,}000}{22.36} \times 0.04042 = 1341.7 \times 0.04042 = 54.23 \text{ kg/s} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$ --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified] **(a)** Derive the area-Mach relation: $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$ State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$) flows isentropically through a duct. The static pressure and static temperature at a section are measured as $p = 150 \text{ kPa}$ and $T = 350 \text{ K}$, while the stagnation values are $p_0 = 400 \text{ kPa}$ and $T_0 = 450 \text{ K}$. - (i) Determine the local Mach number $M$. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Using the isentropic gas table, find the area ratio $A/A^*$ at this section. **(2 marks)** - (iii) If the local duct area is $A = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$, find the mass flow rate. **(3 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Steady, 1-D flow 2. Calorically perfect gas ($c_p, c_v, \gamma$ = const) 3. Isentropic process (no shocks, friction, or heat transfer) 4. Negligible body forces 5. No shaft work **Derivation Steps (6 marks):** **Step 1** — Continuity equation (mass conservation): $$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho A V = \rho^* A^* V^*$$ **Step 2** — Divide both sides and rearrange: $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho} \cdot \frac{V^*}{V}$$ **Step 3** — Use $V = Ma = M\sqrt{\gamma R T}$ and $V^* = a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$: $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\gamma R T^*}}{M\sqrt{\gamma R T}} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho} \cdot \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{T^*}{T}}$$ **Step 4** — Isentropic density-temperature relation: $\rho^*/\rho = (T^*/T)^{1/(\gamma-1)}$ $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{1/2} = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1} + \frac{1}{2}}$$ **Step 5** — Combine the exponents: $\frac{1}{\gamma-1} + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2 + \gamma - 1}{2(\gamma-1)} = \frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}$ $$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left(\frac{T^*}{T}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$ **Step 6** — Critical temperature from $T_0/T = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$ evaluated at $M=1$ gives $T_0/T^* = (\gamma+1)/2$. So $T^*/T_0 = 2/(\gamma+1)$. Also $T_0/T = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$, hence: $$\frac{T^*}{T} = \frac{T^*}{T_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{T} = \frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)$$ **Step 7** — Substitute back: $$\boxed{\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Model Solution (b):** **Part (i) — Mach number from pressure ratio:** $$\frac{p_0}{p} = \frac{400}{150} = 2.667$$ Cross-reference with the isentropic table ($\gamma = 1.4$): | $M$ | $p_0/p$ | |:---:|:---:| | 0.80 | 1.7600 (approx) | | 1.00 | 1.8929 (approx) | | 1.50 | 3.6710 (approx) | Since $2.667$ lies between $M=1.0$ and $M=1.5$, interpolate: $$M = 1.0 + \frac{2.667 - 1.8929}{3.6710 - 1.8929}(1.5 - 1.0) = 1.0 + \frac{0.7741}{1.7781}(0.5) = 1.0 + (0.4354)(0.5) = 1.0 + 0.218 = 1.218$$ **$M \approx 1.22$ (supersonic)** $\quad (2 \text{ marks})$ **Cross-check with temperature ratio:** $$T_0/T = 450/350 = 1.2857$$ $$M = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\gamma-1}\left(\frac{T_0}{T} - 1\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{0.4}(0.2857)} = \sqrt{1.4285} = 1.195$$ (Slight discrepancy due to measurement rounding; accept $M \approx 1.2$) **Part (ii) — Area ratio at $M = 1.22$:** From isentropic table (interpolation between $M=1.20$, $A/A^* = 1.0304$ and $M=1.22$ approx $1.0160$): **$A/A^* \approx 1.02$** $\quad (2 \text{ marks})$ **Part (iii) — Mass flow rate:** $$A^* = \frac{A}{A/A^*} = \frac{0.08}{1.02} = 0.0784 \text{ m}^2$$ Use $T_0 = 450$ K, $p_0 = 400$ kPa: $$\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$ The constant $\sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}} = 0.04042$ (from §5.2 Q-A above) $$\dot{m} = \frac{400 \times 10^3 \times 0.0784}{\sqrt{450}} \times 0.04042 = \frac{31{,}360}{21.21} \times 0.04042$$ $$= 1478.5 \times 0.04042 = 59.76 \text{ kg/s} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$ **$\dot{m} \approx 59.8$ kg/s** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Branch selection error ($-2$ to $-3$ marks):** When $A/A^* > 1$ is given, students often pick only the subsonic Mach without justification. The examiner expects: (a) computation of **both** Mach numbers via interpolation, and (b) a **physical justification** for selecting one (e.g., "since this is the exit of a CD nozzle, the flow is supersonic"). > > 2. **Stagnation vs. static confusion ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Confusing $p_0$ with $p$, or $T_0$ with $T$. Pitot tube measures stagnation; static tube measures static. A 1.5 m/s error in $a$ propagates to 50+ m/s error in $V$. > > 3. **Forgetting to use $T^*$ in choked-flow formula ($-1$ mark):** Mass flow rate uses $A^*$ and $T_0$, **not** $A$ and $T_0$. Many students plug in the wrong area. > > 4. **Unit mismatch in $\gamma$ and $R$ ($-1$ mark):** $R = 287$ J/(kg·K) requires temperatures in **Kelvin**, pressures in **Pascals** (or careful use of kPa with matching factors). > > 5. **Skipping interpolation steps ($-0.5$ to $-1$ mark):** If $A/A^* = 2.20$ is between table rows $M=2.19$ and $M=2.20$, you must show the linear interpolation formula explicitly, not just write the final $M$ value. > > 6. **Not stating assumptions ($-1$ mark in derivations):** For "derive" type questions, the assumptions list is mandatory. Typical ones: 1-D, steady, perfect gas, isentropic, calorically perfect, negligible body forces, no shaft work. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — KTU Module 1: Use of Gas Tables** > > - **Two reference states:** **Stagnation** (subscript 0, conserved across adiabatic processes) and **Critical/Sonic** (superscript \*, reference at $M=1$). > - **Isentropic Table indexed by $M$:** gives $p/p_0, T/T_0, \rho/\rho_0, A/A^*, p/p^*, T/T^*, V/a^*$. > - **Area-Mach relation is implicit** in $M$ — that is the *raison d'être* of the gas table. > - **For $A/A^* > 1$, two Mach numbers exist** (subsonic and supersonic) — pick by physical context. > - **At $M=1$:** $T_0/T^* = (\gamma+1)/2 = 1.2$ (for air), $p_0/p^* = [(\gamma+1)/2]^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \approx 1.893$ (for air), $A/A^* = 1$ (by definition). > - **Normal shock table** is indexed by **upstream $M_1$**, gives $M_2, p_2/p_1, T_2/T_1, \rho_2/\rho_1, p_{02}/p_{01}$. Key fact: $T_{02}/T_{01} = 1$ (energy is conserved) but $p_{02}/p_{01} < 1$ (entropy increases). > - **Fanno flow** (friction in constant area duct): indexed by $M$ on the Fanno table; gives $4fL^*/D, p/p^*, T/T^*, \rho/\rho^*, \dot{m}/\dot{m}^*$. Maximum entropy at $M=1$. > - **Rayleigh flow** (heat addition in frictionless constant area duct): indexed by $M$ on the Rayleigh table; gives $T_0/T_0^*, T/T^*, p/p^*, \rho/\rho^*, V/V^*$. Maximum entropy at a subsonic $M = 1/\sqrt{\gamma}$. > - **Linear interpolation formula (commit to memory):** > $$X = X_1 + \frac{Y - Y_1}{Y_2 - Y_1}(X_2 - X_1)$$ > - **Standard values to remember (air, $\gamma = 1.4$):** $(\gamma-1)/2 = 0.2$, $\gamma/(\gamma-1) = 3.5$, $(\gamma+1)/2 = 1.2$, $(\gamma+1)/[2(\gamma-1)] = 3$, $1/(\gamma-1) = 2.5$. > - **Choked mass flow (key formula):** > $$\dot{m} = \frac{p_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$ > - **Exam strategy:** Always (a) state assumptions, (b) show interpolation explicitly, (c) justify branch choice, (d) maintain unit consistency, (e) carry $\gamma = 1.4$ to final numerical step.

topics.title: Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Compressible Fluid Flow** under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PEMET632), the term **"Assumptions"** refers to the standardized set of idealized physical and thermodynamic conditions imposed on the working fluid and the flow system **before** deriving the governing equations of compressible gas dynamics (Continuity, Momentum, and Energy equations).

These assumptions simplify the otherwise intractable Navier–Stokes equations into a workable set of ODEs that describe **one-dimensional, steady, isentropic/adiabatic flow of an ideal (perfect) gas**. Without these assumptions, closed-form analytical solutions used to build the **isentropic flow tables**, **normal shock relations**, and **Fanno/Rayleigh flow charts** would not exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Module 2 (which covers Isentropic flow, area-velocity relations, and shock phenomena), these assumptions are the **silent foundation** of every equation you will encounter. Board examiners frequently ask: *"State the assumptions made in the analysis of compressible flow."* (Typical weightage: **3–5 marks**).

---

## 1.2 The Twelve Standard Assumptions of Compressible Flow

The universally accepted assumptions, as prescribed in standard gas dynamics references, are tabulated below for crystal-clear recall:

| # | Assumption | Physical Meaning |
|---|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Steady Flow** | Flow parameters at any point do not change with time: $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$ |
| 2 | **One-Dimensional Flow** | Flow properties are uniform across any cross-section and vary only along the streamline. |
| 3 | **Ideal Gas (Perfect Gas)** | The fluid obeys the equation of state: $p = \rho R T$ |
| 4 | **Calorically Perfect Gas** | Specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$) and the ratio $\gamma$ are **constants**, independent of temperature. |
| 5 | **No Body Forces** | Gravitational and electromagnetic forces are neglected (gravity is insignificant at high Mach numbers). |
| 6 | **Inviscid Flow** | The fluid has zero viscosity — applicable for inviscid core of high-speed flows. |
| 7 | **Adiabatic Process** | No heat transfer across the control surface: $q = 0$ |
| 8 | **No Shaft Work** | No work done by turbines, compressors, or pistons along the streamline: $w_s = 0$ |
| 9 | **Homogeneous & Single-Phase** | The fluid is a single chemical species with no phase change. |
| 10 | **No Chemical Reactions** | No combustion, dissociation, or ionization occurs. |
| 11 | **Continuum Hypothesis** | The fluid is treated as a continuous medium (Knudsen number $\ll 1$). |
| 12 | **Isentropic or Non-isentropic (with entropy change specified)** | Flow is either frictionless + adiabatic (isentropic), or has entropy change due to shocks/friction/heat addition. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Idealized Highway Traffic" Model**
>
> Imagine you are a traffic engineer studying vehicles flowing through a toll booth on a highway. To simplify your mathematics, you would assume:
> - Cars don't suddenly appear or disappear (**steady flow**).
> - All cars at a given point move at the **same speed** in the **same direction** (**one-dimensional**).
> - You ignore the effect of wind blowing cars sideways (**no body forces**).
> - You assume each car behaves like an "ideal car" with predictable fuel consumption (**perfect gas**).
> - The toll booth doesn't add or remove energy from cars (**adiabatic, no shaft work**).
>
> Compressible flow analysis uses **exactly the same philosophy**, but with a gas at speeds approaching or exceeding the **speed of sound** ($a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$). These assumptions allow engineers to predict the behavior of jet engines, rocket nozzles, and shock waves using neat algebraic formulas instead of solving millions of coupled differential equations.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are referenced throughout Module 2:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314\ \text{kJ/kmol·K}$
- **Specific Gas Constant for Air:** $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$
- **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$
- **Specific Heat at Constant Pressure (Air):** $C_p = 1004.5\ \text{J/kg·K}$
- **Specific Heat at Constant Volume (Air):** $C_v = 717.5\ \text{J/kg·K}$
- **Critical Speed of Sound in Air:** $a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** Always carry the value of $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$ in your mental cache — these two numbers alone unlock ~80% of Module 2 problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Assumption — Structured Logical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why "Steady Flow"?

In practical engineering systems like **jet engines**, **rocket nozzles**, and **wind tunnels**, the time-scale of operation is much larger than the time taken for the flow to traverse the domain. Hence, $\partial / \partial t = 0$ is justified.

- **Mathematical Consequence:** Removes time derivative from continuity and momentum equations.
- **Physical Consequence:** Engineers can measure parameters at one instant and use them throughout the design.

### 2.1.2 Why "One-Dimensional"?

The **Mach number** $M$ in most nozzles, diffusers, and ducts varies predominantly along the axis of the device. Cross-stream variations (boundary layers excepted) are second-order effects.

- **Mathematical Consequence:** All flow properties depend only on the axial coordinate $x$: $V = V(x)$, $p = p(x)$, $\rho = \rho(x)$.
- **Practical Utility:** Enables use of area $A(x)$ as a function of $x$ — the basis of the famous **Area-Velocity Relation**:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$$

### 2.1.3 Why "Ideal Gas"?

For air at temperatures up to roughly **500 K**, and for most engineering gases (N₂, O₂, CO₂, combustion products at moderate temperatures), the **ideal gas law** $p = \rho R T$ holds with less than 5% error. This single relation connects pressure, density, and temperature.

### 2.1.4 Why "Calorically Perfect Gas" ($\gamma = $ constant)?

For air below ~**800 K**, the vibrational modes of molecules are not excited. Hence $C_p$, $C_v$, and $\gamma$ remain effectively constant. This allows **closed-form** solutions for $T_0/T$, $p_0/p$, and $\rho_0/\rho$ in terms of Mach number alone:

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2$$

$$\frac{p_0}{p} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$$

$$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)^{1/(\gamma - 1)}$$

### 2.1.5 Why "Adiabatic, No Shaft Work"?

Most high-speed nozzles and diffusers are **insulated** and have **no rotating machinery** along the streamline. Hence the energy equation reduces to the **steady flow energy equation (SFEE)** in its simplest form:

$$h_0 = h + \frac{V^2}{2} = \text{constant}$$

This is the cornerstone of stagnation enthalpy, stagnation temperature, and the definition of stagnation sound speed $a_0$.

### 2.1.6 Why "Inviscid"?

In the **inviscid core** of a high-speed flow (away from boundary layers), viscous effects are negligible. This assumption is vital to derive:
- The **isentropic relations** for nozzles/diffusers.
- The **normal shock equations** (Rankine-Hugoniot).
- The **oblique shock** and **Prandtl-Meyer expansion** theories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Fanno flow (flow with friction) and Rayleigh flow (flow with heat addition) are the **two controlled relaxations** of these assumptions — they are addressed in later modules and form favorite KTU Part B questions.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Expression | Where Used |
|---|----------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $p = \rho R T$ | All problems |
| 2 | Speed of Sound | $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Mach number |
| 3 | Mach Number | $M = V/a = V/\sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | All problems |
| 4 | Stagnation Temperature Ratio | $T_0/T = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2$ | Isentropic flow |
| 5 | Stagnation Pressure Ratio | $p_0/p = (T_0/T)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$ | Isentropic flow |
| 6 | Stagnation Density Ratio | $\rho_0/\rho = (T_0/T)^{1/(\gamma - 1)}$ | Isentropic flow |
| 7 | Area-Velocity Relation | $\dfrac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\dfrac{dV}{V}$ | Nozzle/Diffuser design |
| 8 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{const}$ | Continuity |
| 9 | Critical Condition | $M = 1 \Rightarrow A = A^*$ | Choked flow |
| 10 | Stagnation Enthalpy (SFEE) | $h_0 = C_p T_0 = C_p T + V^2/2$ | Energy equation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These assumptions and equations are the daily bread of engineers at **ISRO** (rocket nozzle design), **HAL** (jet engine intakes), **DRDO** (wind tunnel testing), and **General Electric Aviation** (turbine blade cooling analysis).

---

## 2.3 The Assumption-Equation Dependency Map

| Assumption Relaxed | New Equation Introduced | New Flow Type |
|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Adiabatic → Heat added | $dq \neq 0$ | Rayleigh Flow |
| Inviscid → Friction | $df \neq 0$ | Fanno Flow |
| Isentropic → Entropy jump | $\Delta s \neq 0$ | Normal/Oblique Shock |
| 1-D → 2-D expansion | $\Delta s = 0$, wave pattern | Prandtl-Meyer Expansion |

This map is your **golden key** for Module 2 — every flow type you'll study is born from a controlled violation of one of these assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Energy Equation Reduces to $h_0 = $ constant

> **Objective:** Starting from the most general Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for an open system control volume, rigorously derive the constant stagnation enthalpy equation used in all compressible flow analyses.

### Step 1: Write the General SFEE

The most general SFEE applied to a 1-D control volume is:

$$q - w = \Delta h + \Delta \left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right) + \Delta g z$$

where:
- $q$ = heat transfer per unit mass
- $w$ = work per unit mass (shaft + flow work)
- $\Delta h$ = change in specific enthalpy
- $V$ = flow velocity
- $g z$ = potential energy per unit mass

### Step 2: Apply the Assumptions

Apply each KTU-mandated assumption one by one:

**Assumption 1 — Adiabatic Process:** $q = 0$

**Assumption 2 — No Shaft Work:** $w_s = 0$. Also, since the control volume has no moving boundaries in 1-D analysis, flow work is accounted for in enthalpy.

**Assumption 3 — No Body Forces:** Neglect gravity $\Rightarrow \Delta g z = 0$

Substituting:

$$0 - 0 = \Delta h + \Delta \left(\frac{V^2}{2}\right) + 0$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Differential Form

Rewriting for an infinitesimal control volume (between station 1 and station 2):

$$h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} = h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

### Step 4: Recognize the Constant of Motion

This holds for **any** two stations along the streamline. Hence:

$$h + \frac{V^2}{2} = h_0 = \text{constant}$$

$$\boxed{h_0 = h + \frac{V^2}{2}}$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Stagnation Temperature

Using $h = C_p T$ (valid for a calorically perfect gas):

$$C_p T_0 = C_p T + \frac{V^2}{2}$$

Dividing by $C_p T$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{V^2}{2 C_p T}$$

### Step 6: Introduce Mach Number

Recall $a^2 = \gamma R T$ and $C_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{V^2}{2 C_p T} = \frac{V^2}{2 T} \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma R} = \frac{V^2}{2 \gamma R T} \cdot (\gamma - 1) = \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{2} \cdot \frac{V^2}{a^2} = \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{2} M^2$$

### Step 7: Final Compact Result

$$\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2$$

This is the **single most important equation** in compressible flow. Every isentropic table in the world begins with this relation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Board Exam Insight:** Examiners often award marks if you explicitly write each assumption **before** plugging it into the equation. This is called *showing "due diligence"* in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Area-Velocity Relation (Preview for Module 2)

### Step 1: Continuity Equation

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$$

### Step 2: Logarithmic Differentiation

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

### Step 3: Momentum Equation (Euler form, inviscid)

$$\frac{dp}{\rho} + V\,dV = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow dp = -\rho V\,dV$$

### Step 4: Speed of Sound Relation (Isentropic)

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = \frac{dp}{d\rho}\big|_s$$

$$\Rightarrow d\rho = \frac{dp}{a^2} = -\frac{\rho V\,dV}{a^2}$$

### Step 5: Substituting Back into Continuity

$$-\frac{\rho V\,dV}{a^2 \rho} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

$$-\frac{V\,dV}{a^2} + \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

### Step 6: Define Mach Number and Rearrange

Using $M^2 = V^2 / a^2$:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{V\,dV}{a^2} - \frac{dV}{V} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** This single equation reveals the **diverging nozzle behavior** ($A \uparrow \Rightarrow V \uparrow$) for $M < 1$ and the **converging-diverging nozzle behavior** for $M > 1$. This is the heart of Module 2.

---

## 3.3 The Continuity Assumption: Why "No Mass Addition"?

The continuity equation derivation assumes the flow has **no fuel injection**, **no air bleed**, and **no boundary layer suction**. In reality:
- Rocket nozzles inject fuel → violates the assumption.
- Bleed systems on jet engine inlets extract air → violates the assumption.

In such cases, the equation becomes:

$$\dot{m}_2 - \dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_{\text{injected}}$$

For KTU standard problems, this is taken as **zero**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Hierarchy of Assumptions in Compressible Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressible Flow Analysis] --> B[Kinematic Assumptions]
    A --> C[Thermodynamic Assumptions]
    A --> D[Transport Assumptions]
    A --> E[Geometric Assumptions]

    B --> B1[Steady Flow]
    B --> B2[One-Dimensional]
    B --> B3[Continuum]

    C --> C1[Ideal Gas]
    C --> C2[Calorically Perfect Gas]
    C --> C3[Adiabatic Process]
    C --> C4[No Shaft Work]

    D --> D1[Inviscid Flow]
    D --> D2[No Body Forces]

    E --> E1[Single-Phase]
    E --> E2[No Chemical Reactions]
    E --> E3[Homogeneous]

    B1 --> R1[Reduces Continuity to d by d t equals 0]
    B2 --> R2[Enables Area Velocity Relation]
    B3 --> R3[Validates Navier Stokes Framework]
    C1 --> R4[Yields p equals rho R T]
    C2 --> R5[Yields Constant gamma]
    C3 --> R6[Yields h0 equals constant]
    C4 --> R7[SFEE Simplifies]
    D1 --> R8[Enables Euler Equation]
    D2 --> R9[Neglects Gravity Potential]
    E1 --> R10[Enables Single EOS]
    E2 --> R11[No Source Term]
    E3 --> R12[Uniform Properties]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Assumptions Feed Into Governing Equations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Layer 1 Physical Reality] --> node2[Layer 2 Apply Assumptions]
    node2 --> node3[Layer 3 Governing Equations]
    node3 --> node4[Layer 4 Analytical Solutions]
    node4 --> node5[Layer 5 Engineering Design]

    node2 --> A1[Steady]
    node2 --> A2[One D]
    node2 --> A3[Ideal Gas]
    node2 --> A4[Inviscid]
    node2 --> A5[Adiabatic]

    node3 --> E1[Continuity]
    node3 --> E2[Momentum]
    node3 --> E3[Energy]
    node3 --> E4[State Equation]

    node4 --> S1[Isentropic Flow Tables]
    node4 --> S2[Normal Shock Relations]
    node4 --> S3[Oblique Shock Chart]
    node4 --> S4[Fanno Line]
    node4 --> S5[Rayleigh Line]

    node5 --> D1[Rocket Nozzles]
    node5 --> D2[Jet Engine Inlets]
    node5 --> D3[Wind Tunnel Diffusers]
    node5 --> D4[Gas Turbines]
    node5 --> D5[Supersonic Wind Tunnels]

    style node1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node4 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style node5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree — Which Assumption to Relax?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Isentropic Baseline] --> Q1{Heat Added?}
```
