# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Fanno flow and state any four assumptions used in its analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fanno flow** is defined as the steady, one-dimensional, adiabatic flow of a fluid through a constant cross-sectional area duct with friction being the only irreversibility.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. **Steady flow** — flow properties do not change with time.
> 2. **One-dimensional flow** — properties uniform over any cross-section.
> 3. **Adiabatic process** — no heat transfer across duct walls (**dQ = 0**).
> 4. **Constant cross-sectional area** — dA = 0.
> 5. **No shaft work** — dW = 0.
> 6. **Ideal gas** with constant specific heats.
> 7. **Friction is the only cause of entropy increase** — ds ≥ 0 (with equality only for reversible flow, but Fanno flow has ds > 0).

**[Marks Distribution: Definition = 1, Assumptions = 2]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Sketch the Fanno curve on a temperature-entropy (T-s) diagram and label the critical point.**

**Model Answer:**

```
   T │      ●───── T₀ = constant (since h₀ is constant, T₀ is constant)
     │     ╱
     │    ╱
     │   ╱
     │  ●   ← M = 1, s = s_max (peak point)
     │   ╲
     │    ╲
     │     ╲
     │      ●
     └─────────────────► s
           s_max
```

- The two branches (subsonic on the left, supersonic on the right) meet at **M = 1** which is the **maximum entropy** point.
- Both branches approach **T₀ = constant** as M → 0 or M → ∞.
- The curve is bounded above by an isothermal line **T = T₀**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the Fanno parameter (4fL*/D) as a function of the inlet Mach number M₁. State clearly the assumptions used.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Air enters a constant-area duct at M₁ = 0.3, P₁ = 5 bar, T₁ = 350 K. The friction factor is f = 0.005 and the duct diameter is D = 0.15 m. Calculate:**
  1. **The maximum length of the duct (L*) before the flow chokes.**
  2. **The static pressure, temperature, and velocity at the exit (M = 1).**
  3. **The stagnation pressure loss (P₀₁ − P₀₂) across the duct.**

**Given:** γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/kg·K, cₚ = 1005 J/kg·K.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Assumptions [1 mark]:**
- Steady, 1-D, adiabatic, constant area, ideal gas, friction only irreversibility.

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Governing equations:**

Momentum: $dP + \rho V dV + (4f/D)(\rho V^2/2)dL = 0$

Energy (adiabatic): $dh + V dV = 0 \Rightarrow dH = -V dV$

Continuity: $\rho V = \text{const} \Rightarrow d\rho/\rho + dV/V = 0$

**Step 2 [1 mark] — Mach number relations:**

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2}\cdot\frac{dM}{M} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dP}{P} = -\gamma M^2 \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 3 [2 marks] — Combine to get separable form:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} + \gamma M^2 \frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{2\gamma M^2 f\,dL}{D}$$

Using the substitution for dV/V in terms of dM/M and integrating from M₁ to M = 1:

**Step 4 [2 marks] — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2} + \frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_1^2}{2 + (\gamma-1)M_1^2}\right]}$$

> **Why this form?** The first term dominates near M = 0 (logarithmic divergence as M → 0); the second term is the entropy-generation correction.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** M₁ = 0.3, P₁ = 5 × 10⁵ Pa, T₁ = 350 K, f = 0.005, D = 0.15 m, γ = 1.4.

**Step 1: Compute 4fL*/D at M₁ = 0.3 [2 marks]**

$$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - (0.3)^2}{1.4 \cdot (0.3)^2} + \frac{1.4+1}{2(1.4)}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.4 \cdot (0.3)^2}{2 + 0.4 \cdot (0.3)^2}\right]$$

First term:
$$\frac{1 - 0.09}{1.4 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.91}{0.126} = 7.2222$$

Second term:
$$\frac{2.4}{2.8}\ln\!\left[\frac{0.216}{2.036}\right] = 0.8571 \times \ln(0.1061) = 0.8571 \times (-2.2441) = -1.9235$$

Sum:
$$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = 7.2222 - 1.9235 = 5.2987$$

**Step 2: Compute L* [1 mark]**

$$L^* = \frac{5.2987 \times 0.15}{4 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.7948}{0.02} = 39.74 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Properties at exit (M = 1) [2 marks]**

$$T^* = T_0 \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma+1} = T_1 \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right) \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma+1}$$

$$T_0 = 350 \times \left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.09\right) = 350 \times 1.018 = 356.3 \text{ K}$$

$$T^* = 356.3 \times \frac{2}{2.4} = 356.3 \times 0.8333 = 296.92 \text{ K} \approx 297 \text{ K}$$

$$P^* = P_0 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$$

$$P_0 = P_1 \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 5 \times 10^5 \times (1.018)^{3.5}$$

$$(1.018)^{3.5} \approx 1.0647 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 \approx 5.3235 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P^* = 5.3235 \times 10^5 \times 0.5283 = 2.8124 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.81 \text{ bar}$$

$$V^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 296.92} = \sqrt{119,314} \approx 345.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Stagnation pressure loss [2 marks]**

The stagnation pressure ratio from M₁ to M = 1:

$$\frac{P_{0,2}}{P_{0,1}} = \frac{M_1}{1}\sqrt{\frac{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}(1)^2}} = 0.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.018}{1.2}}$$

$$= 0.3 \times \sqrt{0.8483} = 0.3 \times 0.9210 = 0.2763$$

$$P_{0,2} = 0.2763 \times 5.3235 \times 10^5 = 1.4709 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.47 \text{ bar}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_0 = P_{0,1} - P_{0,2} = 5.3235 - 1.4709 = 3.85 \text{ bar}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks] With the aid of neat sketches, describe the Fanno curve on:**
  1. **h-s diagram**
  2. **P-v diagram**

   **Explain the significance of the point M = 1 in both diagrams.**

**(b) [7 Marks] A combustion gas (γ = 1.33, R = 320 J/kg·K) flows through a constant-area duct with inlet conditions M₁ = 2.5, P₁ = 1.2 bar, T₁ = 800 K. The friction factor is f = 0.0035 and duct diameter is D = 0.20 m. Find:**
  1. **The maximum length of the duct (L*) before the flow chokes.**
  2. **The mass flow rate per unit area (G = ρV).**
  3. **The static pressure and temperature when the flow has decelerated to M₂ = 1.5.**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Fanno Curve Sketches [7 Marks]

**[3 marks] h-s Diagram:**

The Fanno curve on an h-s diagram is bounded above by the horizontal line h = h₀ (stagnation enthalpy is constant). It opens to the LEFT and has its peak at the point of maximum entropy (s = s_max) where M = 1.

```
   h │           h = h₀  (stagnation enthalpy constant)
   h₀├────────────●━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━━━
     │             ╲      ╱
     │              ╲    ╱
     │               ╲  ╱
     │                ╲╱
     │                 ●  ← M = 1, s = s_max
     │                 
     └──────────────────────► s
                s_max ↑
```

- **Subsonic branch:** lies on the upper-left (M < 1)
- **Supersonic branch:** lies on the upper-right (M > 1)
- **Flow direction:** horizontal lines moving rightward (s increases)

**[3 marks] P-v Diagram:**

The Fanno curve on a P-v diagram is a monotonically decreasing curve from upper-left to lower-right, with the critical (M = 1) point in the middle.

```
   P │ ●
     │   ╲
     │     ●  ← M = 1 (max entropy)
     │       ╲
     │         ●
     │           ╲
     │             ●
     │               ╲
     │                 ●
     │                   ●
     │                      ●
     └──────────────────────────► v
```

- Upper-left: M → 0, P → P₀
- Middle: M = 1, P = P*, v = v* (max entropy)
- Lower-right: M → ∞, P → 0

**[1 mark] Significance of M = 1:**

At M = 1:
- **Entropy is maximum** for the given stagnation state.
- The flow is **choked** — the mass flow rate is maximum.
- The point separates the **subsonic** and **supersonic** branches.
- It is the **limit** of Fanno process — fluid cannot be decelerated further in supersonic branch or accelerated further in subsonic branch by friction alone.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:** M₁ = 2.5, P₁ = 1.2 × 10⁵ Pa, T₁ = 800 K, f = 0.0035, D = 0.20 m, γ = 1.33, R = 320 J/kg·K.

**Step 1: Compute 4fL*/D at M₁ = 2.5 [2 marks]**

$$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - (2.5)^2}{1.33 \cdot (2.5)^2} + \frac{1.33+1}{2(1.33)}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.33 \cdot (2.5)^2}{2 + 0.33 \cdot (2.5)^2}\right]$$

First term:
$$\frac{1 - 6.25}{1.33 \times 6.25} = \frac{-5.25}{8.3125} = -0.6316$$

Second term:
$$\frac{2.33}{2.66}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.33 \times 6.25}{2 + 0.33 \times 6.25}\right] = 0.876 \times \ln\!\left[\frac{14.5625}{4.0625}\right]$$

$$= 0.876 \times \ln(3.5846) = 0.876 \times 1.2766 = 1.1183$$

Sum:
$$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = -0.6316 + 1.1183 = 0.4867$$

**Step 2: Maximum length L* [1 mark]**

$$L^* = \frac{0.4867 \times 0.20}{4 \times 0.0035} = \frac{0.0973}{0.014} = 6.95 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate per unit area G [1 mark]**

$$V_1 = M_1 \sqrt{\gamma R T_1} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{1.33 \times 320 \times 800} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{340,480}$$

$$V_1 = 2.5 \times 583.5 = 1458.7 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1}{RT_1} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{320 \times 800} = \frac{120000}{256000} = 0.4688 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$G = \rho_1 V_1 = 0.4688 \times 1458.7 = 683.8 \text{ kg/(s·m}^2)$$

**Step 4: Properties at M₂ = 1.5 [3 marks]**

**Temperature at M₂:**

$$T_0 = T_1\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right) = 800\left(1 + 0.165 \times 6.25\right) = 800 \times 2.0313 = 1625 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{T_0}{1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_2^2} = \frac{1625}{1 + 0.165 \times 2.25} = \frac{1625}{1.3713} = 1185 \text{ K}$$

**Pressure at M₂:**

$$P_0 = P_1\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \times (2.0313)^{4.0303}$$

$$(2.0313)^{4.0303} \approx 18.36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 \approx 22.03 \text{ bar}$$

$$P_2 = \frac{P_0}{\left(1 + 0.165 M_2^2\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}} = \frac{22.03 \times 10^5}{(1.3713)^{4.0303}}$$

$$(1.3713)^{4.0303} \approx 2.78 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_2 \approx \frac{22.03}{2.78} = 7.92 \text{ bar}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 1185 \text{ K}, \quad P_2 \approx 7.92 \text{ bar}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Fanno Flow Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that h₀ is constant (not h).** Students often write $h_2 = h_1$ which is **wrong** — the *static* enthalpy changes; only the *stagnation* enthalpy is preserved.
>
> 2. **Confusing T₀ and T***. The stagnation temperature T₀ is **always greater** than the critical temperature T* (since T* = T₀ × 2/(γ+1) < T₀).
>
> 3. **Sign of velocity change.** In subsonic Fanno flow, friction **accelerates** the flow (V increases), not decelerates. In supersonic Fanno flow, friction **decelerates** the flow. Conflating these is a common error.
>
> 4. **Friction factor convention.** The KTU convention uses the **Darcy friction factor f** in 4fL/D. Some books use the **Fanning friction factor Cf** in Cf·L/D = 4fL/D. Mixing these up leads to factor-of-4 errors in L*.
>
> 5. **M is NOT equal to V/a₀.** Always use V/a (local speed of sound), not V/a₀ (stagnation speed of sound). This is a subtle but critical distinction.
>
> 6. **Inlet vs outlet starred properties.** When the problem says "flow at M₁ = 0.3 in a pipe of length L > L*", the inlet is NOT at the starred condition — the starred condition is the **choked exit**. Re-read the problem statement carefully.
>
> 7. **Logarithm arguments must be dimensionless and positive.** The expression $\ln[(\gamma+1)M^2 / (2 + (\gamma-1)M^2)]$ is always valid (between 0 and 1) for finite M, so this is rarely an issue, but watch the algebra.
>
> 8. **Units of fL*/D are dimensionless.** Don't write "m" — the value is purely a number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fanno Curve on h-s and P-v Diagrams**

- [x] **Fanno flow** = adiabatic + constant area + friction (only irreversibility)
- [x] **Stagnation enthalpy h₀ is constant** (this is the key constraint)
- [x] **Static enthalpy h decreases** as friction converts KE to heat
- [x] **Entropy s always increases** (second law: ds > 0)
- [x] **Maximum entropy** occurs at **M = 1** (peak of Fanno curve on h-s)
- [x] **Fanno curve on h-s** opens LEFT, peak at M = 1, bounded above by h = h₀
- [x] **Fanno curve on P-v** monotonically decreases from upper-left to lower-right
- [x] **Friction accelerates subsonic flow** and **decelerates supersonic flow** (always towards M = 1)
- [x] **Critical (starred) properties at M = 1:**
  - T*/T₀ = 2/(γ+1) = 0.8333 (air)
  - P*/P₀ = 0.5283 (air)
  - ρ*/ρ₀ = 0.6339 (air)
- [x] **Fanno parameter formula:** 4fL*/D = (1−M²)/(γM²) + (γ+1)/(2γ)·ln[(γ+1)M²/(2+(γ−1)M²)]
- [x] **Stagnation pressure drops** along the Fanno process (P₀₂ < P₀₁)
- [x] **Two branches:** subsonic (M < 1) on left of peak; supersonic (M > 1) on right
- [x] **Engineering applications:** gas pipelines, jet engine ducts, rocket nozzles, wind tunnels
- [x] **The fluid's actual path on h-s is a horizontal line** at h = h₀ moving right (NOT the Fanno curve itself)
- [x] **Maximum L* is finite** for any inlet M ≠ 1; beyond it, the upstream condition must change

---

*End of Notes — PEMET632, Module 2, Topic: Fanno Curve on h-s and P-v Diagram*

**Total study time recommended: 4–5 hours** (including numerical problem practice).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fanno flow relations for a perfect gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fanno flow relations for a perfect gas # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Fanno Flow** is defined as the **steady, one-dimensional, adiabatic (no heat transfer) flow of a perfect gas through a constant cross-sectional area duct with friction (wall shear stress) being the only cause of entropy change and pressure drop.** The Fanno flow model assumes: 1. **Perfect gas** with constant specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$) and constant ratio of specific heats, $\gamma = C_p/C_v$. 2. **Steady flow** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$). 3. **One-dimensional flow** (properties uniform across any cross-section). 4. **Adiabatic duct** ($dQ = 0$ → no heat transfer across the walls). 5. **Constant area** ($dA = 0$). 6. **Friction present** ($df \neq 0$ → wall shear stress causes losses). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The Fanno flow equations give the variation of fluid properties ($T$, $P$, $\rho$, $V$, $M$, $s$) along the duct length as a function of the **Mach number** and the **friction parameter** $\dfrac{4fL}{D}$ (or $4fL^*/D$). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine water flowing through a long, narrow garden hose. The hose is rigid (constant area), no heat is added or lost, but the **inner wall rubs against the water** (friction). This friction: - **Slows the water down** slightly, - Creates a **pressure drop** along the length, - And **heats the water** a tiny bit due to viscous dissipation. Now, replace that water with **air moving at high speed (compressible)** through a long pipe (like a jet engine tailpipe or a gas pipeline). The behaviour is similar, but much more dramatic: - Because air is compressible, density and temperature also change. - As friction acts, the flow may **accelerate** (subsonic) or **decelerate** (supersonic), always tending to drive the Mach number toward **unity (1.0)**. - At $M=1$, the flow becomes **choked**, and the entropy reaches a **maximum**. The length required to reach this state is the **maximum length, $L^*$**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** "Fanno" flow = **F**riction in a **A**diabatic duct of constant **N**o **N**on-uniform **O**ne-d area → all $dA=0$, $dQ=0$ except $df \neq 0$. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Reference States | Symbol | Meaning | Value/Note | |---|---|---| | $\gamma$ | Ratio of specific heats | **1.4** for air | | $R$ | Universal gas constant | **287 J/(kg·K)** for air | | $C_p$ | Specific heat at constant pressure | **1004.5 J/(kg·K)** for air | | $C_v$ | Specific heat at constant volume | **717.5 J/(kg·K)** for air | | $a$ | Speed of sound | $\sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | | $^*$ (asterisk) | Denotes property at the **choked** (sonic) condition $M=1$ | Reference state | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations Starting from the **continuity**, **momentum**, and **energy** equations for steady 1-D flow with $dA=0$: **Continuity Equation:** $$\rho V = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$ **Momentum Equation (with friction):** $$V\,dV + \frac{dP}{\rho} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ where $f$ is the **Fanning friction factor** and $D$ is the hydraulic diameter. **Energy Equation (adiabatic):** $$h + \frac{V^2}{2} = h_0 = \text{constant}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a perfect gas, the stagnation enthalpy $h_0 = C_p T_0$ remains constant, meaning the **stagnation temperature $T_0$ is constant** in Fanno flow. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow 1. **Energy equation** → relates $T$ and $V$ → since $T_0$ is constant, a velocity change corresponds to a temperature change. 2. **Continuity equation** → relates $\rho$ and $V$ → mass flow rate is conserved. 3. **Momentum equation** → includes friction → pressure drop is governed by $f$ and $L/D$. 4. **Equation of state** → $P = \rho R T$ → closes the system. Combining these yields the **Fanno flow equation** and the property ratios. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorize the structure, not just the result.** ### A. Fanno Flow Property Ratios (vs. Choked State, $M=1$) | Property | Ratio Expression | Valid Range | |---|---|---| | $\dfrac{T}{T^*}$ | $\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}$ | All $M$ | | $\dfrac{P}{P^*}$ | $\dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$ | All $M$ | | $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho^*}$ | $\dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}$ | All $M$ | | $\dfrac{V}{V^*}$ | $M \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$ | All $M$ | | $\dfrac{s - s^*}{R}$ | $-\ln M^2 - \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}\right] + \text{const}$ | All $M$ | > **KTU Note:** For all property ratios $T/T^*$, $P/P^*$, $\rho/\rho^*$, $V/V^*$: the value is **1.0** at $M = 1$ and approaches **0** or $\infty$ as $M \to 0$ or $M \to \infty$ (except $T/T^* \to (\gamma+1)/2\gamma$ as $M \to \infty$). ### B. Friction Parameter Equations | Friction Parameter | Expression | Definition | |---|---|---| | Dimensionless length | $\dfrac{4fL^*}{D}$ | Max length to reach $M=1$ from $M$ | | Fanno equation (differential) | $\dfrac{4fL^*}{D} = \dfrac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]$ | For subsonic/super-sonic → $M$ | | Fanno equation (integral form) | $\dfrac{4f(L_2 - L_1)}{D} = \left[\dfrac{1 - M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2} + \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{(\gamma + 1)M_1^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M_1^2}\right)\right] - \left[\dfrac{1 - M_2^2}{\gamma M_2^2} + \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{(\gamma + 1)M_2^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M_2^2}\right)\right]$ | Between two stations | > [!TIP] > **Shortcut Notation:** $\dfrac{4fL^*}{D} = F(M)$, where $F(M)$ is a function tabulated in the standard **Fanno flow tables** for $\gamma = 1.4$. ### C. Special Values at the Sonic (Choked) Condition | Quantity | Value at $M=1$ | |---|---| | $T/T^*$ | $1$ | | $P/P^*$ | $1$ | | $\rho/\rho^*$ | $1$ | | $V/V^*$ | $1$ | | $T^*/T_0$ | $\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1} = 0.8333$ (for $\gamma=1.4$) | | $P^*/P_0$ | $\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = 0.5283$ (for $\gamma=1.4$) | | $\rho^*/\rho_0$ | $\left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{1/(\gamma-1)} = 0.6339$ (for $\gamma=1.4$) | | $V^*/a^*$ | $1$ (definition) | | $V^*/a_0$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\cdot\dfrac{T^*}{T_0}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\cdot\dfrac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}}$ — for $\gamma=1.4$: $V^*/a_0 = 0.9129$ | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Where Fanno Flow Occurs | |---|---| | **Jet engine tailpipes** | Adiabatic, constant-area duct with wall friction | | **Gas pipelines** | Long-distance high-pressure gas transport | | **Wind tunnels** | Test-section flow with wall friction | | **Rocket nozzles** | Combustion chamber to nozzle exit (with friction) | | **Steam turbines (low-pressure)** | Approximated as Fanno flow at low Mach | | **Meteorological flows** | Adiabatic atmospheric ducting | > **Why does it matter?** In any constant-area, insulated, friction-bearing duct, **Fanno relations are the only tool** that gives you the property variation and the **maximum permissible length** before choking. Beyond $L^*$, the flow cannot accelerate/decelerate further — **choking occurs** and the upstream conditions are fixed. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fanno Flow Equation ### Step 1 — Combine Continuity, Momentum, and Energy Starting from the differential momentum equation for steady 1-D flow with friction and constant area: $$V\,dV + \frac{dP}{\rho} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ Divide through by $V^2$: $$\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dP}{\rho V^2} + \frac{4f}{2D}dx = 0$$ Using continuity $\dfrac{dV}{V} = -\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ and the isentropic speed of sound relation $\dfrac{dP}{\rho} = -a^2 \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$: $$\frac{dV}{V} - \frac{a^2}{\rho V^2}\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{4f}{2D}dx = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{a^2}{V^2}\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4f}{2D}dx = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Introduce Mach Number Since $M = V/a$, and using the energy equation to relate $a$ to $V$: $$T_0 = T + \frac{V^2}{2C_p} = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)$$ So: $$\frac{a^2}{V^2} = \frac{1}{M^2}$$ Substituting: $$\frac{dV}{V}\left(1 + \frac{1}{M^2}\right) + \frac{4f}{2D}dx = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Express $dV/V$ in terms of $M$ Differentiating $M^2 = V^2/(\gamma R T)$ and using $T_0 = $ const: $$2M\,dM = M^2\left[\frac{2dV}{V} - \frac{dT}{T}\right]$$ With $dT/T_0 = -(\gamma-1)M^2 dM/[M(1 + (\gamma-1)M^2/2)]$ (from the energy equation), this simplifies to: $$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{dM}{M}\left[\frac{1 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2}\right]$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and Integrate After substitution into the momentum equation and simplification: $$\frac{4f}{D}dx = -\frac{dM}{M}\left[\frac{(1 - M^2)(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2)}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2 \cdot \frac{1 + (\gamma-1)M^2}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2}}\right]$$ The simplified form, after integration from station 1 to station 2: $$\frac{4f(L_2 - L_1)}{D} = \left[\frac{1 - M_1^2}{\gamma M_1^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left(\frac{(\gamma + 1)M_1^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M_1^2}\right)\right] - \left[\frac{1 - M_2^2}{\gamma M_2^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left(\frac{(\gamma + 1)M_2^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M_2^2}\right)\right]$$ This is the **Fanno flow equation** in its complete form. ### Step 5 — Maximum Length Form For the case where $M_2 = 1$ (choked), $L_2 = L^*$, and the expression reduces to: $$\boxed{\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Property Ratios ### Temperature Ratio From $T_0 = T(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2)$ and $T^* = T_0/(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}) = T_0 \cdot \dfrac{2}{\gamma + 1}$: $$\frac{T}{T^*} = \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}}{\ 1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}$$ ### Pressure Ratio Using $P/P_0 = [1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2]^{-\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ and similarly for $P^*$: $$\frac{P}{P^*} = \left[\frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2}\right]^{1/2}\cdot \frac{1}{M} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$$ ### Density Ratio From continuity, $\rho V = \rho^* V^*$ → $\rho/\rho^* = V^*/V$. With $V^*/V = (M^*/M)\cdot(a^*/a) = (1/M)\sqrt{T^*/T}$: $$\frac{\rho}{\rho^*} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{T^*}{T}} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}$$ ### Velocity Ratio By continuity, $V/V^* = \rho^*/\rho$: $$\frac{V}{V^*} = M\sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Behaviour — Limit Cases Let us verify the limits to build intuition: | Limit | $M \to 0$ (subsonic → 0) | $M = 1$ (choked) | $M \to \infty$ (supersonic → ∞) | |---|---|---|---| | $T/T^*$ | $(\gamma+1)/2 \approx 1.2$ | $1$ | $0$ | | $P/P^*$ | $\to \infty$ | $1$ | $0$ | | $\rho/\rho^*$ | $\to \infty$ | $1$ | $0$ | | $V/V^*$ | $0$ | $1$ | $\to \infty$ | | $4fL^*/D$ | $\to \infty$ | $0$ | $\to \infty$ | | $s - s^*$ | $0$ (max entropy) | $0$ (max) | $0$ (max) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** The entropy change $s - s^*$ is **zero at the sonic condition** and negative (or zero) on either side. The function $s - s^*$ is therefore a **maximum** at $M = 1$ on the Fanno curve. The two branches (subsonic and supersonic) approach this maximum. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Validation — Worked Derivation **Problem:** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$) enters a Fanno duct at $M_1 = 0.5$. Find $T_1/T^*$, $P_1/P^*$, $\rho_1/\rho^*$, and $4fL_1^*/D$. **Solution:** **Temperature ratio:** $$\frac{T_1}{T^*} = \frac{1.4 + 1}{2 + (1.4 - 1)(0.5)^2} = \frac{2.4}{2 + 0.1} = \frac{2.4}{2.1} \approx 1.143$$ **Pressure ratio:** $$\frac{P_1}{P^*} = \frac{1}{0.5}\sqrt{\frac{2.4}{2.1}} = 2 \times \sqrt{1.1429} \approx 2 \times 1.0690 \approx 2.138$$ **Density ratio:** $$\frac{\rho_1}{\rho^*} = \frac{1}{0.5}\sqrt{\frac{2.1}{2.4}} = 2 \times \sqrt{0.8750} \approx 2 \times 0.9354 \approx 1.871$$ **Friction parameter (subsonic branch):** $$\frac{4fL_1^*}{D} = \frac{1 - 0.25}{1.4 \times 0.25} + \frac{2.4}{2.8}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.4 \times 0.25}{2.1}\right]$$ $$= \frac{0.75}{0.35} + 0.8571 \times \ln(0.2857) = 2.1429 + 0.8571 \times (-1.2528)$$ $$\approx 2.1429 - 1.0739 \approx 1.069$$ > [!NOTE] > These values are **independently verifiable** against any standard Fanno flow table for $\gamma = 1.4$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fanno Flow Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INPUT SPECIFICATIONS:
Inlet Mach M1
Inlet Pressure P1
Inlet Temperature T1

Duct diameter D
Friction factor f] --> B[GAS PROPERTY DATABASE

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define Fanno flow. List the key assumptions used in deriving Fanno flow relations.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Fanno flow** is the steady, one-dimensional, adiabatic flow of a perfect gas through a **constant cross-sectional area duct** with **friction** as the only cause of pressure drop and entropy change. > > **Key Assumptions:** > 1. Perfect gas with constant specific heats ($\gamma = $ const). > 2. Steady, one-dimensional flow. > 3. Adiabatic duct ($dQ = 0$). > 4. Constant area ($dA = 0$). > 5. No shaft work ($dW = 0$). > 6. Friction present, characterized by the Fanning friction factor $f$. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State the Fanno flow equation. What is the significance of the parameter $4fL^*/D$?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Fanno flow equation gives the **maximum length of duct** $L^*$ required for a fluid to reach the sonic condition ($M = 1$) from an initial Mach number $M$: > $$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]$$ > > **Significance:** > - $L^*$ is the **maximum permissible length** of the duct before the flow chokes. > - If actual duct length $L > L^*$, the inlet conditions adjust; the flow is limited to a lower Mach number at inlet. > - It defines the **choking criterion** for Fanno flow. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the basic governing equations for steady 1-D flow, derive the Fanno flow equation: $$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]$$ **(b) [7 Marks]** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$) flows in a constant-area duct with inlet conditions $P_1 = 1.0$ MPa, $T_1 = 500$ K, and $M_1 = 0.4$. The duct has a diameter of 0.05 m and a Fanning friction factor $f = 0.005$. Determine: - (i) The maximum duct length $L^*$ for the flow to remain unchoked. - (ii) The pressure, temperature, and density at the choked exit if the actual length is $L^*$. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** > **Step 1 — Governing Equations:** > **Continuity:** $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = 0$ > **Momentum (1-D, with friction, constant A):** $V\,dV + \dfrac{dP}{\rho} + \dfrac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$ > **Energy (adiabatic):** $C_p T_0 = C_p T + \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ → $T_0$ = constant. > **Step 2 — Differentiate Energy Equation:** > $$C_p\,dT + V\,dV = 0 \Rightarrow dT = -\frac{V\,dV}{C_p}$$ > **Step 3 — Speed of Sound Relation:** > Using $a^2 = \gamma R T$ and $P = \rho R T$: > $$\frac{dP}{\rho} = \gamma R\,dT - a^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$ > **Step 4 — Use Continuity:** $d\rho/\rho = -dV/V$, so: > $$\frac{dP}{\rho} = \gamma R\,dT + a^2 \frac{dV}{V}$$ > **Step 5 — Substitute into Momentum:** > $$V\,dV + \gamma R\,dT + a^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ > **Step 6 — Eliminate $dT$ via Energy:** > $$V\,dV + \gamma R\left(-\frac{V\,dV}{C_p}\right) + a^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ > $$V\,dV\left(1 - \frac{\gamma R}{C_p}\right) + a^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ > Since $\gamma R / C_p = (\gamma - 1)$: > $$V\,dV(2 - \gamma) + a^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4fV^2}{2D}dx = 0$$ > **Step 7 — Divide by $V^2$ and use $M = V/a$, $a^2 = V^2/M^2$:** > $$\frac{dV}{V}(2 - \gamma) + \frac{1}{M^2}\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{4f}{2D}dx = 0$$ > $$\frac{dV}{V}\left(2 - \gamma + \frac{1}{M^2}\right) = -\frac{2f}{D}dx$$ > **Step 8 — Replace $dV/V$ in terms of $dM$:** > From $M^2 = V^2 / (\gamma R T)$ and energy equation: > $$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2}\cdot\frac{dM}{M}$$ > **Step 9 — Final Integration:** > After substitution and integration from $M$ to $1$ (over length $L^*$): > $$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]$$ > ∎ --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** > **Given:** $P_1 = 1.0$ MPa, $T_1 = 500$ K, $M_1 = 0.4$, $D = 0.05$ m, $f = 0.005$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). > **Step 1 — Compute $4fL^*/D$ at $M_1 = 0.4$:** > $$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - 0.16}{1.4 \times 0.16} + \frac{2.4}{2.8}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.4 \times 0.16}{2 + 0.4 \times 0.16}\right]$$ > $$= \frac{0.84}{0.224} + 0.8571 \times \ln\!\left[\frac{0.384}{2.064}\right]$$ > $$= 3.75 + 0.8571 \times \ln(0.1860) = 3.75 + 0.8571 \times (-1.6819)$$ > $$\approx 3.75 - 1.4420 = 2.308$$ > **Step 2 — Solve for $L^*$:** > $$L^* = \frac{2.308 \times 0.05}{4 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.1154}{0.02} = 5.77 \text{ m}$$ > **Step 3 — Property ratios at $M_1 = 0.4$:** > $$T_1/T^* = \frac{2.4}{2 + 0.4 \times 0.16} = \frac{2.4}{2.064} = 1.1628$$ > $$P_1/P^* = \frac{1}{0.4}\sqrt{1.1628} = 2.5 \times 1.0784 = 2.6960$$ > $$\rho_1/\rho^* = \frac{1}{0.4}\sqrt{\frac{2.064}{2.4}} = 2.5 \times 0.9273 = 2.3183$$ > **Step 4 — Compute $\rho_1$ (and hence $\rho^*$):** > $$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^6}{287 \times 500} = 6.97 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ > $$\rho^* = \frac{\rho_1}{2.3183} = \frac{6.97}{2.3183} \approx 3.006 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ > **Step 5 — Compute $T^*$ and $P^*$:** > $$T^* = \frac{T_1}{1.1628} = \frac{500}{1.1628} = 430.0 \text{ K}$$ > $$P^* = \frac{P_1}{2.6960} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^6}{2.6960} \approx 0.3709 \text{ MPa}$$ > **Step 6 — Verification via ideal gas law:** > $$P^* = \rho^* R T^* = 3.006 \times 287 \times 430.0 \approx 0.3709 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \;\checkmark$$ > **Final Answers (Q-A-b):** > - **(i) Maximum length $L^* \approx 5.77$ m** > - **(ii) At choked exit:** $T^* \approx 430.0$ K, $P^* \approx 0.3709$ MPa, $\rho^* \approx 3.006$ kg/m³. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Alternative]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Fanno flow property ratios: $$\frac{T}{T^*} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}, \quad \frac{P}{P^*} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}, \quad \frac{\rho}{\rho^*} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}$$ **(b) [7 Marks]** Air enters a Fanno duct at $M_1 = 2.5$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 80$ kPa. Determine: - (i) The stagnation temperature $T_0$ and stagnation pressure $P_0$. - (ii) The critical (sonic) properties $T^*$, $P^*$, $\rho^*$. - (iii) The friction parameter $4fL^*/D$ for the supersonic branch. - (iv) The entropy change per unit mass between the inlet and the choked condition. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** > **Step 1 — Energy Equation for Perfect Gas:** > $$T_0 = T_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2C_p} = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)$$ > At $M = 1$ (choked): > $$T_0 = T^*\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}\right) = T^*\cdot\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}$$ > Therefore: > $$\frac{T}{T^*} = \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}}{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}$$ > **Step 2 — Pressure Ratio (Isentropic Process):** > Using $\dfrac{P}{P_0} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$ and similarly for $P^*$: > $$\frac{P}{P^*} = \left(\frac{T}{T^*}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)} = \left[\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)}$$ > **Step 3 — Apply Continuity for Density Ratio:** > From $\rho V = \rho^* V^*$, $V/V^* = (M/M^*)\sqrt{T^*/T} = (1/M)\sqrt{T^*/T}$: > $$\frac{\rho}{\rho^*} = \frac{V^*}{V} = M\sqrt{\frac{T}{T^*}} = M\sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$ > Inverting: > $$\frac{\rho}{\rho^*} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}$$ > **Step 4 — Cross-Verification (Ideal Gas Law):** > $$\frac{P}{P^*} = \frac{\rho}{\rho^*}\cdot\frac{T}{T^*} = \left(\frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}\right)\cdot\left(\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right)$$ > $$= \frac{1}{M}\cdot\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}\cdot\sqrt{\gamma + 1} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}} \;\checkmark$$ > ∎ --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** > **Given:** $M_1 = 2.5$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 80$ kPa, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K). > **Step 1 — Stagnation Properties:** > $$T_0 = T_1\left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}\times 6.25\right) = 300 \times (1 + 1.25) = 300 \times 2.25 = 675 \text{ K}$$ > $$P_0 = P_1\left(1 + \frac{0.4}{2}\times 6.25\right)^{3.5} = 80 \times (2.25)^{3.5}$$ > Computing: $(2.25)^{3.5} = 2.25^3 \times 2.25^{0.5} = 11.39 \times 1.5 = 17.085$ > $$P_0 = 80 \times 17.085 \approx 1366.8 \text{ kPa}$$ > **Step 2 — Critical (Choked) Properties:** > $$T^* = T_0 \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} = 675 \times \frac{2}{2.4} = 562.5 \text{ K}$$ > $$P^* = P_0 \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma + 1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma - 1)} = 1366.8 \times (0.8333)^{3.5} = 1366.8 \times 0.5283 \approx 722.0 \text{ kPa}$$ > $$\rho_1 = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{80000}{287 \times 300} = 0.929 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ > $$\rho^* = \rho_0 \cdot (0.8333)^{1/(\gamma - 1)} = \frac{P_0}{R T_0} \times 0.6339 = \frac{1366800}{287 \times 675} \times 0.6339$$ > $$\rho_0 = 7.05 \text{ kg/m}^3 \Rightarrow \rho^* = 7.05 \times 0.6339 = 4.47 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ > **Step 3 — Friction Parameter at $M_1 = 2.5$:** > $$\frac{4fL^*}{D} = \frac{1 - 6.25}{1.4 \times 6.25} + \frac{2.4}{2.8}\ln\!\left[\frac{2.4 \times 6.25}{2 + 0.4 \times 6.25}\right]$$ > $$= \frac{-5.25}{8.75} + 0.8571 \times \ln\!\left[\frac{15}{4.5}\right]$$ > $$= -0.6 + 0.8571 \times \ln(3.333)$$ > $$= -0.6 + 0.8571 \times 1.2040 = -0.6 + 1.0318 \approx 0.4318$$ > **Step 4 — Entropy Change (using standard Fanno relation):** > $$\frac{s_1 - s^*}{R} = -\ln M_1^2 - \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M_1^2}{\gamma + 1}\right]$$ > $$= -\ln(6.25) - \frac{0.4}{1.4}\ln\!\left[\frac{4.5}{2.4}\right]$$ > $$= -1.8326 - 0.2857 \times \ln(1.875) = -1.8326 - 0.2857 \times 0.6286$$ > $$= -1.8326 - 0.1796 = -2.0122$$ > This is **negative**, confirming $s_1 < s^*$ for $M_1 > 1$ (supersonic branch). > **Final Answers (Q-B-b):** > - **(i)** $T_0 = 675$ K, $P_0 \approx 1366.8$ kPa > - **(ii)** $T^* = 562.5$ K, $P^* \approx 722.0$ kPa, $\rho^* \approx 4.47$ kg/m³ > - **(iii)** $4fL^*/D \approx 0.432$ > - **(iv)** $s_1 - s^* \approx -2.0122 \, R = -577.6$ J/(kg·K) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the stagnation temperature is constant** in Fanno flow (not stagnation pressure — that decreases due to friction). > 2. **Mixing up subsonic vs supersonic branches** in the friction parameter formula — both give the same $4fL^*/D$ value, but the **direction** of property change is opposite. > 3. **Confusing Fanning friction factor $f$** (used in $4fL/D$) with the **Darcy friction factor** $f_D$ (used in $f_D L/D$). Conversion: $f_D = 4f$. > 4. **Not checking for choking**: if the actual duct length exceeds $L^*$, the inlet conditions must be modified — students often assume the inlet Mach number is fixed. > 5. **Sign errors in entropy change**: $s - s^*$ is **negative** on both branches (since $M=1$ is the maximum). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📌 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Fanno flow** = adiabatic, constant area, with friction (only $df \neq 0$, all others = 0). - ✅ **$T_0$ = constant**, but $P_0$ **decreases** along the duct (friction loss). - ✅ **Three reference states**: Inlet (1), Outlet (2), Critical/Choked (*). - ✅ **Master Formula — Temperature ratio:** $\dfrac{T}{T^*} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}$ - ✅ **Master Formula — Pressure ratio:** $\dfrac{P}{P^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$ - ✅ **Master Formula — Density ratio:** $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho^*} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}}$ - ✅ **Master Formula — Velocity ratio:** $\dfrac{V}{V^*} = M\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}}$ - ✅ **Fanno Equation:** $\dfrac{4fL^*}{D} = \dfrac{1 - M^2}{\gamma M^2} + \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma + 1)M^2}{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}\right]$ - ✅ **For $\gamma = 1.4$ at $M=1$:** $T^*/T_0 = 0.8333$, $P^*/P_0 = 0.5283$, $\rho^*/\rho_0 = 0.6339$. - ✅ **Choking** occurs at $M = 1$ where entropy is **maximum** and duct length equals $L^*$. - ✅ **Subsonic Fanno flow** (M < 1): $V$ ↑, $P$ ↓, $T$ ↓, $\rho$ ↓ as $M$ → 1. - ✅ **Supersonic Fanno flow** (M > 1): $V$ ↓, $P$ ↑, $T$ ↑, $\rho$ ↑ as $M$ → 1. - ✅ **Friction factor convention:** Fanno equation uses **Fanning** factor in $4fL/D$. - ✅ **Entropy maximum principle:** Both subsonic and supersonic branches approach $s_{\max}$ at $M=1$ from below. - ✅ **Cross-check:** All property ratios = 1.0 at $M = 1$ (this is your verification step). - ✅ **Units:** $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$ for air, $C_p = 1004.5$ J/(kg·K). - ✅ **Engine use:** Jet engine tailpipes, gas pipelines, and rocket nozzles operating at constant area. > **🎯 Final Memory Mnemonic:** **"FPT" — Friction, Perfect gas, constant area, Two equations, One maximum (at M=1)"** — capture the Fanno flow essence in one breath.

topics.title: Variation of Mach number with duct length
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variation of Mach number with duct length

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Quasi-One-Dimensional Compressible Flow**, the **Variation of Mach Number with Duct Length** describes how the local flow Mach number ($M$) changes along the streamwise coordinate ($x$) of a duct whose cross-sectional area $A(x)$ varies gradually. The governing differential relationship is:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\,\frac{dV}{V}$$

This equation, derived from continuity and isentropic flow relations, links the geometric taper of the duct (rate of area change $\frac{dA}{dx}$) to the resulting acceleration ($\frac{dV}{dx}$) or deceleration of the compressible fluid along the length of the duct.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quasi-1D Assumption:** The flow properties (V, P, T, $\rho$, M) are assumed uniform across any cross-section, but allowed to vary in the streamwise (x) direction. The duct area $A$ may vary with $x$, and the sidewalls can diverge or converge at a small angle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway On-Ramp

Imagine driving on a **highway** (supersonic flow, M > 1). If the road suddenly **narrows** (converging duct), cars must **slow down and bunch together** (subsonic-like behavior, deceleration). Conversely, if the highway **widens** (diverging duct), cars **spread out and speed up** (acceleration).

Now flip the scenario: you are in a **city lane** (subsonic flow, M < 1). To merge onto a highway, the lane must **widen** so traffic can **accelerate** to highway speeds. If the city lane **narrows**, cars must **decelerate** even further.

The **Mach number** acts as the "speed limit indicator" of this flow, and the duct's cross-sectional area acts as the "road geometry." The mathematical sign of $(M^2 - 1)$ **decides** whether the flow speeds up or slows down as the area changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (KTU High-Yield):** The behavior of compressible flow **inverts** at $M = 1$. For $M < 1$ (subsonic), a converging duct accelerates the flow. For $M > 1$ (supersonic), a converging duct decelerates the flow. This single fact is the foundation of **convergent-divergent (C-D) nozzles** used in jet engines, rockets, and supersonic wind tunnels.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / SI Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------------------|
| $\gamma$ | Ratio of specific heats | **1.4** (for air) |
| $R$ | Specific gas constant for air | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| $a^*$ | Speed of sound at M = 1 (sonic condition) | $\sqrt{\gamma R T^*}$ |
| $A^*$ | Throat area (where M = 1) | Critical area reference |
| $A/A^*$ | Area ratio (dimensionless) | Drives all flow behavior |

> [!TIP]
> The throat area $A^*$ is the **reference area** for an isentropic nozzle. The expression $\frac{A}{A^*}$ is a function of $M$ alone (for a given $\gamma$) and is the cornerstone of all nozzle and diffuser design problems in the KTU syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

To understand how Mach number varies with duct length, we walk through the chain of governing equations:

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation for Quasi-1D Flow):**

For a steady flow with uniform properties across any cross-section, mass conservation requires:

$$\rho(x) \, V(x) \, A(x) = \text{constant} = \dot{m}$$

Differentiating logarithmically with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Isentropic Process Relation (Momentum / Energy):**

For an isentropic, reversible adiabatic flow, the speed of sound definition $a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s$ gives:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho} + V\,dV = 0 \quad \text{(Euler's equation along a streamline)}$$

Combining with the isentropic relation $a^2 = \frac{dP}{d\rho}$:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = a^2 \implies \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{V^2}{a^2}\left(-\frac{dV}{V}\right) = M^2\left(-\frac{dV}{V}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into Continuity:**

$$-M^2\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0$$

$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$$

This is the **master differential equation** for area-velocity coupling in quasi-1D compressible flow.

## 2.2 Why "How" the Duct Geometry Drives Mach Number

From the master equation, we can solve for the velocity gradient:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1}{M^2 - 1} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$

The Mach number change $dM$ is related to the velocity change via $M = V/a$. For a more direct duct-length analysis, we use the chain rule to express $\frac{dM}{dx}$ in terms of known area gradients.

## 2.3 The Four Canonical Duct Geometries

| Duct Type | $dA/dx$ | Flow Regime | Resulting $dM/dx$ |
|-----------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Converging** (subsonic) | $dA/dx < 0$ | $M < 1$ | $dM/dx > 0$ (accelerates) |
| **Converging** (supersonic) | $dA/dx < 0$ | $M > 1$ | $dM/dx < 0$ (decelerates) |
| **Diverging** (subsonic) | $dA/dx > 0$ | $M < 1$ | $dM/dx < 0$ (decelerates) |
| **Diverging** (supersonic) | $dA/dx > 0$ | $M > 1$ | $dM/dx > 0$ (accelerates) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **convergent-divergent (C-D) nozzle** exploits this dual behavior: the **convergent section** accelerates the flow from $M < 1$ up to $M = 1$ at the **throat**, and the **divergent section** further accelerates the flow into the **supersonic regime** ($M > 1$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho V A = \text{const}$ | Mass conservation (quasi-1D) | kg/s |
| 2 | $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$ | **Master area-velocity relation** | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1}{M^2 - 1}\frac{dA}{A}$ | Velocity response to area change | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M}\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | **Area-Mach number relation** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V} + M^2\frac{dT}{T} - 2M\frac{dM}{M}$ (Fanno) | Flow with friction correction | — |
| 6 | $\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$ | Stagnation temperature ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Isentropic pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}$ | Isentropic density ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\frac{A}{A^*} = 1$ at $M = 1$ | Throat condition (sonic) | — |
| 10 | $A \cdot P = \text{const}$ (choked flow) | Mass flow rate proxy | N |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When using the area-Mach relation (Formula 4), students often confuse the **subsonic branch** ($M < 1$, $A/A^* > 1$) with the **supersonic branch** ($M > 1$, $A/A^* > 1$). The same area ratio can correspond to **two different Mach numbers**. Always verify by checking $P/P_0$ or $T/T_0$ against the problem's back-pressure conditions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Jet Engine & Rocket Nozzles:** C-D nozzles accelerate exhaust gases to supersonic speeds to generate thrust. The variation of M with duct length directly determines the **exit Mach number** and **thrust coefficient**.
2. **Supersonic Wind Tunnels:** The test section of a supersonic tunnel is the diverging section of a C-D nozzle, and the Mach number uniformity depends on the precise area-gradient profile.
3. **Steam Turbines & Gas Turbines:** Blade passages act as converging/diverging ducts. Mach number variation along the blade length affects efficiency and shock formation.
4. **Scramjet Inlets:** The inlet duct is shaped to compress the flow gradually, decelerating it from supersonic flight speed to subsonic combustor conditions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Area-Mach Number Relation (The Crown Jewel)

This is the derivation KTU examiners most frequently test. We begin with the master equation and integrate using isentropic flow relations.

**Starting Point:** From the master relation derived in Section 2:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step A:** Isolate $dV/V$:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1}{M^2 - 1}\frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step B:** Use the speed of sound definition $a^2 = \gamma R T$, and the perfect gas isentropic relation $\frac{P}{\rho^{\gamma}} = \text{const}$. Apply logarithmic differentiation:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \gamma \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step C:** The energy equation for isentropic flow gives:

$$c_p dT_0 = 0 \implies dT_0 = 0 \implies \frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2 = \text{const}$$

Differentiating the energy relation:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = -(\gamma-1)M^2 \frac{dM}{M} \cdot \frac{1}{1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

A more direct path uses $M = V/a$ and $a^2 = \gamma R T$, so:

$$\frac{dM}{M} = \frac{dV}{V} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{dT}{T}$$

**Step D:** From the perfect gas law and isentropic process:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \frac{dP}{P} - \frac{dT}{T} = -\frac{V\,dV}{RT} - \frac{dT}{T} = -\frac{\gamma-1}{2}\frac{dT}{T} \cdot \frac{2}{\gamma-1} \cdot (\text{mach terms})$$

A cleaner path: use the isentropic relation $T/T_0$ to get density ratio, then integrate.

**Step E (Direct Integration):** Start from:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dV}{V}$$

with $\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -M^2 \frac{dV}{V}$ from continuity-isentropy coupling.

$$\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{dV}{V}(1 - M^2)$$

This is a restatement. Now substitute $M^2 = \frac{V^2}{\gamma R T}$ and use $T_0 = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)$.

The standard integration (omitted in many textbooks but expected at KTU) proceeds by writing everything in terms of $M$:

**Step F:** After substituting the differential relations and integrating from $M$ to $M = 1$ (where $A = A^*$), the closed-form result is:

$$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M} \left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation in Module 2** for KTU exams. Memorize it, understand its dual-valued nature, and practice using gas tables / software to invert it.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mass Flow Rate Through a Nozzle

The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho V A$$

Using $\rho = \frac{P}{RT}$ and $V = M a = M \sqrt{\gamma R T}$:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{PA}{RT} \cdot M \sqrt{\gamma R T} = \frac{PM A}{\sqrt{RT}} \sqrt{\gamma}$$

Substituting $P = P_0 \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ and $T = T_0 \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{-1}$:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_0 A}{\sqrt{RT_0}} \cdot \sqrt{\gamma} \cdot M \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{-\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

**Step G:** Differentiate $\dot{m}$ with respect to $M$ and set $d\dot{m}/dM = 0$ to find the condition for **maximum mass flow rate** (choking condition):

$$\frac{d\dot{m}}{dM} = 0 \implies M = 1$$

The corresponding maximum (choked) mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m}_{\max} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

This is the famous **Saint-Venant–Wantzel equation** for choked nozzle flow.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Equations and Their Physical Meaning

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Conditions |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|------------|
| Continuity (log form) | $\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0$ | Mass is conserved in a varying duct | Steady, 1D |
| Master relation | $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2-1)\frac{dV}{V}$ | Couples geometry to flow speed | Quasi-1D, isentropic |
| Area-Mach | $\frac{A}{A^*} = f(M, \gamma)$ | Links local area to local M | Isentropic, perfect gas |
| Energy | $\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$ | Total temperature is constant | Adiabatic, no work |
| Mass flow | $\dot{m} = \rho V A$ | Throat flow rate | Any compressible flow |
| Choked flow | $M = 1$ at minimum area | Maximum $\dot{m}$ condition | $P_b/P_0 \leq P^*/P_0$ |
| Pressure ratio at choke | $\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Critical back-pressure | For air: $P^*/P_0 = 0.5283$ |

## 3.4 Sample Calculation Setup (Numerical Template)

**Problem Setup (typical KTU Part B style):**

A convergent nozzle has an inlet area $A_1 = 0.05\,\text{m}^2$ with $P_0 = 600\,\text{kPa}$, $T_0 = 500\,\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$. At the exit plane, $M_2 = 0.85$. Find:
- (i) Exit area $A_2$
- (ii) Exit static pressure $P_2$
- (iii) Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$

**Solution Template:**

**Step (i) Compute $A/A^*$ at $M_2$:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A^*} = \frac{1}{0.85}\left[\frac{2}{2.4}\left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.85^2\right)\right]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.85}\left[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.1445)\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{0.85}\left[0.8333 \times 1.1445\right]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.85}\left[0.9538\right]^{3} = \frac{1}{0.85} \times 0.8678 = 1.0209$$

**Step (ii) Find $A^*$:** Since the inlet has $M_1$ (low), but for convergent nozzle $A^* = A_2 / (A_2/A^*)$. Use mass flow rate to find $A^*$:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

Solving for $A^*$:

$$A^* = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{T_0}}{P_0} \cdot \left[\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}\right]^{-1/2}$$

**Step (iii) Mass flow rate:** Computed from the choked flow equation once the throat condition is established.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If the problem gives $P_0$, $T_0$, and exit pressure $P_e$, and asks for $M_e$, you do **not** need the area-Mach relation at all. Just compute $P_e/P_0$ and use isentropic tables: $P_0/P = (1 + 0.2M^2)^{3.5}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define the term *choking* in compressible flow. What is the condition under which choking occurs in a convergent nozzle?

**Model Answer:**

Choking is the condition in compressible flow where the **mass flow rate through a duct reaches a maximum** and cannot be increased further by lowering the downstream (back) pressure. Once choked, the flow at the **minimum area (throat)** becomes sonic ($M = 1$), and any further reduction in back pressure has no effect on the mass flow rate or on the conditions upstream of the throat.

**Choking condition:** For a convergent nozzle with isentropic flow, choking occurs when the back-pressure ratio falls to or below the critical value:

$$\frac{P_b}{P_0} \leq \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$), this critical ratio is **0.5283**. At this point, the throat Mach number equals **1**, and the mass flow rate is given by the Saint-Venant–Wantzel equation.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** State the area-Mach number relation for isentropic flow of a perfect gas through a variable area duct. Why does the same area ratio correspond to two different Mach numbers?

**Model Answer:**

The area-Mach number relation for isentropic flow of a perfect gas is:

$$\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M} \left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

where $A^*$ is the throat area (where $M = 1$).

**Two Mach numbers for one area ratio:** From the governing equation $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$, when $M < 1$ the flow accelerates in a converging duct, and when $M > 1$ the flow accelerates in a diverging duct. The mathematical function $A/A^*(M)$ has a **minimum at $M = 1$** (where $A/A^* = 1$) and rises symmetrically on both the subsonic and supersonic sides. Therefore, for any $A/A^* > 1$, there are **two valid solutions**: a subsonic $M$ and a supersonic $M$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**(a)** Derive the area-Mach number relation for one-dimensional isentropic flow of a perfect gas through a variable area duct, starting from the continuity equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A convergent-divergent nozzle has an inlet (reservoir) stagnation pressure $P_0 = 800\,\text{kPa}$ and stagnation temperature $T_0 = 600\,\text{K}$. The throat area is $A_t = 0.02\,\text{m}^2$ and the exit area is $A_e = 0.035\,\text{m}^2$. For air, take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$. The nozzle operates in **designed supersonic condition** with isentropic flow throughout. Determine: **(7 marks)**

   (i) The exit Mach number $M_e$

   (ii) The exit static pressure $P_e$ and static temperature $T_e$

   (iii) The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1: Continuity Equation (Quasi-1D)**

$$\rho V A = \text{constant} = \dot{m}$$

Differentiating logarithmically:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2: Euler's Equation Along a Streamline**

For steady, inviscid, adiabatic flow:

$$V\,dV + \frac{dP}{\rho} = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3: Isentropic Sound Speed Definition**

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = \frac{dP}{d\rho} \implies \frac{dP}{d\rho} = a^2 \quad \text{...(3)}$$

From (2) and (3):

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\frac{V\,dV}{a^2} = -M^2 \frac{dV}{V} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4: Substituting (4) into (1)**

$$-M^2 \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dV}{V} + \frac{dA}{A} = 0$$

$$\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V} \quad \text{...(Master equation)}$$

**Step 5: Expressing in Mach Number Form**

Using $M^2 = \frac{V^2}{\gamma R T}$ and $T_0 = T\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right) = \text{constant}$:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{dM}{M} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{dM}{M} - \frac{(\gamma-1)M^2\,dM/M}{2 + (\gamma-1)M^2}$$

After algebraic manipulation and integrating from $M$ to $M = 1$ (where $A = A^*$):

$$\ln\frac{A}{A^*} = -\ln M + \frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} \ln\left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M} \left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $P_0 = 800\,\text{kPa}$, $T_0 = 600\,\text{K}$, $A_t = A^* = 0.02\,\text{m}^2$, $A_e = 0.035\,\text{m}^2$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$.

**(i) Exit Mach Number:**

Area ratio at exit:

$$\frac{A_e}{A^*} = \frac{0.035}{0.02} = 1.75$$

For isentropic flow with designed supersonic exit, we use the **supersonic branch** of the area-Mach relation:

$$1.75 = \frac{1}{M_e} \left[\frac{2}{2.4}\left(1 + 0.2 M_e^2\right)\right]^{3}$$

This requires iterative solution. Trial $M_e = 2.20$:

$$= \frac{1}{2.20}[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 4.84)]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.20}[0.8333 \times 1.968]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2.20}[1.640]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.20} \times 4.412 = 2.005$$

Trial $M_e = 2.10$:

$$= \frac{1}{2.10}[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 4.41)]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.10}[0.8333 \times 1.882]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2.10}[1.568]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.10} \times 3.857 = 1.837$$

Trial $M_e = 2.07$:

$$= \frac{1}{2.07}[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 4.285)]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.07}[0.8333 \times 1.857]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2.07}[1.548]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.07} \times 3.708 = 1.792$$

Trial $M_e = 2.06$:

$$= \frac{1}{2.06}[0.8333 \times (1 + 0.2 \times 4.244)]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.06}[0.8333 \times 1.849]^{3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2.06}[1.541]^{3} = \frac{1}{2.06} \times 3.659 = 1.776$$

Converging to $\boxed{M_e \approx 2.05}$

**(ii) Exit Static Pressure and Temperature:**

$$T_e = \frac{T_0}{1 + 0.2 M_e^2} = \frac{600}{1 + 0.2 \times 4.20} = \frac{600}{1.84} = 326.1\,\text{K}$$

$$P_e = \frac{P_0}{(1 + 0.2 M_e^2)^{3.5}} = \frac{800}{(1.84)^{3.5}} = \frac{800}{8.34} = 95.9\,\text{kPa}$$

**(iii) Mass Flow Rate (using choked flow at throat):**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}} \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$$

Computing the constant term:

$$\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}} = \frac{1.4}{287}\left(\frac{2}{2.4}\right)^6 = 0.004878 \times 0.08779 = 0.0004283$$

$$\sqrt{0.0004283} = 0.02070$$

Now:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{800{,}000 \times 0.02}{\sqrt{600}} \times 0.02070 = \frac{16{,}000}{24.495} \times 0.02070$$

$$= 653.20 \times 0.02070 = 13.52\,\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 13.52\,\text{kg/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 — Alternative]

**(a)** Starting from the basic conservation laws, derive the **critical pressure ratio** $P^*/P_0$ at which sonic conditions ($M = 1$) are attained at the throat of a convergent nozzle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$) flows isentropically through a convergent nozzle of exit area $0.04\,\text{m}^2$. The reservoir conditions are $P_0 = 500\,\text{kPa}$ and $T_0 = 400\,\text{K}$. If the back pressure is $P_b = 200\,\text{kPa}$, determine: **(7 marks)**

   (i) Whether the flow is choked

   (ii) The exit Mach number $M_e$

   (iii) The exit velocity $V_e$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Derivation of Critical Pressure Ratio

**Step 1:** At the throat (minimum area) of a convergent nozzle, the maximum velocity is reached. From $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$, for $\frac{dA}{dx} < 0$ (converging) and finite $\frac{dV}{dx}$, the only way the equation is satisfied is when $M = 1$ (sonic condition).

**Step 2:** At $M = 1$, the isentropic relations give:

$$\frac{T^*}{T_0} = \frac{2}{\gamma+1} = \frac{2}{2.4} = 0.8333 \quad \text{(for air)}$$

**Step 3:** Using the isentropic pressure relation:

$$\frac{P_0}{P} = \left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

At $M = 1$:

$$\frac{P_0}{P^*} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} = \left(0.8333\right)^{3.5} = 0.5283$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{P^*}{P_0} = 0.5283 \text{ (for air)}}$$

In general:

$$\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $A_e = 0.04\,\text{m}^2$, $P_0 = 500\,\text{kPa}$, $T_0 = 400\,\text{K}$, $P_b = 200\,\text{kPa}$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**(i) Check for Choking:**

$$\frac{P_b}{P_0} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.40$$

Compare with critical ratio for air: $P^*/P_0 = 0.5283$.

Since $0.40 < 0.5283$, the back pressure is **below** the critical value, so the flow is **choked** at the exit plane.

**(ii) Exit Mach Number:**

Since this is a **convergent nozzle only** (no divergent section), the maximum Mach number attainable at the exit is $M = 1$. So:

$$\boxed{M_e = 1.0 \text{ (sonic at exit, flow is choked)}}$$

**(iii) Exit Velocity:**

$$T_e = T^* = \frac{2}{\gamma+1} T_0 = 0.8333 \times 400 = 333.33\,\text{K}$$

$$a_e = a^* = \sqrt{\gamma R T^*} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 333.33} = \sqrt{133{,}866} = 365.9\,\text{m/s}$$

$$V_e = M_e \cdot a_e = 1.0 \times 365.9 = 365.9\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_e \approx 365.9\,\text{m/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Branch Selection Error:** In a C-D nozzle problem, students often pick the **subsonic root** of the area-Mach relation even when the problem specifies "designed supersonic exit." Always check whether the design intent is subsonic or supersonic.
> 2. **Forgetting the Stagnation Condition:** Some students try to compute $T_e$ or $P_e$ using $M_e$ alone, forgetting that $T_0$ and $P_0$ must be the **stagnation** values, not the inlet static values (unless the inlet is itself at rest).
> 3. **Mass Flow Rate Units:** The Saint-Venant–Wantzel equation requires $P_0$ in **Pa** (not kPa) and $T_0$ in **K**. A common error is mixing units and getting mass flow rates off by factors of $\sqrt{1000}$ or $1000$.
> 4. **Choking Misconception:** In a **convergent-only** nozzle, the maximum exit Mach number is **1** (sonic at the exit plane). In a **convergent-divergent** nozzle, the throat can be sonic and the exit can be **supersonic**.
> 5. **Off-Design Conditions:** When the back pressure is between $P_e$ (subsonic design) and $P^*$ (choking), expansion waves or shocks may occur in the divergent section. KTU problems often test this nuance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### Core Equations to Memorize

- **Master Relation:** $\frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$
- **Area-Mach Number:** $\frac{A}{A^*} = \frac{1}{M} \left[\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2\right)\right]^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$
- **Critical Pressure Ratio (air):** $\frac{P^*}{P_0} = 0.5283$
- **Isentropic Pressure:** $\frac{P_0}{P} = (1 + 0.2 M^2)^{3.5}$
- **Isentropic Temperature:** $\frac{T_0}{T} = 1 + 0.2 M^2$
- **Isentropic Density:** $\frac{\rho_0}{\rho} = (1 + 0.2 M^2)^{2.5}$
- **Choked Mass Flow:** $\dot{m} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{T_0}}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R}\left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$

### Physical Insights to Remember

- The area-Mach relation has a **minimum at $M = 1$** where $A/A^* = 1$
- The same $A/A^* > 1$ corresponds to **two Mach numbers** (subsonic and supersonic)
- **Converging duct** accelerates subsonic flow, decelerates supersonic flow
- **Diverging duct** decelerates subsonic flow, accelerates supersonic flow
- **C-D nozzle:** Convergent part accelerates flow to $M = 1$ at throat, divergent part accelerates further to supersonic
- **Choking** occurs when $P_b/P_0 \leq 0.5283$ (for air)
- In a **convergent-only** nozzle, max exit $M$ is 1

### Common KTU Question Types

1. **Derivation of area-Mach relation** (7 marks)
2. **Find exit Mach from given area ratio** (using isentropic tables or iteration)
3. **Determine if flow is choked** (compare $P_b/P_0$ with 0.5283)
4. **Compute mass flow rate** (use choked flow equation)
5. **Find exit pressure, temperature, velocity** (use isentropic relations)

### Numerical Values to Remember (for air, $\gamma = 1.4$)

- $\frac{\gamma-1}{2} = 0.2$
- $\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)} = 3$
- $\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} = 3.5$
- $\frac{1}{\gamma-1} = 2.5$
- $\frac{2}{\gamma+1} = 0.8333$
- $P^*/P_0 = 0.5283$
- $T^*/T_0 = 0.8333$
- $\rho^*/\rho_0 = 0.6339$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always start by listing the **given quantities**, then identify **which branch** of the area-Mach relation to use (subsonic or supersonic), and finally compute step-by-step. KTU examiners reward **clear methodology** and **correct unit usage** as much as the final numerical answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choking due to friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Choking due to friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Choking due to friction** is the phenomenon in compressible gas dynamics where the mass flow rate through a **constant-area duct** (pipe or nozzle-like passage) reaches a **maximum, finite, limiting value** when the effect of wall friction is taken into account. Mathematically, choking occurs at the duct exit when the local Mach number reaches **unity ($M = 1$)**, and any further attempt to increase the mass flow rate by increasing the upstream-to-downstream pressure ratio becomes physically impossible.

The governing flow model is known as **Fanno flow** — a steady, one-dimensional, adiabatic flow of a perfect gas through a constant cross-sectional area duct with wall friction (no heat transfer, no shaft work). The state of the gas at any section of such a duct lies on a curve called the **Fanno line** on the enthalpy–entropy ($h$–$s$) diagram, and the **choked (sonic) point** corresponds to the apex (maximum entropy point) of this Fanno line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between three types of choking covered in Compressible Fluid Flow:
> 1. **Choking in a Convergent Nozzle** (geometric choking, Module 1)
> 2. **Choking due to Friction** (Fanno flow choking, Module 2) ← *Current Topic*
> 3. **Choking due to Heat Addition** (Rayleigh flow choking, Module 2/3)
> All three yield $M = 1$ at the critical section but the physics and the controlling parameter differ.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a long, narrow garden hose connected to a pressurized air compressor, with a partially opened valve at the far end.

- **Without friction (frictionless duct):** The air accelerates smoothly. No matter how long the hose is, the exit velocity continues to grow as you increase the upstream pressure.
- **With friction (real hose):** Friction between the air and the hose wall *robs* kinetic energy from the moving gas. As the gas accelerates down the hose, friction *also* increases (since wall shear stress $\tau_w \propto V^2$). At some point, the **rate of kinetic energy gain from expansion exactly equals the rate of energy loss to friction** — this is the **choked condition**.

Once choking sets in, the gas at the exit is traveling at exactly the **local speed of sound** ($M = 1$). A pressure signal from the downstream side can no longer travel upstream to "inform" the source about further pressure changes, so the mass flow rate is "locked" at its maximum value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Specific heat ratio: $\gamma = 1.4$ for air (diatomic)
> - Gas constant for air: $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
> - Darcy–Weisbach friction factor: $4f$ (the factor of 4 comes from the Fanning $f$ multiplied by 4, or equivalently, the Darcy friction factor)
> - Hydraulic diameter: $D_h = 4A/P$

## 1.3 Why Choking Matters in Engineering

In real engineering systems, **no duct is truly frictionless**. Choking due to friction controls the maximum deliverable mass flow in:

- Natural gas pipelines
- Compressed air distribution networks
- High-altitude pneumatic systems
- Wind tunnels with long settling chambers
- Rocket engine feed lines
- High-pressure steam injection lines

A KTU student must be able to predict **at what duct length** choking occurs for a given inlet Mach number $M_1$, or conversely, **what is the maximum allowable pipe length** before choking restricts the system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of Classical Fanno Flow

To develop a tractable analytical model, the following idealized assumptions are made:

1. **Steady flow** ($\partial/\partial t = 0$)
2. **One-dimensional flow** (properties uniform over the cross-section)
3. **Adiabatic walls** ($dQ = 0$ — no heat transfer with surroundings)
4. **Constant duct area** ($dA = 0$)
5. **Wall friction present** ($f > 0$, i.e., $dP \neq 0$ even though $dA = 0$)
6. **Perfect gas** ($P = \rho R T$, constant specific heats)
7. **Negligible body forces** (no gravitational effects)

## 2.2 Governing Equations

From the **continuity equation** (constant area, steady):

$$\rho V = G = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating: $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dV}{V} = 0$

From the **steady flow energy equation** (adiabatic, no work):

$$h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} = h_0 = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating and using $dh = c_p \, dT$: $c_p \, dT + V \, dV = 0$

From the **momentum equation** (with friction):

$$dP + \rho V \, dV + \dfrac{4f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2} dx = 0$$

## 2.3 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Choking

The three governing equations above form a closed system. The friction term $\dfrac{4f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2} dx$ is **always negative** (it always resists motion). The momentum equation dictates that for the pressure $P$ to drop, the velocity $V$ must rise (to gain kinetic energy). 

**The Catch:** As $V$ rises, two things happen simultaneously:
- Friction loss $\propto V^2$ **grows quadratically**
- The compressibility effect (Mach number rise) makes the gas "stiffer" — additional pressure drop is needed to extract the same incremental velocity

The combination of these two effects means there is a **finite duct length** beyond which the gas cannot accelerate any further — it reaches the **maximum possible entropy point** at $M = 1$. This is the **choked condition**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Second Law Connection:**
> The Fanno line on an $h$–$s$ diagram is an inverted parabola-like curve. Entropy is **maximum** at $M = 1$. Any further friction work (irreversibility) without further pressure drop actually *decreases* entropy on the Fanno line — a physical impossibility in adiabatic flow. Hence $M = 1$ is the upper limit.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition at $M = 1$ |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Fanno Friction Parameter (max) | $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1-M^{2}}{\gamma M^{2}} + \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)M^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}\right]$ | $\dfrac{1-M^{2}}{\gamma M^{2}} = 0$ at $M=1$ |
| 2 | Static Pressure Ratio | $\dfrac{P}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}$ | $= 1$ |
| 3 | Static Temperature Ratio | $\dfrac{T}{T^{*}} = \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}$ | $= 1$ |
| 4 | Static Density Ratio | $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}}$ | $= 1$ |
| 5 | Velocity Ratio | $\dfrac{V}{V^{*}} = M\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}$ | $= 1$ |
| 6 | Stagnation Pressure Ratio | $\dfrac{P_0}{P_0^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\!\left[\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}\right]^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}}$ | $= 1$ |
| 7 | Stagnation Temperature Ratio | $\dfrac{T_0}{T_0^{*}} = 1$ | $= 1$ (adiabatic — always true) |
| 8 | Entropy Change | $s^{*} - s = -R\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}\right]$ | $= 0$ |
| 9 | Continuity Form | $G = \rho V = \rho^{*} V^{*} = \rho^{*} a^{*}$ | — |
| 10 | Speed of Sound at $M=1$ | $V^{*} = a^{*} = \sqrt{\gamma R T^{*}}$ | $V = a$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The asterisk $^{*}$ denotes properties at the **choked (sonic) section** where $M = 1$. Any section where $M < 1$ is **subsonic** (on the lower branch of the Fanno line), and $M > 1$ is **supersonic** (upper branch). Note that in a Fanno duct with friction, the flow always **moves toward $M = 1$** — subsonic flow accelerates, supersonic flow decelerates.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of long pneumatic pipelines:** Used to size the maximum allowable length before choking restricts flow.
- **Rocket engine injector feed lines:** Critical to ensure the lines do not choke prematurely and starve the combustion chamber.
- **Natural gas trunk lines:** Engineers use the Fanno parameter to size compressor station spacing.
- **Vacuum systems & high-altitude wind tunnels:** Predict sonic choking location in test section supply lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fanno Flow Equation (4fL/D parameter)

This is the most critical derivation for KTU Module 2. We combine the three governing equations.

### Step 1: Energy equation in terms of Mach number

From $c_p \, dT + V \, dV = 0$, divide both sides by $c_p T$:

$$\frac{dT}{T} + \frac{V \, dV}{c_p T} = 0$$

Using $a^2 = \gamma R T$ and $V^2 = M^2 a^2 = M^2 \gamma R T$:

$$\frac{dT}{T} + \frac{\gamma R T \cdot M^{2} \, d(M^{2})^{1/2}}{c_p T} = 0$$

After simplification (and noting $c_p = \gamma R/(\gamma-1)$):

$$\frac{dT}{T} = -\frac{\gamma-1}{2} d(M^{2})$$

### Step 2: Isentropic relations between differentials

For a perfect gas, the isentropic relation is $P/\rho^\gamma = \text{const}$ (locally, between closely-spaced sections). Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \gamma \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

### Step 3: Combine with continuity

From continuity, $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\dfrac{dV}{V}$. So:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\gamma \frac{dV}{V}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $V = Ma$ and express in $M$

Since $a^2 = \gamma R T$, we have $V^2 = M^2 \gamma R T$. Taking logarithmic differential:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d(M^{2})}{M^{2}} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{dT}{T}$$

Substituting the energy equation $\dfrac{dT}{T} = -\dfrac{\gamma-1}{2} d(M^{2})$:

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{d(M^{2})}{2M^{2}} - \frac{\gamma-1}{4} d(M^{2}) = \frac{1}{2M^{2}}\!\left[1 + \tfrac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\right] d(M^{2})$$

### Step 5: Final assembly into the momentum equation

The momentum equation: $dP + \rho V \, dV + \dfrac{4f}{D}\dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}dx = 0$

Dividing by $P$ and using $P = \rho R T$, $\rho V^2 = \gamma P M^2$:

$$\frac{dP}{P} + \frac{\rho V \, dV}{P} + \frac{4f}{D}\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2P}dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dP}{P} + \gamma M^{2}\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{2f \gamma M^{2}}{D}dx = 0$$

Substituting $\dfrac{dP}{P} = -\gamma \dfrac{dV}{V}$:

$$-\gamma \frac{dV}{V} + \gamma M^{2}\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{2f \gamma M^{2}}{D}dx = 0$$

$$\gamma(M^{2} - 1)\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{2f \gamma M^{2}}{D}dx$$

$$\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{2f M^{2}}{(M^{2} - 1)D}dx$$

### Step 6: Integrate from inlet (1) to choking point (*)

$$\int_{V_1}^{V^{*}}\frac{dV}{V} = -\int_{0}^{L^{*}}\frac{2f M^{2}}{(M^{2} - 1)D}dx$$

> [!NOTE]
> This integral is non-trivial because $M$ is a function of $V$ and $x$. The detailed integration (using the relations between $M$ and $V$ established in Step 4) yields the celebrated Fanno result shown below.

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1-M_{1}^{2}}{\gamma M_{1}^{2}} + \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)M_{1}^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M_{1}^{2}}\right]\;}$$

This equation gives the **maximum length of duct** (from inlet at $M_1$ to exit at $M^* = 1$) before choking occurs. The same formula is used to find the length between any two Mach numbers $M_1$ and $M_2$ by taking the **difference** of the parameter evaluated at each Mach number.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pressure, Temperature & Density Ratios

Starting from the integrated momentum and energy equations, and using the isentropic relations:

### Temperature ratio derivation

From $c_p dT + V dV = 0$ integrated, with the speed of sound relation $a^{*2} = \gamma R T^{*}$, and noting $T_0 = T + V^2/(2c_p) = T^{*}$ at the sonic point (since $V^{*} = a^{*}$ and $T_0 = T + V^2/(2c_p) = T^{*} + a^{*2}/(2c_p) = T^{*}[1 + (\gamma-1)/2]$ — but $T_0 = $ const along the duct):

Using the isentropic relation $T_0/T = 1 + [(\gamma-1)/2]M^{2}$:

$$\frac{T}{T^{*}} = \frac{T_0/T^{*}}{T_0/T} = \frac{[1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}]}{1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}} = \frac{(\gamma+1)/2}{1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{T}{T^{*}} = \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}\;}$$

### Pressure ratio derivation

Using the isentropic pressure relation: $P/P_0 = (T/T_0)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$

$$\frac{P}{P^{*}} = \left(\frac{T}{T^{*}}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \left(\frac{T^{*}}{T_0}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} \cdot \frac{1}{M}\cdot\ldots$$

Combining with the continuity equation and after substantial algebra:

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}\;}$$

### Density ratio derivation

From ideal gas law: $\rho/\rho^{*} = (P/P^{*})(T^{*}/T)$

$$\dfrac{\rho}{\rho^{*}} = \left(\dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}\right)\left(\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\rho}{\rho^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma+1}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}}\;}$$

### Velocity ratio derivation
