From $\rho V = \rho^{*} V^{*}$: $\;V/V^{*} = \rho^{*}/\rho$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{V}{V^{*}} = M\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Entropy Change

For adiabatic flow with friction, the entropy generation per unit mass is:

$$s^{*} - s = c_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T^{*}}{T}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P^{*}}{P}\right)$$

Substituting the ratios and simplifying:

$$s^{*} - s = -R\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1}{M}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}{\gamma+1}}\right] \;\geq\; 0$$

This is consistent with the Second Law: the flow always moves *toward* higher entropy, reaching maximum at $M = 1$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Air ($\gamma = 1.4$) enters a constant-area duct of diameter $D = 0.05$ m at $M_1 = 0.5$, $P_1 = 500$ kPa, $T_1 = 400$ K. The friction factor is $4f = 0.02$. Determine:
- (a) The maximum duct length before choking
- (b) The exit (choked) values of $P^{*}$, $T^{*}$, $\rho^{*}$, $V^{*}$
- (c) The mass flow rate at the choked condition

### Part (a): Maximum length

$$\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1-0.25}{1.4 \times 0.25} + \dfrac{2.4}{2.8}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2.4 \times 0.25}{2 + 0.4 \times 0.25}\right]$$

$$= \dfrac{0.75}{0.35} + 0.8571 \ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.6}{2.1}\right]$$

$$= 2.1429 + 0.8571 \times \ln(0.2857)$$

$$= 2.1429 + 0.8571 \times (-1.2528)$$

$$= 2.1429 - 1.0738 = 1.0691$$

$$L^{*} = \dfrac{1.0691 \times 0.05}{0.02} = 2.673 \text{ m}$$

### Part (b): Choked state properties

$$\dfrac{P}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{0.5}\sqrt{\dfrac{2+0.4(0.25)}{2.4}} = 2 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2.1}{2.4}} = 2 \times 0.9354 = 1.8708$$

$$P^{*} = \dfrac{500}{1.8708} = 267.3 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\dfrac{T}{T^{*}} = \dfrac{2.4}{2+0.4(0.25)} = \dfrac{2.4}{2.1} = 1.1429$$

$$T^{*} = \dfrac{400}{1.1429} = 350.0 \text{ K}$$

$$\rho^{*} = \dfrac{P^{*}}{RT^{*}} = \dfrac{267300}{287 \times 350} = 2.661 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

$$V^{*} = a^{*} = \sqrt{\gamma R T^{*}} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 350} = 375.1 \text{ m/s}$$

### Part (c): Mass flow rate

Inlet density: $\rho_1 = P_1/(RT_1) = 500000/(287 \times 400) = 4.355$ kg/m³
Inlet velocity: $V_1 = M_1 a_1 = 0.5 \times \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 400} = 0.5 \times 400.93 = 200.5$ m/s

$$G = \rho_1 V_1 = 4.355 \times 200.5 = 873.0 \text{ kg/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\dot{m} = G \cdot A = 873.0 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^{2} = 873.0 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 1.714 \text{ kg/s}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fanno Line on h–s Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The Fanno line is the locus of all thermodynamic states reachable by adiabatic, constant-area, frictional flow of a perfect gas, starting from a given inlet state. Below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the key interactions of Fanno flow parameters, rendered in Mermaid (a physical 2D plot is replaced by an architectural map to comply with the safe-rendering rule).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Inlet State Section 1: M1, P1, T1, rho1, V1"] --> nodeB["Friction Acts on Wall Shear tau_w"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Gas State Evolves Along Fanno Line"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Local Mach Number M"}
    nodeD -- "M less than 1 subsonic" --> nodeE["Subsonic Branch: V increases, P decreases, s increases"]
    nodeD -- "M equals 1 SSONIC CHOKING" --> nodeF["Apex of Fanno Line: max entropy, max mass flow"]
    nodeD -- "M greater than 1 supersonic" --> nodeG["Supersonic Branch: V decreases, P increases, s increases"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Locked Maximum Mass Flow Rate Gmax"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Downstream Pressure Changes Cannot Propagate Upstream"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["System Behaviour: Downstream of choke is decoupled from upstream"]

    style nodeA fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#5a3d1e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#3d1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#5a5a1e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#1e5a3d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#a02020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#1e5a3d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#a02020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeI fill:#5a1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeJ fill:#202060,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Schematic of a Constant-Area Duct with Friction

The following Mermaid block represents the physical layout of a Fanno duct and the evolution of Mach number along its length, replacing what would be a 2D engineering drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Inlet 1: M1, P1, T1"] --> nodeDuctA["Constant Area Duct: friction factor f, diameter D"]
    nodeDuctA -- "Section x: Mx less than 1" --> nodeMid["Intermediate Section: M, P, T, rho, V evolving"]
    nodeMid -- "Section 2: M2 less than 1, accelerating" --> nodeExit["Exit: M exits subsonic"]
    nodeMid -- "Critical Length Lstar reached" --> nodeSonic["Exit becomes SONIC: M equals 1, CHOKING"]
    nodeSonic --> nodeLocked["Mass flow rate G is now maximum and locked"]
    nodeLocked --> nodeDown["Downstream pressure cannot affect upstream"]

    style nodeIn fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeDuctA fill:#5a4a1e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeMid fill:#1e5a4a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeExit fill:#1e5a3d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeSonic fill:#a02020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeLocked fill:#a02020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeDown fill:#202060,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Identifying the Flow Regime

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["Given: Inlet M1, Pipe length L, D, f"] --> nodeCompute["Compute Fanno Parameter 4fL/D for the given pipe"]
    nodeCompute --> nodeCompare{"Compare 4fL/D with 4fLstar/D evaluated at M1"}
    nodeCompare -- "4fL/D less than 4fLstar/D" --> nodeSub["Flow remains subsonic, exit M2 less than 1, find M2 from Fanno tables"]
    nodeCompare -- "4fL/D equals 4fLstar/D" --> nodeSonic["Exit is exactly sonic M2 equals 1, choking condition"]
    nodeCompare -- "4fL/D greater than 4fLstar/D" --> nodeImpossible["MASS FLOW RATE MUST READJUST, inlet M1 cannot be sustained at given L"]
    nodeImpossible --> nodeResolve["Solve backwards: find the inlet M1 such that choking occurs at length L"]

    style nodeStart fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeCompute fill:#5a4a1e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeCompare fill:#5a5a1e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeSub fill:#1e5a3d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeSonic fill:#a02020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeImpossible fill:#602020,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeResolve fill:#202060,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** A **red node** denotes the choking condition ($M=1$). A **blue node** denotes an inlet or boundary condition. A **green node** denotes a subsonic or feasible operating state. A **purple node** denotes the post-choke information isolation. The arrows represent the direction of gas flow and the causal sequence of physical events.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define choking in a compressible flow with friction. Why does the flow reach a maximum Mach number of unity?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Choking due to friction is the condition in adiabatic, constant-area compressible flow where the local Mach number reaches unity ($M = 1$) at the duct exit, beyond which the mass flow rate cannot increase regardless of any further increase in the upstream-to-downstream pressure ratio.
- **(1 mark)** As the gas accelerates down the duct, friction continuously dissipates mechanical energy. The momentum equation shows that the rate of frictional momentum loss $\propto V^2$ grows quadratically with velocity.
- **(1 mark)** Beyond a certain duct length, the kinetic energy gain from expansion can no longer compensate the frictional energy loss, the gas reaches its maximum entropy state on the Fanno line, and the local Mach number asymptotes to unity. Any further friction is thermodynamically inconsistent with the adiabatic constraint.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State the assumptions made in the analysis of Fanno flow."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(1 mark)** Steady, one-dimensional flow with constant cross-sectional area ($dA = 0$).
2. **(1 mark)** Adiabatic process with no shaft work — only friction acts on the gas (no heat transfer, $dQ = 0$).
3. **(1 mark)** Perfect gas behaviour with constant specific heats; body forces (gravity) are negligible; friction factor $f$ is assumed constant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Derive the expression for the maximum length of a constant-area duct with friction. An adiabatic constant-area duct of diameter $D = 0.10$ m carries air ($\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/kg·K) with inlet conditions $M_1 = 0.3$, $P_1 = 800$ kPa, $T_1 = 500$ K. The friction factor is $4f = 0.024$. Determine:**
- **(a)** The maximum length of the duct for which the flow does not choke.
- **(b)** The exit pressure, temperature, density, and velocity at the choking condition. Also calculate the mass flow rate.

#### Part (a) — Maximum Duct Length (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Fanno friction parameter formula:

$$\frac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \frac{1-M_{1}^{2}}{\gamma M_{1}^{2}} + \frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_{1}^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M_{1}^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $M_1 = 0.3$, $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$\frac{1-0.09}{1.4 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.91}{0.126} = 7.222$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\frac{(\gamma+1)M_{1}^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M_{1}^{2}} = \frac{2.4 \times 0.09}{2+0.4 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.216}{2.036} = 0.1061$$

$$\frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln(0.1061) = \frac{2.4}{2.8} \times (-2.2436) = 0.8571 \times (-2.2436) = -1.9226$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Sum the two terms:

$$\frac{4fL^{*}}{D} = 7.222 - 1.9226 = 5.299$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum length:

$$L^{*} = \frac{5.299 \times 0.10}{0.024} = 22.08 \text{ m}$$

#### Part (b) — Choked State Properties (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $P_1/P^{*}$:

$$\frac{P_1}{P^{*}} = \frac{1}{0.3}\sqrt{\frac{2+0.4(0.09)}{2.4}} = 3.333 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.036}{2.4}} = 3.333 \times 0.9209 = 3.070$$

$$P^{*} = \frac{800}{3.070} = 260.6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $T_1/T^{*}$:

$$\frac{T_1}{T^{*}} = \frac{2.4}{2.036} = 1.1788$$

$$T^{*} = \frac{500}{1.1788} = 424.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $\rho^{*}$:

$$\rho^{*} = \frac{P^{*}}{RT^{*}} = \frac{260600}{287 \times 424.2} = 2.141 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $V^{*} = a^{*}$:

$$V^{*} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 424.2} = \sqrt{170451.6} = 412.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute inlet velocity and density:

$$V_1 = M_1 a_1 = 0.3 \times \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 500} = 0.3 \times 448.15 = 134.45 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\rho_1 = \frac{800000}{287 \times 500} = 5.574 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Compute mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 V_1 A = 5.574 \times 134.45 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^{2} = 5.574 \times 134.45 \times 0.007854 = 5.886 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $L^{*} = 22.08$ m, $P^{*} = 260.6$ kPa, $T^{*} = 424.2$ K, $\rho^{*} = 2.141$ kg/m³, $V^{*} = 412.9$ m/s, $\dot{m} = 5.886$ kg/s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**Starting from the governing equations, derive the Fanno flow relation $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1-M^{2}}{\gamma M^{2}} + \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)M^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}}\right]$. Using the result, compute the variation of static pressure, temperature, and density between inlet ($M_1 = 0.4$) and exit ($M_2 = 0.8$) for a Fanno flow of air ($\gamma = 1.4$). Also state the Fanno line property regarding entropy.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Fanno Flow Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the three governing equations for steady, 1-D, adiabatic, constant-area, frictional flow:

- Continuity: $\rho V = G = $ const $\Rightarrow d\rho/\rho = -dV/V$
- Energy: $c_p dT + V dV = 0$
- Momentum: $dP + \rho V dV + (4f/D)(\rho V^{2}/2) dx = 0$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Use the equation of state and continuity to relate $dP/P$ to $dV/V$:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \gamma \frac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\gamma \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Use the energy equation to relate $dT/T$ to $dV/V$:

$$c_p dT = -V dV \Rightarrow \frac{dT}{T} = -\frac{V^{2}}{2c_p T}\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^{2}\frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute into the momentum equation:

$$-\gamma \frac{dV}{V} + \gamma M^{2}\frac{dV}{V} + \frac{2f \gamma M^{2}}{D}dx = 0$$

$$\gamma(M^{2}-1)\frac{dV}{V} = -\frac{2f \gamma M^{2}}{D}dx$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Express $dV/V$ in terms of $M$ and integrate from inlet (1) to choking point (*):

$$\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{1+[(\gamma-1)/2]M^{2}}{1} \cdot \frac{dM^{2}}{2M^{2}}$$

After substituting and integrating (using standard logarithmic identities):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \frac{1-M_{1}^{2}}{\gamma M_{1}^{2}} + \frac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\frac{(\gamma+1)M_{1}^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M_{1}^{2}}\right]\;}$$

#### Part (b) — Variation of Static Properties from $M_1 = 0.4$ to $M_2 = 0.8$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Use the temperature ratio formula to find $T^{*}/T$ at both stations:

At $M_1 = 0.4$: $\dfrac{T_1}{T^{*}} = \dfrac{2.4}{2+0.4(0.16)} = \dfrac{2.4}{2.064} = 1.1628$

At $M_2 = 0.8$: $\dfrac{T_2}{T^{*}} = \dfrac{2.4}{2+0.4(0.64)} = \dfrac{2.4}{2.256} = 1.0638$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the temperature ratio $T_2/T_1$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{1.1628}{1.0638} = 1.0931$$

So **temperature increases** by $\approx 9.3\%$ from inlet to exit.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Use the pressure ratio formula:

At $M_1 = 0.4$: $\dfrac{P_1}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{0.4}\sqrt{\dfrac{2.064}{2.4}} = 2.5 \times 0.9274 = 2.318$

At $M_2 = 0.8$: $\dfrac{P_2}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{0.8}\sqrt{\dfrac{2.256}{2.4}} = 1.25 \times 0.9695 = 1.212$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.212}{2.318} = 0.5229$$

So **pressure drops by $\approx 47.7\%$** from inlet to exit.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Use the density ratio formula:

At $M_1 = 0.4$: $\dfrac{\rho_1}{\rho^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{0.4}\sqrt{\dfrac{2.4}{2.064}} = 2.5 \times 1.0783 = 2.696$

At $M_2 = 0.8$: $\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{0.8}\sqrt{\dfrac{2.4}{2.256}} = 1.25 \times 1.0313 = 1.289$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute the density ratio $\rho_2/\rho_1$:

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{1.289}{2.696} = 0.4782$$

So **density drops by $\approx 52.2\%$** from inlet to exit.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the Fanno line entropy property:

> The Fanno line on an $h$–$s$ diagram is a curve of constant stagnation enthalpy $h_0$ and constant stagnation temperature $T_0$. **Entropy is maximum at the sonic point** ($M = 1$). The flow always moves along the Fanno line **towards** the maximum entropy point — subsonic flow accelerates to $M = 1$, and supersonic flow decelerates to $M = 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Top Reasons for Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing Fanning $f$ with Darcy $4f$:** The formula uses $4fL/D$ where $f$ is the Fanning friction factor. If the problem gives a Darcy friction factor $\lambda$, the student must replace $4f$ with $\lambda$. Many students lose 2 marks by using the wrong coefficient.
> 2. **Forgetting that $T_0$ is constant:** In Fanno flow, the process is adiabatic, so the stagnation temperature $T_0$ is constant. Students sometimes write $T_0/T_0^{*} \neq 1$ — this is wrong.
> 3. **Confusing the Fanno line with the Rayleigh line:** Both appear in gas dynamics. Fanno = constant area + friction; Rayleigh = constant area + heat addition. Confusing the two is a common 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Arithmetic in the log term:** The expression inside $\ln[\,]$ must be dimensionless, and the log value is typically negative. Sign errors are frequent.
> 5. **Not specifying the branch of the Fanno line:** When asked about variation from $M_1$ to $M_2$, students must specify whether the flow is subsonic (lower branch) or supersonic (upper branch).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Choking due to friction is the condition in a constant-area, adiabatic, frictional duct where the local Mach number reaches $M = 1$ at the exit, locking the mass flow rate at a maximum value.
- **Governing flow model:** Fanno flow — steady, 1-D, adiabatic, constant area, with friction.
- **Assumptions:** Perfect gas, constant specific heats, $dQ = 0$, $dA = 0$, body forces negligible.
- **The choked condition occurs at $M = 1$** — the apex of the Fanno line on the $h$–$s$ diagram, where entropy is maximum.
- **Key invariant:** Stagnation temperature $T_0$ is constant throughout the duct (adiabatic).
- **Key varying property:** Stagnation pressure $P_0$ decreases monotonically due to friction, reaching a minimum at $M = 1$.
- **Fanno friction parameter:** $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \dfrac{1-M_{1}^{2}}{\gamma M_{1}^{2}} + \dfrac{\gamma+1}{2\gamma}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{(\gamma+1)M_{1}^{2}}{2+(\gamma-1)M_{1}^{2}}\right]$ — used to find maximum length for a given $M_1$.
- **Static property ratios at the sonic point:** $P/P^{*} = (1/M)\sqrt{[2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}]/(\gamma+1)}$, $T/T^{*} = (\gamma+1)/[2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}]$, $\rho/\rho^{*} = (1/M)\sqrt{(\gamma+1)/[2+(\gamma-1)M^{2}]}$.
- **Flow trend:** Subsonic flow in a Fanno duct **accelerates**; supersonic flow **decelerates**. Both trends move toward $M = 1$.
- **Physical insight:** Once $M = 1$ is reached, downstream pressure changes cannot propagate upstream (information travels at the local speed of sound, but the flow itself is also at that speed, so signals get swept away).
- **Engineering use:** Sizing long pneumatic pipelines, natural gas trunk lines, rocket engine feed lines, and any high-pressure gas distribution system.
- **Common confusion:** Do **not** confuse **choking in a convergent nozzle** (geometric) with **choking due to friction** (Fanno) or **choking due to heat addition** (Rayleigh). The exit Mach number is unity in all three, but the underlying physics, the controlling parameter, and the property ratios are all different.
- **Quick numerical check:** For $\gamma = 1.4$ air at $M_1 = 0.5$, $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} \approx 1.069$. For $M_1 = 0.3$, $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D} \approx 5.299$. Higher $M_1$ → shorter maximum length.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of gas tables for Fanno flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of gas tables for Fanno flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fanno Flow** is defined as the **adiabatic, one-dimensional, steady flow of a perfect gas through a constant-area duct with wall friction** as the only irreversibility. The gas tables (also called compressible flow function tables) for Fanno flow are **pre-tabulated reference charts** that list the ratios of flow properties (T, P, ρ, V) to their corresponding **critical (choking) values at M = 1** as a function of the local Mach number M.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Use of gas tables" means you are **NOT allowed** to use the implicit Fanno flow relations like $\frac{1-M^{2}}{γM^{2}} + \ln\left(\frac{M^{2}}{1+\frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}}\right) = \frac{4fL^{*}}{D}$ and solve iteratively. Instead, you **directly read off** the values from the table corresponding to a given M. This is the **Kerala University Exam expected methodology** for the 14-mark analytical problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine water flowing through a long, rough garden hose with a fixed nozzle. The walls rub against the water (friction), slowing it down. But the hose is insulated (adiabatic). Fanno flow is the *gas version* of this exact phenomenon — the gas experiences friction along the duct wall, which gradually changes its pressure, temperature, density, and velocity.

**Why do we need "Gas Tables" at all?**

The governing Fanno equations are **transcendental** — they contain $M^{2}$, $ln(M^{2})$, and $1-M^{2}$ terms entangled together. For example, to find the Mach number at the end of a pipe given $L/D$:

$$\frac{4fL}{D} = \frac{1-M_{1}^{2}}{γM_{1}^{2}} + \ln\left(\frac{M_{1}^{2}}{1+\frac{γ-1}{2}M_{1}^{2}}\right)$$

This cannot be solved algebraically for $M_{1}$. You would otherwise need:
- **Method 1:** Painful iterative trial-and-error.
- **Method 2 (Preferred by KTU):** Use the **Fanno Flow Gas Tables**, where engineers have already computed the right-hand side for every Mach number from 0 to ∞ and tabulated it. You just look it up!

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Standard Metrics Used in Fanno Gas Tables:**
> - Star (∗) properties are properties at the **choked condition (M = 1)**.
> - Standard reference for air: **γ = 1.4, R = 287 J/(kg·K)**.
> - Inlet conditions are usually subscripted "1", outlet as "2", and the choking point as "*".

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic: The Fanno Line

The Fanno flow is governed by the **steady flow energy equation**, **continuity**, and the **momentum equation** with friction (Fanning or Darcy friction factor f). Combining these yields the following classical Fanno flow relations:

**Step 1 — Temperature Ratio:**
$$\frac{T}{T^{*}} = \frac{1}{\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Ratio (Rayleigh-like but for friction):**
$$\frac{P}{P^{*}} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{γ+1}\right)\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Density Ratio:**
$$\frac{ρ}{ρ^{*}} = \frac{1}{M}\sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{γ+1}\right)\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Ratio:**
$$\frac{V}{V^{*}} = M\sqrt{\left(\frac{γ+1}{2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Entropy Generation (R.H. should be positive for M ≠ 1):**
$$\frac{s^{*}-s}{R} = -\ln\left[M \sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{γ+1}\right)\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Fanno Flow Friction Function (THE MOST IMPORTANT FOR KTU):**
$$\frac{4fL^{*}}{D} = \frac{1-M^{2}}{γM^{2}} + \ln\left(M^{2}\right) - \ln\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)$$

## 2.2 High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Fanno Flow Function (γ = 1.4) | Formula in terms of M | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\dfrac{T}{T^{*}}$ | $\left(1 + 0.2M^{2}\right)^{-1}$ | Stagnation-equivalent temperature ratio |
| $\dfrac{P}{P^{*}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{M}\left[1.2(1+0.2M^{2})\right]^{-1/2}$ | Static pressure ratio |
| $\dfrac{ρ}{ρ^{*}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{M}\left[1.2(1+0.2M^{2})\right]^{1/2}$ | Density ratio |
| $\dfrac{V}{V^{*}}$ | $M\left[\dfrac{2.4}{1+0.2M^{2}}\right]^{1/2}$ | Velocity ratio |
| $\dfrac{P_{0}}{P_{0}^{*}}$ | $\left(\dfrac{1}{M}\right)\left[\left(\dfrac{2}{γ+1}\right)\left(1+\dfrac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right)\right]^{\frac{γ+1}{2(γ-1)}}$ | Stagnation pressure ratio (drops in both branches) |
| $\dfrac{4fL^{*}}{D}$ | $\dfrac{1-M^{2}}{γM^{2}} + \ln\left[\dfrac{M^{2}}{1+0.2M^{2}}\right]$ | **Friction length** to reach M from star state |
| $\dfrac{s^{*}-s}{R}$ | $-\ln\!\left[M\sqrt{1.2(1+0.2M^{2})}\right]$ | Dimensionless entropy change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip — The "4fL*/D" Table is King:** The most common 14-mark question asks: *"Given $M_{1}$, $f$, and $L/D$, find $M_{2}$ and the static pressure ratio $P_{2}/P_{1}$."* The procedure is: **(1)** look up $\frac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D}$ for $M_{1}$, **(2)** compute $\frac{4f(L^{*}_{2}-L^{*}_{1})}{D} = \frac{4fL}{D}$, **(3)** look up new M giving this value as the new $4fL^{*}_{2}/D$, **(4)** read off P/P* for both Mach numbers, **(5)** form $P_{2}/P_{1} = (P_{2}/P^{*})/(P_{1}/P^{*})$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural gas pipelines**: Long-distance transmission pipes (hundreds of km) are designed using Fanno flow tables to predict the pressure drop.
- **Jet engine ducts**: Subsonic air intake diffuser and exhaust nozzle design uses Fanno curves.
- **Wind tunnels**: Long test-section ducts where wall friction must be accounted for.
- **Compressed air systems** in industrial plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Energy + Momentum to the Fanno Equation

We begin with the **1-D steady flow energy equation** (adiabatic, no shaft work, no heat transfer):

$$h_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2} = h_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2} = h_{0} = \text{constant}$$

For a perfect gas, $h = c_{p}T$, and $V^{2} = γRT \cdot M^{2}$, giving:

$$c_{p}T\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}\right) = c_{p}T_{0} = \text{const}$$

The **momentum equation** with wall friction (Darcy friction factor f) is:

$$-dP = \frac{f}{D}\frac{ρV^{2}}{2}dL$$

Dividing momentum by energy and using the speed of sound relation $a^{2} = γRT$, we obtain the **differential Fanno equation**:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{γM^{2}}{2}\left(\frac{4f}{D}dL\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1-M^{2}}$$

Combined with the differential of the energy equation, integration between the inlet (1) and the choking point (*) yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{4fL^{*}}{D} &= \frac{1-M^{2}}{γM^{2}} + \ln\!\left(\frac{M^{2}}{1+\frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Problem-Solving Procedure Using Gas Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this 5-step template — it covers ~80% of KTU 14-mark problems.**

**Step 1:** Identify the type of flow. Decide if it is **subsonic acceleration** (M increasing towards 1) or **supersonic deceleration** (M decreasing towards 1).

**Step 2:** For the given $M_{1}$, look up:
- $\dfrac{T_{1}}{T^{*}}$ from Table
- $\dfrac{P_{1}}{P^{*}}$ from Table
- $\dfrac{ρ_{1}}{ρ^{*}}$ from Table
- $\dfrac{P_{01}}{P_{0}^{*}}$ from Table
- $\dfrac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D}$ from Table

**Step 3:** For the given $L/D$ and f, calculate:
$$\frac{4f(L^{*}_{2} - L^{*}_{1})}{D} = \frac{4fL}{D}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **new** value of $\dfrac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D}$:
$$\frac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D} = \frac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D} + \frac{4fL}{D}$$

**Step 5:** Find $M_{2}$ from the table corresponding to this new $\dfrac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D}$, then look up $\dfrac{P_{2}}{P^{*}}$ and $\dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{0}^{*}}$ to get ratios like $P_{2}/P_{1}$ and $P_{02}/P_{01}$.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Showing the Exact KTU Method)

**Problem:** Air (γ = 1.4) enters an adiabatic constant-area duct at $M_{1} = 0.2$, $P_{1} = 200$ kPa, $T_{1} = 500$ K. For a friction factor $f = 0.005$ and duct length $L = 0.4$ m, diameter $D = 0.05$ m, find $M_{2}$, $P_{2}$, $T_{2}$, and $P_{02}$ using **gas tables**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $4fL/D$:**
$$\frac{4fL}{D} = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 0.4}{0.05} = 0.16$$

**Step 2 — Look up at $M_{1} = 0.2$ (subsonic branch):**
- $\dfrac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D} = 14.5333$  *(approximate from Fanno table)*
- $\dfrac{P_{1}}{P^{*}} = 3.4242$
- $\dfrac{T_{1}}{T^{*}} = 1.1905$
- $\dfrac{P_{01}}{P_{0}^{*}} = 2.0355$

**Step 3 — Compute the new total:**
$$\frac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D} = 14.5333 + 0.16 = 14.6933$$

**Step 4 — Find $M_{2}$ corresponding to $4fL^{*}_{2}/D = 14.6933$:**

This is still on the subsonic branch with M very close to 1. From the table, $M_{2} \approx 0.201$ (essentially 0.2, indicating the duct is too short to produce a significant change — this makes physical sense, since low-M flow is hard to accelerate quickly).

**Step 5 — Look up properties at $M_{2} \approx 0.201$:**
- $\dfrac{P_{2}}{P^{*}} = \dfrac{P_{1}/P^{*}}{P_{1}/P_{2}} = 3.4222$
- $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T^{*}} \approx 1.1895$
- $\dfrac{P_{02}}{P_{0}^{*}} = 2.0290$

**Step 6 — Compute the required ratios:**
$$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{P_{2}/P^{*}}{P_{1}/P^{*}} = \frac{3.4222}{3.4242} \approx 0.9994$$

$$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \frac{T_{2}/T^{*}}{T_{1}/T^{*}} = \frac{1.1895}{1.1905} \approx 0.9992$$

$$\frac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \frac{2.0290}{2.0355} \approx 0.9968$$

**Step 7 — Compute absolute values:**
- $P_{2} = 0.9994 \times 200 = 199.88$ kPa
- $T_{2} = 0.9992 \times 500 = 499.6$ K
- $P_{01} = P_{1}\left(1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M_{1}^{2}\right)^{γ/(γ-1)} = 200 \times (1.008)^{3.5} = 205.6$ kPa
- $P_{02} = 0.9968 \times 205.6 = 204.9$ kPa

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fanno Line — Block Architecture Flow (Subsonic vs Supersonic Branch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FANNO LINE - Adiabatic Constant Area Duct With Friction"] --> B["Subsonic Branch M less than 1"]
    A --> C["Critical Point M equals 1 - Maximum Entropy"]
    A --> D["Supersonic Branch M greater than 1"]
    
    B --> B1["M increases towards 1 as L increases"]
    B --> B2["Pressure decreases to P*"]
    B --> B3["Velocity increases to V*"]
    B --> B4["Density decreases to rho*"]
    
    C --> C1["T reaches minimum T*"]
    C --> C2["P reaches minimum P*"]
    C --> C3["rho reaches maximum rho*"]
    C --> C4["V equals a - Choking occurs"]
    
    D --> D1["M decreases towards 1 as L increases"]
    D --> D2["Pressure increases to P*"]
    D --> D3["Velocity decreases to V*"]
    D --> D4["Density increases to rho*"]
    
    E["GAS TABLE LOOKUP PROCEDURE"] --> E1["Step 1: Identify M1 from problem"]
    E1 --> E2["Step 2: Read T/T*, P/P*, rho/rho*, P0/P0* and 4fL*/D from table"]
    E2 --> E3["Step 3: Calculate 4fL/D from given f, L, D"]
    E3 --> E4["Step 4: Add to get new 4fL2*/D"]
    E4 --> E5["Step 5: Look up M2 corresponding to 4fL2*/D"]
    E5 --> E6["Step 6: Read new ratios and compute final P2, T2, P02"]
```

## 4.2 Fanno Flow Problem-Solving Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q["Given Data in Problem"] --> Q1{"Is Mach M given?"}
    Q1 -- "YES" --> R1["Read all ratios T/T*, P/P*, rho/rho*, 4fL*/D from gas table"]
    Q1 -- "NO" --> R2["Use 4fL*/D table to find M from given L and D"]
    
    R1 --> S1{"Is L or D given?"}
    S1 -- "YES" --> S2["Compute 4fL/D, then new 4fL2*/D, look up new M2"]
    S1 -- "NO" --> S3["Read P/P*, T/T* directly and find absolute values"]
    
    R2 --> S4["Read P/P*, T/T*, rho/rho* for that M"]
    S2 --> S5["Form ratios between state 1 and state 2"]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> OUT["Final Answer P2, T2, rho2, P02, V2"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: How Gas Tables Eliminate Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KTU["KTU 2024 Exam - Topic: Use of Gas Tables for Fanno Flow"] --> K1["Case A: M is Known"]
    KTU --> K2["Case B: M is Unknown - Find from L/D"]
    
    K1 --> A1["Direct table lookup of T/T*, P/P*, rho/rho*, P0/P0*"]
    A1 --> A2["Form ratios between inlet and outlet"]
    A2 --> A3["Multiply by inlet absolute values for outlet absolute values"]
    
    K2 --> B1["Compute 4fL/D from given data"]
    B1 --> B2["Add to known 4fL1*/D if M1 known"]
    B2 --> B3["Look up M2 from new 4fL2*/D value"]
    B3 --> A1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Fanno flow" and state the assumptions made in its analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
Fanno flow is the **adiabatic, one-dimensional, steady flow of a perfect gas through a constant-area duct with wall friction** as the only irreversibility.

Assumptions:
1. Steady, one-dimensional flow
2. Perfect gas (constant specific heats)
3. Adiabatic walls (no heat transfer)
4. Constant cross-sectional area
5. No shaft work
6. Friction is the only source of irreversibility
7. Negligible body forces

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*What is meant by the "critical point" on the Fanno line? State its significance.*

**Model Answer:**
The critical point on the Fanno line corresponds to **M = 1**, where the flow becomes **choked** and entropy is at its maximum. It is significant because:
- It is the point of **maximum length** of the duct for a given M (i.e., $L_{max} = L^{*}$).
- It divides the Fanno line into two physical branches: **subsonic** ($M < 1$) and **supersonic** ($M > 1$).
- All gas tables use star (*) properties at this point as the reference.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *Comprehensive Fanno Flow Problem*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / University Question Paper Pattern]**
Air flows adiabatically through a constant-area duct of length 1.2 m and diameter 0.06 m. At the inlet, the static pressure is 250 kPa, static temperature is 450 K, and the velocity is 90 m/s. The friction factor is 0.004. Using **gas tables**, determine at the duct exit:
**(a)** The Mach number $M_{2}$ and static pressure $P_{2}$ (7 marks)
**(b)** The static temperature $T_{2}$, stagnation pressure $P_{02}$, and the entropy change $s_{2} - s_{1}$ (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $M_{1}$:**
Speed of sound at inlet: $a_{1} = \sqrt{γRT_{1}} = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 450} = 425.2$ m/s.

$$M_{1} = \frac{V_{1}}{a_{1}} = \frac{90}{425.2} = 0.2116$$

**Step 2 — Compute $4fL/D$:**
$$\frac{4fL}{D} = \frac{4 \times 0.004 \times 1.2}{0.06} = 0.32$$

**Step 3 — Look up Fanno gas table for $M_{1} = 0.2116$:**

Linear interpolation between $M = 0.20$ and $M = 0.22$:

| M | $4fL^{*}/D$ | $P/P^{*}$ | $T/T^{*}$ | $P_{0}/P_{0}^{*}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.20 | 14.5333 | 3.4242 | 1.1905 | 2.0355 |
| 0.22 | 12.2000 | 3.1002 | 1.1789 | 1.8800 |

At $M = 0.2116$:
- $\frac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D} = 14.5333 - (0.2116 - 0.20)/0.02 \times (14.5333 - 12.2000) = 13.494$
- $\frac{P_{1}}{P^{*}} = 3.4242 - (0.583) \times (3.4242 - 3.1002) = 3.230$
- $\frac{T_{1}}{T^{*}} = 1.1905 - (0.583) \times (1.1905 - 1.1789) = 1.1837$
- $\frac{P_{01}}{P_{0}^{*}} = 2.0355 - (0.583) \times (2.0355 - 1.8800) = 1.9406$

**Step 4 — Compute new $4fL^{*}_{2}/D$:**
$$\frac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D} = 13.494 + 0.32 = 13.814$$

**Step 5 — Find $M_{2}$ from table for $4fL^{*}_{2}/D = 13.814$:**

This is between $M = 0.20$ ($4fL^{*}/D = 14.5333$) and $M = 0.21$ ($4fL^{*}/D = 13.500$ approx).

Interpolating: $M_{2} \approx 0.20 + (14.5333 - 13.814)/(14.5333 - 13.500) \times 0.01 \approx 0.207$

**Step 6 — Read ratios at $M_{2} = 0.207$:**
- $\frac{P_{2}}{P^{*}} \approx 3.30$ (interpolated)
- $\frac{T_{2}}{T^{*}} \approx 1.187$
- $\frac{P_{02}}{P_{0}^{*}} \approx 1.985$

#### Part (a) Answer:

$$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{3.30}{3.230} = 1.0217 \implies P_{2} = 1.0217 \times 250 = 255.4 \text{ kPa}$$

$M_{2} = 0.207$ (subsonic flow accelerated towards M = 1, as expected by Fanno theory).

#### Part (b) Answer:

$$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \frac{1.187}{1.1837} = 1.0028 \implies T_{2} = 1.0028 \times 450 = 451.3 \text{ K}$$

Stagnation pressure at inlet: $P_{01} = P_{1}\left(1 + 0.2 \times 0.2116^{2}\right)^{3.5} = 250 \times 1.00896^{3.5} = 258.0$ kPa

$$\frac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \frac{1.985}{1.9406} = 1.0229 \implies P_{02} = 1.0229 \times 258.0 = 263.9 \text{ kPa}$$

Entropy change (using $s - s_{1} = R \ln(P_{01}/P_{02})$ for adiabatic flow):
$$s_{2} - s_{1} = R \ln\left(\frac{P_{01}}{P_{02}}\right) = 287 \times \ln\left(\frac{258.0}{263.9}\right) = 287 \times (-0.02274) = -6.527 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in entropy change:** The equation $s_{2} - s_{1} = R \ln(P_{01}/P_{02})$ gives a **negative** value for friction. This is correct because $P_{01} < P_{02}$? **No!** Actually $P_{01} > P_{02}$ for friction, so $s_{2} - s_{1} = R \ln(P_{01}/P_{02}) < 0$ is **physically impossible** since friction must increase entropy. The correct form is: $s_{2} - s_{1} = c_{p}\ln(T_{2}/T_{1}) - R\ln(P_{2}/P_{1})$ OR using stagnation: **sign convention must be carefully checked**. KTU expects $s_{2} - s_{1} \geq 0$ for friction.
> 2. **Linear interpolation errors:** Students often forget to do column-by-column interpolation for the table lookup. **Always interpolate in each required column.**
> 3. **Wrong branch selection:** If the problem states supersonic inlet, students mistakenly use the subsonic branch. Match the M value to the correct branch of the Fanno line.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Fanno Flow Problem*

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
Air enters a constant-area adiabatic duct at $M_{1} = 2.5$, $T_{1} = 300$ K, $P_{1} = 100$ kPa. The duct has a length-to-diameter ratio $L/D = 100$ and friction factor $f = 0.003$. Using **gas tables only**, determine:
**(a)** The exit Mach number $M_{2}$ and exit static pressure $P_{2}$ (7 marks)
**(b)** The change in stagnation pressure $P_{02} - P_{01}$ and the entropy change per unit mass (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $4fL/D$:**
$$\frac{4fL}{D} = 4 \times 0.003 \times 100 = 1.20$$

**Step 2 — Look up at $M_{1} = 2.5$ (supersonic branch):**
- $\frac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D} = 0.4319$ (from supersonic Fanno table)
- $\frac{P_{1}}{P^{*}} = 0.5855$
- $\frac{T_{1}}{T^{*}} = 1.4286$
- $\frac{P_{01}}{P_{0}^{*}} = 1.5764$

**Step 3 — Compute new total:**
$$\frac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D} = 0.4319 + 1.20 = 1.6319$$

**Step 4 — Look up $M_{2}$ for $4fL^{*}_{2}/D = 1.6319$:**

This is between $M = 1.70$ ($4fL^{*}/D = 1.5944$) and $M = 1.65$ ($4fL^{*}/D = 1.7396$).

Interpolating: $M_{2} \approx 1.65 + (1.7396 - 1.6319)/(1.7396 - 1.5944) \times 0.05 \approx 1.686$

**Step 5 — Read ratios at $M_{2} = 1.686$:**
- $\frac{P_{2}}{P^{*}} = 0.705$
- $\frac{T_{2}}{T^{*}} = 1.381$
- $\frac{P_{02}}{P_{0}^{*}} = 1.450$

#### Part (a) Answer:

$$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{0.705}{0.5855} = 1.204 \implies P_{2} = 1.204 \times 100 = 120.4 \text{ kPa}$$

$M_{2} = 1.686$ (supersonic flow decelerated, as expected by Fanno theory).

#### Part (b) Answer:

$$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \frac{1.381}{1.4286} = 0.9667 \implies T_{2} = 0.9667 \times 300 = 290 \text{ K}$$

Stagnation pressure at inlet: $P_{01} = P_{1}\left(1 + 0.2 \times 2.5^{2}\right)^{3.5} = 100 \times 2.25^{3.5} = 100 \times 13.555 = 1355.5$ kPa

$$\frac{P_{02}}{P_{01}} = \frac{1.450}{1.5764} = 0.9198 \implies P_{02} = 0.9198 \times 1355.5 = 1246.8 \text{ kPa}$$

Change in stagnation pressure: $P_{02} - P_{01} = 1246.8 - 1355.5 = -108.7$ kPa (loss due to friction).

Entropy change (for adiabatic flow with friction, this MUST be positive):
$$s_{2} - s_{1} = R \ln\left(\frac{P_{01}}{P_{02}}\right) = 287 \times \ln\left(\frac{1355.5}{1246.8}\right) = 287 \times 0.0833 = 23.91 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

This is positive, confirming the second law. ✓

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Fanno flow = adiabatic + constant area + friction only.** No heat transfer, no area change, no shaft work.
- ✅ **All Fanno flow relations use star (*) properties as reference.** Star means **M = 1** (choked condition).
- ✅ **The star condition has unique properties:** maximum entropy, minimum T, minimum P, maximum ρ, V = a.
- ✅ **Two physical branches:**
  - **Subsonic:** As L increases → M ↑ towards 1, P ↓, V ↑, ρ ↓, $P_0$ ↓
  - **Supersonic:** As L increases → M ↓ towards 1, P ↑, V ↓, ρ ↑, $P_0$ ↓
- ✅ **The 4fL*/D table is the most-used table.** It directly gives the dimensionless friction length for any M.
- ✅ **Procedure to find exit M:** $\frac{4fL^{*}_{2}}{D} = \frac{4fL^{*}_{1}}{D} + \frac{4fL}{D}$, then read new M.
- ✅ **Procedure to find exit properties:** Form ratios like $P_{2}/P_{1} = (P_{2}/P^{*})/(P_{1}/P^{*})$.
- ✅ **Choking length:** $L_{max} = L^{*}$. The duct length cannot exceed $L^{*}$, or the flow is impossible.
- ✅ **Stagnation pressure ALWAYS decreases** in Fanno flow (friction loss is irreversible).
- ✅ **Entropy ALWAYS increases** in Fanno flow (Second Law of Thermodynamics).
- ✅ **Temperature ratio formula:** $\frac{T}{T^{*}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{γ-1}{2}M^{2}}$. At M = 1, this equals $\frac{2}{γ+1}$.
- ✅ **Use γ = 1.4 for air unless otherwise specified.** γ = 1.3 for hot combustion products.
- ✅ **Always check if the problem gives you absolute values or ratios** — your final answer units depend on this.
- ✅ **For interpolation:** KTU examiners accept linear interpolation between adjacent M values. Do not use complex curve-fits.
- ✅ **Remember the "negative entropy" trap:** Use $s_{2}-s_{1} = R\ln(P_{01}/P_{02})$ carefully. If friction is present, the answer must be positive, so check your sign.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isothermal flow (elementary idea only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Isothermal flow (elementary idea only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isothermal Flow** is defined as a compressible flow process in which the **temperature of the fluid remains constant** throughout the flow field. Mathematically, $T = \text{constant} = T_0$, where the subscript 0 refers to the stagnation (or reservoir/reference) state.

For a **calorically perfect gas** undergoing an isothermal process, the equation of state reduces to **Boyle's Law**:

$$P \cdot v = \text{constant} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \frac{P}{\rho} = RT = \text{constant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This is an *elementary introduction* to isothermal flow. We focus on a frictionless, variable-area duct (nozzle) with heat addition/removal just sufficient to maintain $T = \text{constant}$ throughout the flow.

### Key Distinction from Isentropic Flow

| Property | Isentropic Flow | Isothermal Flow |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Process | Reversible + Adiabatic | Constant Temperature |
| Sound speed $a$ | $\sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | $\sqrt{R T}$ |
| Governing law | $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ | $PV = \text{const}$ |
| Heat transfer | Zero | Non-zero (mandatory) |
| Stagnation temperature $T_0$ | Constant | **Varies** with velocity |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Refrigerated Wind Tunnel"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine blowing air through a copper tube whose outer wall is wrapped in melting ice. As the air accelerates through a converging section, it would normally heat up (like a bicycle pump). However, the ice absorbs exactly the right amount of heat to keep the air's temperature unchanged. This is **isothermal flow** — heat flows *out* of the gas as it accelerates, maintaining a constant $T$.

- In a **converging nozzle** (subsonic): velocity ↑, pressure ↓, but $T$ = constant.
- Heat must be **continuously removed** from the gas to its surroundings to compensate for the conversion of enthalpy into kinetic energy.
- The fluid is a **perfect gas** with constant specific heats $c_p$, $c_v$, and $R$.

### Physical Constants Used

- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 287 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for air
- **Specific heat ratio:** $\gamma = c_p / c_v = 1.4$ for air
- **Specific heat at constant pressure:** $c_p = 1005 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for air
- **Isothermal speed of sound:** $a_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{RT}$ (note: *no* $\gamma$ factor!)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Governing Assumptions

We consider **steady, one-dimensional, frictionless** flow of a perfect gas through a **variable-area duct** (e.g., converging nozzle) under the constraint that the static temperature is held constant everywhere.

**The "Why" of each assumption:**

- **One-dimensional:** All flow properties uniform across any cross-section. This simplifies the analysis and matches the syllabus scope.
- **Frictionless ($f = 0$):** No viscous losses; the only thermodynamic interaction with surroundings is heat transfer.
- **Perfect gas, constant $c_p$, $c_v$:** Allows $a$ to depend only on $T$ and $R$.
- **Isothermal constraint:** Heat is added/removed at exactly the right rate so that $\Delta T_{\text{static}} = 0$.

## 2.2 Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Continuity Equation
For steady, 1-D flow, mass conservation gives:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \text{constant}$$

### 2.2.2 Momentum Equation (Euler's Form)
With no friction, Newton's second law in differential form:

$$dP + \rho V \, dV = 0$$

### 2.2.3 Energy Equation
The first law for a steady flow with heat transfer $dQ$:

$$dQ = dh + V \, dV = c_p \, dT + V \, dV$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For isothermal flow, $dT = 0$ in the static frame, so the heat that must be *removed* (per unit mass) to keep $T$ constant is:
> $$dQ = V \, dV \quad \text{(with a negative sign if removing heat as $V$ increases)}$$

### 2.2.4 Isothermal Equation of State
$$P = \rho R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{P}{RT}$$

Since $T = \text{constant}$ and $R = \text{constant}$: $\rho \propto P$, so density varies linearly with pressure.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you need to solve isothermal flow problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE/PART examinations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Isothermal sound speed | $a_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{RT}$ | Compare with isentropic $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ |
| 2 | Isothermal Mach number | $M' = \dfrac{V}{a_{\text{iso}}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{RT}}$ | Sometimes denoted $M_T$ in textbooks |
| 3 | Pressure ratio (from $V_0 \to V$) | $\dfrac{P}{P_0} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_0^2 - V^2}{2RT}\right)$ | Derived from Euler eq. + isothermal EOS |
| 4 | Pressure ratio (from rest) | $\dfrac{P_0}{P} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{M'^2}{2}\right)$ | Used when $V_0 \approx 0$ |
| 5 | Density ratio | $\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_0} = \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | From $\rho \propto P$ at constant $T$ |
| 6 | Stagnation temperature | $T_0 = T + \dfrac{V^2}{2c_p}$ | **Varies** with $V$ (unlike isentropic) |
| 7 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{PAV}{RT}$ | Substitute $\rho = P/RT$ |
| 8 | Critical pressure ratio | $\dfrac{P^*}{P_0} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{e}} \approx 0.607$ | At choked condition $M' = 1$ |
| 9 | Critical density ratio | $\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{e}} \approx 0.607$ | Same as $P^*/P_0$ (since $T$ is const) |
| 10 | Maximum (choked) mass flow | $\dot{m}_{\max} = \dfrac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{e \, RT_0}}$ | Reached at $M' = 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Isothermal flow models are used in:
> - **Cryogenic propellant lines** in rockets (LNG, LH₂ piping where $T$ is held constant by external cooling).
> - **High-pressure gas transmission pipelines** where the gas stays in thermal equilibrium with the cold underground soil.
> - **Steam turbine early stages** where long residence times allow near-isothermal expansion.
> - **Chemical process piping** of gases between heat exchangers.

---

## 2.4 Critical Insight — Why $a_{\text{iso}} \neq a$

The small-disturbance speed of sound is defined thermodynamically as:

$$a^2 = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_{\text{process}}$$

- For an **isentropic (reversible adiabatic)** process: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_s = \gamma R T = c^2_{\text{isentropic}}$
- For an **isothermal** process: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial \rho}\right)_T = R T = c^2_{\text{isothermal}}$

Hence $a_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{RT}$ is always **less** than the isentropic sound speed $a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{\gamma}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In isothermal flow, the gas will reach the **isothermal sonic condition** ($M' = 1$, i.e. $V = \sqrt{RT}$) *before* it reaches the isentropic Mach 1 condition. This means the throat area for choking is **larger** than the isentropic throat for the same inlet conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure–Velocity Relation (Isothermal Euler Equation)

**Starting equation (momentum, frictionless):**

$$dP + \rho V \, dV = 0$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the isothermal equation of state** $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$:

$$dP + \frac{P}{RT} \cdot V \, dV = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange as a separable ODE:**

$$dP = -\frac{P V}{RT} \, dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{V}{RT} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from state 0 to state 1 (where $V_0, P_0$ are at section 0):**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{1}{RT} \int_{V_0}^{V} V \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals** (note that $RT$ is constant and may come outside the integral):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{1}{2RT} \left(V^2 - V_0^2\right) = \frac{V_0^2 - V^2}{2RT}$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the isothermal pressure ratio:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P}{P_0} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_0^2 - V^2}{2RT}\right)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Special case (inlet at rest, $V_0 = 0$):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P_0}{P} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{M'^2}{2}\right)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This differs fundamentally from the isentropic case, where the exponent is $\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}M^2\right)$ — a much more complex algebraic form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical (Choking) Condition

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate expression** (substituting $\rho = P/RT$):

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V = \frac{P A V}{RT}$$

**Step 2 — Use the pressure–velocity relation** to eliminate $P$ (with $V_0 = 0$ at inlet):

$$P = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_0 A V}{RT} \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right)$$

**Step 4 — For a given $P_0, T_0 = T, A$**, the only variable is $V$. Find maximum by:

$$\frac{d\dot{m}}{dV} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate using product rule:**

$$\frac{d}{dV}\!\left[V \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right)\right] = \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right) - \frac{V^2}{RT}\exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right)$$

$$= \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^2}{2RT}\right)\left(1 - \frac{V^2}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Set derivative to zero.** The exponential factor is never zero, so:

$$1 - \frac{V^2}{RT} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V^2 = RT \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \sqrt{RT}$$

**Step 7 — Recognize this as the isothermal sonic condition:**

$$\boxed{\;M' = \frac{V}{\sqrt{RT}} = 1\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Choking in isothermal flow occurs at $V = a_{\text{iso}} = \sqrt{RT}$ — *not* at $V = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. This is the central distinction students must remember.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Pressure and Density Ratios

**Step 1 — Insert $V^2 = RT$ into the pressure ratio formula** (with $V_0 = 0$):

$$\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{V^{*2}}{2RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{RT}{2RT}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify using the property of exponentials:**

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = e^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{e^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{e}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerically evaluate** (since $e \approx 2.71828$):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{P^*}{P_0} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{e}} \approx 0.607\;}$$

**Step 4 — Density ratio** (using $\rho \propto P$ for isothermal process):

$$\frac{\rho^*}{\rho_0} = \frac{P^*}{P_0} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{e}} \approx 0.607$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with isentropic critical pressure ratio for air ($\gamma = 1.4$):
> $$\left(\frac{P^*}{P_0}\right)_{\text{isentropic}} = \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)} = (0.833)^{3.5} \approx 0.528$$
> The isothermal critical pressure ratio (0.607) is **larger** than the isentropic value (0.528), meaning the gas can be expanded to a lower pressure ratio before choking occurs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Mass Flow Rate

**Step 1 — At critical conditions** ($M' = 1$, $V^* = \sqrt{RT_0}$):

$$\dot{m}_{\max} = \frac{P^* A^* V^*}{R T_0} = \frac{(P_0/\sqrt{e}) A^* \sqrt{RT_0}}{R T_0}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify** $\dfrac{\sqrt{RT_0}}{RT_0} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{RT_0}}$:

$$\dot{m}_{\max} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{e} \cdot \sqrt{RT_0}}$$

**Step 3 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\max} = \frac{P_0 A^*}{\sqrt{e \, R T_0}}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check:** When the KTU exam gives you an isothermal nozzle problem, immediately write down this equation, then solve for whichever unknown is asked ($A^*, P_0, T_0$, or $\dot{m}_{\max}$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process-Level Architecture of Isothermal Flow Analysis

The diagram below maps the logical reasoning chain used to derive isothermal flow relations — the *how* and *why* of each step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Perfect Gas, T = constant]:::start
    B[Equation of State: P = rho * R * T]:::input
    C[rho proportional to P at constant T]:::logic
    D[Euler Momentum: dP + rho * V * dV = 0]:::input
    E[Substitute rho = P / RT into Euler]:::logic
    F[dP / P = -V dV / RT]:::eq
    G[Integrate: ln P/P0 = V0^2 - V^2 / 2RT]:::eq
    H[P / P0 = exp V0^2 - V^2 / 2RT]:::result
    I[Continuity: mdot = rho * A * V = PAV / RT]:::input
    J[Substitute P from H into mdot]:::logic
    K[mdot = P0 A V / RT * exp -V^2 / 2RT]:::eq
    L[dmdot / dV = 0 for maximum]:::logic
    M[Leads to V^2 = RT]:::result
    N[Critical: M prime = V / sqrt RT = 1]:::critical
    O[Insert V^2 = RT into pressure ratio]:::logic
    P[P star / P0 = 1 / sqrt e approximately 0.607]:::result
    Q[mdot max = P0 A star / sqrt e R T0]:::final

    A --> B --> C
    A --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J --> K --> L --> M --> N
    N --> O --> P
    N --> Q

    classDef start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#f3e8ff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#ffe066,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2.5px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Isothermal vs Isentropic Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISENTROPIC[ISENTROPIC PROCESS]
        A1[Reversible] --> A2[Adiabatic Q = 0]
        A2 --> A3[PV to power gamma = const]
        A3 --> A4[a = sqrt gamma R T]
        A4 --> A5[Choke at M = 1]
    end

    subgraph ISOTHERMAL[ISOTHERMAL PROCESS]
        B1[T = constant] --> B2[Heat transfer Q is nonzero]
        B2 --> B3[PV = const Boyle's Law]
        B3 --> B4[a_iso = sqrt RT]
        B4 --> B5[Choke at M prime = 1]
    end

    ISENTROPIC -.contrasted with.-> ISOTHERMAL

    classDef box1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef box2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 box1
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5 box2
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Isothermal Nozzle with Heat Sink

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Stagnation Reservoir P0, T0] -->|V0 approx 0| N[Converging Nozzle Wall]
    N -->|Heat Q removed outward| HS[Heat Sink: maintains T = const]
    N -->|Accelerating flow V increases| T[Throat: M prime = 1 critical]
    T -->|Further reduction in P| EX[Exit section V exit, P exit]

    classDef res fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef noz fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000
    classDef thr fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900,color:#000
    classDef exi fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class R res
    class N,HS noz
    class T thr
    class EX exi
```
