# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Line Energy Equation (Master Relation)

Starting from the **Energy Equation** for steady 1-D flow with heat addition:

$$dQ = dh + V \, dV$$

Multiplying and dividing by V² using V·dV = V²·dV/V:

$$dQ = dh + V^2 \, \frac{dV}{V}$$

Substituting dV/V = -dρ/ρ (continuity):

$$dQ = dh - V^2 \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

Using equation of state P = ρRT and differentiating logarithmically:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dT}{T}$$

Substituting dh = cp dT:

$$dQ = c_p \, dT - V^2 \left(\frac{dP}{P} - \frac{dT}{T}\right)$$

Introducing M² = V²/(γRT) and rearranging:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{(\gamma - 1)M^2}{1 + (\gamma - 1)M^2} \cdot \frac{dQ}{RT}$$

This is the **fundamental Rayleigh relation** linking temperature change to heat addition.

## 3.2 Derivation of T₀/T₀* Using Gas Tables Approach

### Step 1: Start from the isentropic stagnation relation:

$$T_0 = T \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2\right)$$

For the critical state (M = 1):

$$T_0^* = T^* \left(1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}\right) = T^* \cdot \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}$$

### Step 2: Form the ratio T₀/T₀*:

$$\frac{T_0}{T_0^*} = \frac{T}{T^*} \cdot \frac{1 + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} M^2}{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Rayleigh flow T/T* ratio (derived from continuity + momentum + energy):

$$\frac{T}{T^*} = \frac{(1 + \gamma M^2)^2}{(\gamma + 1)^2 M^2 \left[2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of T/T*:** From mass conservation ρV = ρ*V* and using ideal gas law:
> $$\frac{V}{V^*} = \frac{\rho^*}{\rho} = \frac{P^* T}{P T^*}$$
> Squaring and substituting V² = γRT M²:
> $$\frac{\gamma R T M^2}{(\gamma R T^*)} = \left(\frac{P^* T}{P T^*}\right)^2$$
> Combined with the energy equation h₀ = h + V²/2 = constant in differential form, the relation emerges.

### Step 4: Substitute and simplify:

$$\frac{T_0}{T_0^*} = \frac{(1 + \gamma M^2)^2}{(\gamma + 1)^2 M^2 \left[2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2\right]} \cdot \frac{2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2}{\gamma + 1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_0}{T_0^*} = \frac{(1 + \gamma M^2)\left[2 + (\gamma - 1)M^2\right]}{(\gamma + 1)^2 M^2}}$$

**Verification at M = 1:**

$$\frac{T_0}{T_0^*}\bigg|_{M=1} = \frac{(1 + \gamma)(2 + \gamma - 1)}{(\gamma + 1)^2} = \frac{(1+\gamma)^2}{(\gamma+1)^2} = 1 \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Generalised Procedure Using Gas Tables (Variable Specific Heats)

### **Algorithm: Solving Rayleigh Flow with Gas Tables**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** Inlet state (T₁, P₁, M₁) and either heat added (q) or outlet condition.
> **Find:** Outlet state properties.

**Step A — Stagnation Properties at Inlet (using gas tables):**

$$h_{01} = h(T_1) + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h(T_1) + c_{p1} T_1 \cdot \frac{\gamma_1 - 1}{2} M_1^2$$

Or directly using $T_{01}/T_1 = 1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2$, find T₀₁, then use gas tables for h₀₁.

**Step B — Find the critical stagnation temperature T₀₁* corresponding to inlet state:**

Using tabulated Rayleigh flow data or by interpolation:

$$\frac{T_{01}}{T_{01}^*} = f(M_1) \quad \text{(from Rayleigh table)}$$

$$T_{01}^* = \frac{T_{01}}{f(M_1)}$$

**Step C — Apply Energy Equation to find outlet stagnation temperature:**

$$T_{02} = T_{01} + \frac{q}{c_p}$$

For variable specific heats, use the **mean cp** evaluated between T₀₁ and T₀₂, OR use h directly:

$$h_{02} = h_{01} + q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Find } T_{02} \text{ from gas tables (knowing } h_{02}\text{)}$$

**Step D — Find outlet Mach number using Rayleigh table:**

Since stagnation conditions for Rayleigh flow at the inlet and outlet share the **same critical condition** (for isentropic stagnation relations), we have:

$$T_{02}^* = T_{01}^* \quad \text{(because } T_0^* \text{ depends only on inlet stagnation enthalpy for given gas)}$$

Then:

$$\frac{T_{02}}{T_{01}^*} = g(M_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_2 \text{ from table}$$

**Step E — Determine outlet static properties:**

Use isentropic relations (with gas tables):

$$P_{02} = P_{01} + \Delta P_{\text{momentum}} \quad \text{(using } P_0/P_0^* \text{ ratio from Rayleigh table)}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{T_{02}}{1 + \frac{\gamma_2 - 1}{2} M_2^2}, \quad P_2 = \frac{P_{02}}{\left(\frac{T_{02}}{T_2}\right)^{\gamma_2/(\gamma_2-1)}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example Using Gas Tables

> **Problem:** Air enters a constant-area duct at M₁ = 0.6, T₁ = 400 K, P₁ = 300 kPa. Heat is added until M₂ = 0.9. Using gas tables (variable cp), find: (a) q (kJ/kg), (b) T₂, (c) P₂.

### **Solution Using Gas Tables:**

**Step 1 — At State 1 (M₁ = 0.6, T₁ = 400 K):**

From air gas tables at T₁ = 400 K:

$$h_1 = 400.98 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad P_{r1} = 4.153, \quad \gamma_1 \approx 1.395$$

Stagnation temperature:

$$T_{01} = T_1\left(1 + \frac{\gamma_1 - 1}{2} M_1^2\right) = 400 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.395}{2}(0.36)\right) = 400 \times 1.0711 = 428.44 \text{ K}$$

Stagnation pressure (using Pr ratio from gas tables):

$$P_{r0_1} = P_{r1} \times \left(\frac{T_{01}}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma_1/(\gamma_1-1)} = 4.153 \times (1.0711)^{3.495} \approx 4.153 \times 1.2746 \approx 5.293$$

$$P_{01} = P_1 \times \frac{P_{r0_1}}{P_{r1}} = 300 \times \frac{5.293}{4.153} \approx 382.3 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Determine T₀₁* from Rayleigh table at M₁ = 0.6:**

From Rayleigh flow tables (or formula): 

$$\frac{T_{01}}{T_{01}^*} \approx 0.8629 \quad \text{(for } \gamma = 1.4\text{)}$$

$$T_{01}^* = \frac{428.44}{0.8629} \approx 496.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — For State 2 (M₂ = 0.9):**

From Rayleigh flow table at M₂ = 0.9:

$$\frac{T_{02}}{T_{02}^*} \approx 0.9928$$

Since T₀₂* = T₀₁* (same fluid and duct):

$$T_{02} = 0.9928 \times 496.5 \approx 492.9 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Energy Equation:**

$$q = h_{02} - h_{01} = c_{p,\text{mean}}(T_{02} - T_{01})$$

Using mean cp ≈ 1.015 kJ/(kg·K):

$$q = 1.015 \times (492.9 - 428.44) \approx 1.015 \times 64.46 \approx 65.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Static Temperature at State 2:**

Using γ₂ ≈ 1.39 (at T ≈ 450 K):

$$T_2 = \frac{T_{02}}{1 + \frac{0.39}{2}(0.81)} = \frac{492.9}{1.158} \approx 425.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Static Pressure at State 2:**

From Rayleigh table at M₂ = 0.9:

$$\frac{P_{02}}{P_{02}^*} \approx 1.108$$

$P_{02}^* = P_{01}^* \times \frac{P_{r0_1}^*}{P_{r0_1}}$ — requires iterative use of gas tables, often simplified by assuming:

$$P_{02} = P_{01} \times \frac{P_0/P_0^*|_{M_2}}{P_0/P_0^*|_{M_1}}$$

$$P_{02} = 382.3 \times \frac{1.108}{1.193} \approx 355.0 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_2 = \frac{P_{02}}{\left(\frac{T_{02}}{T_2}\right)^{\gamma_2/(\gamma_2-1)}} = \frac{355.0}{(1.158)^{3.462}} \approx \frac{355.0}{1.642} \approx 216.2 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$q \approx 65.4 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad T_2 \approx 425.7 \text{ K}, \quad P_2 \approx 216.2 \text{ kPa}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rayleigh Flow Process Diagram (Mermaid Block Flow)

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete **Rayleigh Flow Analysis Procedure Using Gas Tables**, mapping the functional steps required to solve practical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Inlet State M1, T1, P1 and Heat Added q] --> B[Look up h1, Pr1 from Air Gas Tables at T1]
    B --> C[Compute T01 using Isentropic Relation T01 = T1 times 1 plus gamma-1 over 2 times M1 squared]
    C --> D[Look up Rayleigh Table for T01 over T01* at M1]
    D --> E[Compute Critical Stagnation Temperature T01*]
    E --> F[Apply Energy Equation: h02 = h01 + q]
    F --> G[Find T02 from Gas Tables using h02]
    G --> H[Recall T02* = T01* since same fluid and stagnation enthalpy base]
    H --> I[Compute T02 over T02* ratio]
    I --> J[Look up Rayleigh Table inversely to find M2]
    J --> K{M2 Subsonic or Supersonic}
    K -->|Subsonic M2 less than 1| L[Compute Static T2 using Isentropic Relation]
    K -->|Supersonic M2 greater than 1| L
    L --> M[Compute Static P2 using Gas Tables Pr function]
    M --> N[Verify Mass Conservation: rho1 V1 = rho2 V2]
    N --> O[END: Final State Properties T2, P2, M2 determined]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Rayleigh Flow T-s Diagram Representation (Block Schematic)

The following schematic shows the **functional mapping** between the Rayleigh curve on a T-s diagram and the analytical procedure using gas tables.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THERMO["THERMODYNAMIC STATE SPACE"]
        direction TB
        T1[State 1: M less than 1 Subsonic Unheated]
        T2[State 2: M less than 1 Subsonic Heated up to MAX s]
        T3[State 3: M equals 1 CRITICAL Maximum Entropy Point]

        T5[State 5: M greater than 1 Supersonic MAX TEMP]
    end

    subgraph TABLE["GAS TABLE OPERATIONS"]
        direction TB
        GA[Step A: Look up h, Pr at T1 in Air Tables]
        GB[Step B: Find T01 by Isentropic Formula]
        GC[Step C: Use Rayleigh Table to find T01 over T01*]
        GD[Step D: Find h02 from Energy Equation h01 + q]
        GE[Step E: Find T02 then M2 from Rayleigh Table]
    end

    T1 --> GA
    GA --> GB
    GB --> GC
    GC --> GD
    GD --> GE
    GE --> T2
    GE --> T3
    GE --> T4
    GE --> T5

```

## 4.3 Rayleigh Flow Heat Addition Branches (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Inlet Flow M1, T1, P1] --> BR{Branch Selection Based on M1}
    BR -->|M1 less than 1 Subsonic| SS[Subsonic Heating Branch]
    BR -->|M1 greater than 1 Supersonic| SP[Supersonic Heating Branch]
    BR -->|M1 equals 1 Critical| CR[Already at Critical Choked State]

    SS --> SS1[Heat Addition Increases T and s]
    SS1 --> SS2[M increases toward 1]
    SS2 --> SS3{T reaches MAX at M equals 1}
    SS3 -->|More Heat| SS4[Flow Becomes Supersonic with DECREASING T]

    SP --> SP1[Heat Addition Decreases M toward 1]
    SP1 --> SP2[T decreases toward MIN at M equals 1]
    SP2 -->|More Heat| SP3[Flow Becomes Subsonic with INCREASING T]

    CR --> CR1[Maximum Entropy Point s equals s max]
    CR1 --> CR2[Further Heat: Cannot be analysed via Rayleigh alone - choking occurs]

    style SS fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px
    style SP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Rayleigh flow. State the assumptions made in its analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rayleigh flow** is the steady, one-dimensional, frictionless flow of a compressible fluid through a constant area duct with heat addition or rejection.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. **One-dimensional, steady flow**
> 2. **Constant cross-sectional area** (dA = 0)
> 3. **Frictionless** (inviscid, no shear stress)
> 4. **No shaft work** (Ws = 0)
> 5. **Ideal gas behaviour** (PV = mRT)
> 6. **Heat transfer allowed** (dQ ≠ 0) — this is the distinguishing feature
> 7. **Negligible potential energy changes**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the critical point (M = 1) in Rayleigh flow?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> At the critical point (M = 1) in Rayleigh flow, the flow attains **maximum entropy (s_max)**, and either maximum or minimum static temperature depending on the branch. The stagnation temperature T₀* is the **maximum possible stagnation temperature** that can be achieved through heat addition alone. Beyond this point, additional heat transfer causes the flow to choke, and further heat addition results in unchoking to the opposite Mach regime. This is why gas tables always tabulate ratios T₀/T₀*, P₀/P₀* as functions of M for direct interpolation during problem-solving.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Air enters a constant-area duct at M₁ = 0.5, T₁ = 350 K, P₁ = 200 kPa. Heat is added at a rate of **120 kJ/kg** to the air. Using **gas tables** for air (variable specific heats), determine:
>
> **(a)** The exit Mach number M₂ (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** The exit static temperature T₂ and pressure P₂ (7 marks)
>
> **Given:** For air, use h(T=350K) = 350.49 kJ/kg, Pr(350K) = 3.806, γ ≈ 1.399 at this temperature.

#### **Solution:**

**Part (a): Finding Exit Mach Number M₂**

**Step 1 — Inlet Stagnation Properties:**

$$T_{01} = T_1 \left(1 + \frac{\gamma_1 - 1}{2} M_1^2\right) = 350 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.399}{2} \times 0.25\right) = 350 \times 1.0499 = 367.46 \text{ K}$$

$$h_{01} = h_1 + c_p T_1 \cdot \frac{\gamma_1 - 1}{2} M_1^2 \approx 350.49 + 1.005 \times 350 \times 0.0499 \approx 350.49 + 17.55 \approx 368.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Stagnation Temperature from Rayleigh Table:**

For M₁ = 0.5, from Rayleigh flow tables:

$$\frac{T_{01}}{T_{01}^*} \approx 0.7901$$

$$T_{01}^* = \frac{367.46}{0.7901} = 465.0 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Outlet Stagnation Conditions:**

$$h_{02} = h_{01} + q = 368.04 + 120 = 488.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$T_{02} \approx T_{01} + \frac{q}{c_p} = 367.46 + \frac{120}{1.005} = 367.46 + 119.40 = 486.86 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Find M₂:**

Since T₀₂* = T₀₁* = 465.0 K:

$$\frac{T_{02}}{T_{02}^*} = \frac{486.86}{465.0} = 1.0470$$

From Rayleigh table, T₀/T₀* = 1.047 corresponds to **M ≈ 1.43 (supersonic branch)** OR **M ≈ 0.62 (subsonic branch)**.

Since heating occurs in a constant-area duct from subsonic M₁ = 0.5, the flow accelerates (M increases) up to M = 1, then becomes supersonic. With this heat addition pushing T₀₂/T₀₂* > 1, the **supersonic branch is selected**: 

$$\boxed{M_2 \approx 1.43}$$

**Part (b): Finding T₂ and P₂**

**Step 5 — Static Temperature T₂:**

Using γ₂ ≈ 1.385 (at T ≈ 475 K from gas tables):

$$T_2 = \frac{T_{02}}{1 + \frac{\gamma_2 - 1}{2} M_2^2} = \frac{486.86}{1 + \frac{0.385}{2} \times 2.045}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{486.86}{1 + 0.394} = \frac{486.86}{1.394} \approx 349.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Stagnation Pressure P₀₂:**

From Rayleigh table at M₁ = 0.5: P₀₁/P₀₁* = 1.114
From Rayleigh table at M₂ = 1.43: P₀₂/P₀₂* = 0.520

$$P_{02} = P_{01} \times \frac{P_{02}/P_{02}^*}{P_{01}/P_{01}^*}$$

$$P_{01} = P_1 \times \left(\frac{T_{01}}{T_1}\right)^{\gamma_1/(\gamma_1-1)} = 200 \times (1.0499)^{3.503} \approx 200 \times 1.1857 = 237.1 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{02} = 237.1 \times \frac{0.520}{1.114} = 237.1 \times 0.467 \approx 110.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 7 — Static Pressure P₂:**

$$P_2 = \frac{P_{02}}{\left(\frac{T_{02}}{T_2}\right)^{\gamma_2/(\gamma_2-1)}} = \frac{110.7}{(1.394)^{3.506}}$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 46.5 \text{ kPa}, \quad T_2 \approx 349.3 \text{ K}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> A frictionless, constant-area combustion chamber receives air at M₁ = 0.3, T₁ = 500 K, P₁ = 500 kPa. Combustion adds **180 kJ/kg** of heat. Using **gas tables**:
>
> **(a)** Compute the stagnation enthalpy ratio h₀₁/h₀₁* and the corresponding critical stagnation temperature (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Determine the exit stagnation temperature T₀₂, exit Mach number M₂, and verify whether the flow remains subsonic or becomes supersonic (7 marks)

#### **Solution:**

**Part (a): Critical Stagnation Enthalpy Ratio**

**Step 1 — Inlet Stagnation Properties:**

At T₁ = 500 K from air gas tables: h₁ = 503.02 kJ/kg, Pr₁ = 8.411

$$T_{01} = 500 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.394}{2} \times 0.09\right) = 500 \times 1.01773 = 508.87 \text{ K}$$

$$h_{01} = 503.02 + 1.015 \times 500 \times 0.01773 \approx 503.02 + 9.00 = 512.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Stagnation Enthalpy (Rayleigh Table Lookup):**

For M₁ = 0.3, from Rayleigh flow tables:

$$\frac{h_{01}}{h_{01}^*} = \frac{T_{01}}{T_{01}^*} \approx 0.6345$$

$$h_{01}^* = \frac{512.02}{0.6345} \approx 806.97 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$T_{01}^* \approx \frac{508.87}{0.6345} \approx 802.0 \text{ K}$$

**Part (b): Exit Conditions and Flow Regime**

**Step 3 — Outlet Stagnation Enthalpy:**

$$h_{02} = h_{01} + q = 512.02 + 180 = 692.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$T_{02} \approx T_{01} + \frac{q}{c_{p,\text{mean}}} = 508.87 + \frac{180}{1.020} \approx 508.87 + 176.47 \approx 685.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Outlet Stagnation Enthalpy Ratio:**

$$\frac{h_{02}}{h_{02}^*} = \frac{h_{02}}{h_{01}^*} = \frac{692.02}{806.97} = 0.8575$$

**Step 5 — Find M₂ from Rayleigh Table:**

From Rayleigh flow tables, h₀/h₀* = 0.8575 corresponds to **M ≈ 0.83 (subsonic branch)**.

Since the flow started at M₁ = 0.3 (subsonic) and has been heated, M increases toward 1 but does not exceed it here because h₀₂/h₀₂* < 1.

**Verification:** h₀₂/h₀₂* = 0.8575 < 1, confirming the flow is on the **subsonic branch** of the Rayleigh curve.

**Step 6 — Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{M_2 \approx 0.83, \quad T_{02} \approx 685.3 \text{ K}, \quad \text{Flow remains SUBSONIC}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Rayleigh Flow Problems Using Gas Tables:**
>
> 1. **Branch Selection Error:** When finding M₂ after heat addition, students often pick the wrong branch (subsonic vs supersonic). Always verify: if heating brings the flow past M = 1, the supersonic branch must be chosen. **Check whether T₀₂/T₀₂* > 1 or < 1.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting Critical Condition Invariance:** Many students incorrectly assume T₀₂* ≠ T₀₁*. In Rayleigh flow, **T₀* and h₀* are invariant** for a given working fluid and inlet stagnation state — this is the cornerstone of using gas tables.
>
> 3. **Using Constant γ Mistakenly:** When the problem states "use gas tables," students must NOT use γ = 1.4 everywhere. **Interpolate γ and cp from gas tables** corresponding to local temperatures.
>
> 4. **Unit Confusion in q:** Heat added q is per unit mass in kJ/kg. Ensure cp is also in kJ/(kg·K), not J/(kg·K).
>
> 5. **Isentropic vs Rayleigh Confusion:** For Rayleigh flow, P₀/P₀* and T₀/T₀* are **NOT isentropic ratios** — they include the heat addition effect. Don't confuse them with standard isentropic flow tables.
>
> 6. **Neglecting Mass Conservation Check:** Always verify ρ₁V₁ = ρ₂V₂ (mass flow rate constant in constant-area duct). This catches computational errors immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Quick Revision Checklist — Use of Gas Tables for Rayleigh Flow**

- **Definition:** Rayleigh flow = frictionless, 1-D, constant-area duct with heat transfer.
- **Master Energy Equation:** $q = h_{02} - h_{01}$ (use gas tables directly).
- **Critical Condition:** At M = 1, $T_0/T_0^* = 1$ and $s = s_{\max}$.
- **Key Invariant:** $T_{02}^* = T_{01}^*$ (same fluid, same inlet stagnation base).
- **Tabulated Quantities in Rayleigh Tables:** $T_0/T_0^*$, $P_0/P_0^*$, $T/T^*$, $P/P^*$, all as functions of M.
- **Heating in Subsonic Branch:** Flow accelerates, M → 1, T rises, P falls, ρ falls.
- **Heating in Supersonic Branch:** Flow decelerates, M → 1, T rises, P rises, ρ rises.
- **Maximum Heat Transfer:** $q_{\max} = c_p(T_0^* - T_{01})$ — occurs at the choking point M = 1.
- **Variable cp:** Gas tables handle this automatically — never assume cp = constant when "use gas tables" is specified.
- **Bridge Formula:** $T_0 = T(1 + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}M^2)$ connects stagnation and static properties.
- **Branch Selection Rule:** $T_{02}/T_{02}^* > 1 \Rightarrow$ supersonic; $T_{02}/T_{02}^* < 1 \Rightarrow$ subsonic.
- **Mass Conservation:** $\rho_1 V_1 = \rho_2 V_2$ — always holds in constant-area duct.
- **Entropy Maximum at M = 1:** Additional heat beyond choking causes flow unchoking phenomena.

> **🎯 Most Important Takeaway for KTU Exams:**
> Master the procedure: **(1)** Find inlet stagnation properties from gas tables → **(2)** Find T₀₁* using Rayleigh table at M₁ → **(3)** Compute T₀₂ using energy equation → **(4)** Compute T₀₂/T₀₂* ratio → **(5)** Inverse lookup to find M₂ → **(6)** Find static properties. Practice this 5-step algorithm until it becomes second nature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of shock wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET632 - COMPRESSIBLE FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Development of shock wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shock Wave:** A shock wave is an extremely thin, almost discontinuous surface across which there is an abrupt, finite change in flow properties — pressure, temperature, density, velocity, and Mach number. Across this surface, the thermodynamic and kinematic state of the gas changes almost instantaneously over a distance of the order of a few molecular mean free paths (typically **10⁻⁵ cm** for air at standard conditions).

**Development of a shock wave** refers to the progressive process by which small, finite-amplitude compression disturbances, originally of infinitesimal strength, accumulate and coalesce in a supersonic flow field until they steepen into a near-discontinuous jump — i.e., a fully formed shock.

The governing flow regime is **supersonic** ($M > 1$), and the fundamental cause of shock formation is the **nonlinear wave-speed dependence on local flow properties** — specifically, the fact that a pressure disturbance travels at a speed equal to the local **speed of sound relative to the fluid**, not relative to a fixed laboratory frame.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The River Rapids" Intuition

Imagine a wide, calm river (subsonic flow) suddenly squeezed between rocks into a narrow gorge (converging channel). As the water (fluid) accelerates into the gorge, a small ripple created upstream travels *slowly* relative to the oncoming current. The ripple is eventually *overtaken* by faster water behind it, the crest piles up, and a steep, breaking wave (a *hydraulic jump* — the surface-water analog of a shock) forms.

In the **gas dynamic case**:
- Subsonic flow ⇒ disturbances propagate *both* upstream and downstream (sound waves outrun the flow and warn the upstream gas).
- Supersonic flow ⇒ disturbances cannot propagate upstream — they are all swept downstream by the flow.
- When a supersonic flow is **decelerated** (e.g., by an adverse pressure gradient), the *compression* signals from downstream run *faster* than those from upstream, because $a + V$ (the downstream-facing wave speed) increases as the gas is compressed and accelerated into the disturbance.
- This chasing effect **steepens** the wavefront progressively until it becomes a near-vertical shock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** A shock wave is *not* created instantaneously by some external "shock maker." It is the **natural, inevitable consequence** of nonlinear acoustic wave propagation in a supersonic stream that experiences sufficient compression.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value (Sea-Level Air) |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat ratio | $\gamma$ | **1.4** |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Speed of sound at 288.15 K | $a_0$ | **340.29 m/s** |
| Mean free path (air) | $\lambda$ | **≈ 6.6 × 10⁻⁸ m** |
| Typical shock thickness | $\delta$ | **≈ 4λ ≈ 10⁻⁵ cm** |

---

## 1.4 Why Study This Topic?

The development of shock waves governs:

- **Aircraft design** — wave drag, transonic buffet, sonic-boom generation
- **Rocket nozzle design** — over/under-expansion, shock-induced separation
- **Steam/gas turbines** — blade-row shock losses
- **Wind tunnel testing** — shock formation inside test sections
- **Scramjet / ramjet inlet design** — oblique shock systems

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us consider a piston moving into a gas column at the *right-hand closed end* of a long duct, with the gas initially at rest at Mach number $M_1 = 0$ (or moving at $M_1 > 0$ in the open case). The piston is pushed in such a way that a small compression disturbance is generated.

1. **Disturbance emitted at $t = 0$**: The piston pushes the gas; a small pressure pulse travels to the right (downstream) at speed $a_1 + V_1$ relative to the laboratory. In a *uniform* supersonic stream, this pulse moves at the **simple-wave** speed $V_1 + a_1$ if it propagates downstream, and $V_1 - a_1$ if upstream.

2. **Emission of a second pulse slightly later ($t = \Delta t$)**: Because the gas just behind the first pulse is now *slightly compressed and accelerated*, the local speed of sound is *higher* ($a \propto \sqrt{T}$, and $T$ rises on compression). Hence the second pulse travels at $V_2 + a_2 > V_1 + a_1$.

3. **Chasing phenomenon**: The second pulse *catches up* to the first. Their separation decreases over time. The wave profile, originally smooth, develops a *steepening* trailing edge (compression side).

4. **Singularity formation**: After a finite distance $x_s$ (the **shock-formation distance**), the trailing slope becomes vertical — mathematically, $\partial p / \partial x \to -\infty$. Physically, dissipative mechanisms (viscosity, thermal conduction) intervene *just before* this singularity, regularising the profile into a thin shock of finite thickness $\delta \sim \lambda$.

5. **Steady-state shock**: For a continuous source of compression (a steadily accelerating piston, or a wedge in supersonic flow), a stationary shock attaches to the geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why expansion fans do NOT form shocks:** In an *expansion* wave, the rear of the wave travels *slower* than the front (because $T$ and $a$ drop). Hence expansion waves *spread out* (rarefaction fans) — they never coalesce into a discontinuity. Only **compression waves** steepen into shocks.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — The Method of Characteristics (Brief)

For a 1-D unsteady isentropic flow of a perfect gas, the governing equations are expressible along **characteristics** $dx/dt = V \pm a$. The Riemann invariants are:

$$J_\pm = V \pm \frac{2a}{\gamma - 1} = \text{constant along } C_\pm \text{ characteristics}$$

For a **simple wave** propagating into a uniform region (e.g., a piston-generated wave moving into gas at rest), one family of characteristics is straight lines. Compatibility along $C_+$ gives:

$$V + \frac{2a}{\gamma - 1} = \text{const}$$

Differentiating implicitly with respect to $x$ at constant $t$ along the simple-wave region gives the **slope** of each $C_+$ characteristic:

$$\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)_{C_+} = V + a = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2} V + \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} V_1$$

where the last equality uses $a_1 + V_1 = $ initial wave speed. This expression is the basis for deriving the shock-formation distance.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle a = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Local speed of sound |
| 2 | $\displaystyle c_{\pm} = V \pm a$ | Characteristic speeds |
| 3 | $\displaystyle J_\pm = V \pm \dfrac{2a}{\gamma - 1}$ | Riemann invariants |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \frac{a_0^2 - a^2}{\gamma - 1} + \frac{V^2}{2} = \text{const}$ | 1-D unsteady energy equation |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)_{C_+} = V + a = \frac{\gamma+1}{2}V + \frac{\gamma-1}{2}V_1$ | Characteristic slope in simple compression wave |
| 6 | $\displaystyle x_s = \frac{2 a_1^3}{(\gamma+1)\,\left|\dfrac{dV_1}{dt}\right|}$ | **Shock-formation distance** (for constant-deceleration piston) |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \Delta x_s = \frac{2 \, M_1^3 \, \left(M_1^2 - 1\right)^{1/2}}{\gamma + 1} \cdot \frac{a_1^3}{\left|\dot V_1\right|}$ | Generalised form with freestream Mach $M_1$ |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\left(M_1^2 - 1\right)$ | Weak-shock limit (linearised) |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \theta = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}\,\tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}\frac{M_1^2-1}{2\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)}-\tan^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{M_1^2-1}{2\left(1+\frac{\gamma-1}{2}M_1^2\right)}}$ | Prandtl–Meyer angle $\nu(M)$ |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \frac{dA}{A} = (M^2 - 1)\frac{dV}{V}$ | Area-velocity relation (quasi-1D) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** Formula **6** and **7** are the *single most-tested* result for this topic in KTU ESE. Memorise the derivation, not just the formula — the question is usually a "derive the shock formation distance" type.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concorde / fighter jets:** Transonic shock formation on wing determines critical Mach number and drag-divergence Mach number.
- **Scramjet inlets:** Oblique shock waves (developed from compression corners) compress incoming air; *where* they intersect dictates combustor entrance conditions.
- **Wind-tunnel design:** The throat of a supersonic nozzle is precisely where the *first* shock forms if the back-pressure is wrong — governing the starting/unsteady behaviour of the tunnel.
- **Implosion dynamics:** Strong shocks in inertial-confinement fusion (relevant to plasma physics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Characteristic Slope in a Simple Compression Wave

### Setup

Consider a long duct filled with gas at rest, $V_1 = 0$, $T = T_1$, $a = a_1$. At $x = 0$, $t = 0$, a piston begins to move into the gas with a small velocity $V_p(t)$. We wish to find the slope $(dx/dt)_{C_+}$ of the $C_+$ characteristics within the simple-wave region generated by the piston.

### Step 1 — Identify the simple wave

Because the gas ahead of the wave is uniform and at rest, the Riemann invariant $J_+$ is constant on $C_+$ characteristics in the simple-wave region:

$$V + \frac{2a}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{2 a_1}{\gamma - 1}$$

This is the standard Riemann-invariant statement for a *right-running* ($C_+$) wave.

### Step 2 — Solve for $a$ in terms of $V$

Rearrange the invariant:

$$\frac{2a}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{2a_1}{\gamma - 1} - V$$

$$a = a_1 - \frac{\gamma - 1}{2} V$$

This shows that as the gas is *accelerated* (compression wave, $V > 0$), the local speed of sound *decreases* in the *lab* if $V_1 = 0$... wait — careful: this relation is for the lab-frame $a$ where $a_1$ is the *lab* sound speed of the undisturbed gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign check:** In a **compression wave**, $V$ increases along the gas path. The local sound speed $a$ relative to the lab decreases, but this is because the gas is decelerating *relative to the wave front*. The wave *crest speed* $V + a$ (which is what matters) **increases** with $V$.

### Step 3 — Compute the wave-crest speed

The $C_+$ characteristic passing through a point moves in the lab frame at speed:

$$\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)_{C_+} = V + a$$

Substituting the Step 2 result:

$$\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)_{C_+} = V + a_1 - \frac{\gamma - 1}{2}V = a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}V$$

This is the **slope of each individual $C_+$ characteristic** in the $(x,t)$ plane. Note that the slope *increases linearly with $V$* — a larger local velocity produces a faster-traveling characteristic.

### Step 4 — Express in terms of the initial wave speed

The very first characteristic (emitted at $t=0$ when $V = 0$ if the piston starts from rest) has slope $a_1$. A characteristic emitted at later time $t = \tau$ when the piston velocity was $V_p(\tau)$ has slope:

$$\left(\frac{dx}{dt}\right)_{C_+(\tau)} = a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}V_p(\tau)$$

For a uniformly accelerated piston $V_p(\tau) = \dot V_p \cdot \tau$, the characteristic slopes increase *linearly* with emission time $\tau$.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Shock-Formation Distance $x_s$

### Setup

A piston in a uniform gas initially at rest is *accelerated* into the gas with constant acceleration $a_p$ (here, the symbol $a_p$ means $\dot V_p$, **not** the speed of sound — to avoid confusion, we use $\dot V_p$). Determine the distance at which characteristics first intersect, signalling shock formation.

### Step 1 — Equation of the $\tau$-characteristic

The $C_+$ characteristic emitted at time $\tau$ from $x = 0$ moves in the lab frame with the constant slope derived in §3.1, Step 4. For a constant-acceleration piston:

$$V_p(\tau) = \dot V_p \,\tau$$

Characteristic slope:

$$m(\tau) = a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2} \dot V_p \,\tau$$

Equation of the characteristic line in the $(x, t)$ plane:

$$x = m(\tau)\,(t - \tau) = \left[a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau\right](t - \tau)$$

### Step 2 — Condition for characteristic intersection (caustic formation)

Two characteristics, emitted at times $\tau$ and $\tau + d\tau$, intersect when their $(x,t)$ loci coincide. Setting $\partial x / \partial \tau = 0$ gives the envelope condition:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \tau}\bigg|_{t} = 0$$

Compute the partial derivative:

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial \tau}\bigg|_{t} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,(t - \tau) + \left[a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau\right](-1) = 0$$

Simplify:

$$\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,(t - \tau) = a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau$$

$$\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,t = a_1 + (\gamma + 1)\dot V_p\,\tau$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{a_1}{\frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p} + 2\tau = \frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + 2\tau$$

### Step 3 — Compute $x_s$

Substitute this $t$ back into the characteristic equation:

$$x_s = \left[a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau\right]\left(\frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + 2\tau - \tau\right)$$

$$x_s = \left[a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau\right]\left(\frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + \tau\right)$$

Expand:

$$x_s = \frac{2 a_1^2}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + a_1\,\tau + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau^2 + (\gamma + 1)\dot V_p\,\tau^2 \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

Wait — let me redo the expansion carefully:

$$x_s = \left[a_1 + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau\right]\left[\frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + \tau\right]$$

$$= a_1 \cdot \frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + a_1 \cdot \tau + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau \cdot \frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau \cdot \tau$$

$$= \frac{2 a_1^2}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + a_1\,\tau + a_1\,\tau + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau^2$$

$$= \frac{2 a_1^2}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p} + 2 a_1\,\tau + \frac{\gamma + 1}{2}\dot V_p\,\tau^2$$

### Step 4 — Find the *minimum* $x_s$ (shock forms at the earliest intersection)

To find when the shock *first* forms, differentiate $x_s$ with respect to $\tau$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d x_s}{d\tau} = 2 a_1 + (\gamma + 1)\dot V_p\,\tau = 0$$

$$\tau^* = -\frac{2 a_1}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p}$$

A *negative* time $\tau^*$ is unphysical — it means the characteristic intersection first occurs at $\tau = 0$, i.e., **at the piston face itself**. This makes sense: a piston accelerating *into* a quiescent gas (no inflow) creates the steepest gradient right at the wall.

The shock-formation distance therefore is obtained by evaluating $x_s$ at $\tau = 0$:

$$\boxed{\,x_s = \frac{2 a_1^2}{(\gamma + 1)\dot V_p}\,}$$

Since $\dot V_p > 0$ (acceleration into the gas), $x_s > 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative form (textbook):** Using $a_1 = M_1 \cdot \text{?}$ — note that if the gas is *not* initially at rest but flowing at $M_1$, then $a_1$ in the formula above is replaced by an effective wave speed, and the result is the more general formula in row 7 of the cheat-sheet table.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Weak-Shock Jump Condition (Linearised Limit)

For a *weak* shock ($M_1 - 1 \ll 1$, i.e., $M_1$ just above unity), expand the exact normal-shock Rankine–Hugoniot relation:

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1}(M_1^2 - 1) + \frac{\gamma(2-\gamma)}{(\gamma+1)^2}(M_1^2-1)^2 + \cdots$$

Dropping higher-order terms:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{p_2}{p_1} \approx 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma + 1}(M_1^2 - 1)\,}$$

This is the **acoustic (linearised) approximation**, valid for $M_1 \lesssim 1.3$.

A useful corollary for the density jump (since $M_1 \approx 1$):

$$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} \approx \frac{(\gamma + 1) M_1^2}{(\gamma - 1) M_1^2 + 2}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A supersonic wind-tunnel test section has $M_1 = 1.6$, $p_1 = 0.5 \,\text{atm}$, $T_1 = 300\,\text{K}$. A small wedge of half-angle $\theta = 5°$ generates a weak oblique shock. Compute the post-shock pressure using (a) the exact $\theta$–$\beta$–$M$ relation, (b) the linearised weak-shock approximation.

### (a) Exact method (qualitative steps; details beyond KTU Module 4 scope)

Use the $\theta$-$\beta$-$M$ chart or solve iteratively:

$$\tan\theta = 2\cot\beta \frac{M_1^2 \sin^2\beta - 1}{M_1^2(\gamma + \cos 2\beta) + 2}$$

For $M_1 = 1.6$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\theta = 5°$: $\beta \approx 38°$ (weak-shock root).

Then $M_{1n} = M_1 \sin\beta \approx 1.6 \times 0.616 \approx 0.985$ — *subsonic normal component*, consistent with an attached oblique shock.

$$M_{2n}^2 = \frac{(\gamma-1)M_{1n}^2 + 2}{2\gamma M_{1n}^2 - (\gamma-1)} = \frac{0.4(0.970) + 2}{2(1.4)(0.970) - 0.4} = \frac{2.388}{2.316} = 1.031$$

$M_{2n} \approx 1.015$, so $p_2/p_1 = 1 + \frac{2(1.4)}{2.4}(0.970 - 1) = 1 + 1.167 \times (-0.030) = 0.965$

Wait — that's wrong; an oblique shock should *raise* pressure. Re-check: for a *normal* shock with $M_{1n} = 0.985 < 1$, no shock exists. The correct $M_{1n}$ must be $> 1$. Re-solve: with $\beta = 38°$ and $M_1 = 1.6$, $\sin\beta = 0.616$, so $M_{1n} = 0.985$ — this is indeed subsonic. The actual oblique shock root for $M_1 = 1.6$, $\theta = 5°$ is $\beta \approx 55°$ giving $M_{1n} \approx 1.31$. Iterating once:

$$\tan 5° = 0.0875, \quad \cot\beta = 0.700$$

Try $\beta = 55°$: $\sin 55° = 0.819$, $M_{1n} = 1.310$, $\cos 2\beta = \cos 110° = -0.342$.

$$\text{RHS} = 2(0.700)\frac{(2.56)(0.671) - 1}{2.56(1.4 - 0.342) + 2} = 1.4 \cdot \frac{0.717}{3.252 + 2} = 1.4 \cdot \frac{0.717}{5.252} = 0.191$$

Hmm, this gives $\tan\theta = 0.191$, i.e., $\theta = 10.8°$, larger than 5°. So $\beta = 55°$ is too large. The weak root for $M_1 = 1.6, \theta = 5°$ is around $\beta \approx 46°$. For brevity we stop here — this is intended to illustrate the **iterative procedure**, not to be solved fully.

### (b) Linearised approximation

$$\frac{p_2}{p_1} \approx 1 + \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}(M_1^2 - 1) = 1 + \frac{2.8}{2.4}(2.56 - 1) = 1 + 1.167 \times 1.56 = 2.82$$

So $p_2 \approx 1.41 \,\text{atm}$. The linearised result overestimates because $M_1 = 1.6$ is *not* close to 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Linearised weak-shock formula is valid only for $M_1 \lesssim 1.3$. For larger $M_1$, full Rankine–Hugoniot relations must be used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Characteristic Steepening and Shock Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas initially at rest, V sub1 equals 0, a equals a sub1] --> B[Piston begins moving into gas at x equals 0, t equals 0]
    B --> C[First compression pulse emitted, travels at speed a sub1]
    C --> D[Piston accelerates further, emits second pulse at t equals delta t]
    D --> E[Second pulse travels faster: V sub p plus a sub 2 greater than a sub 1]
    E --> F[Pulse 2 catches Pulse 1, gradient steepens]
    F --> G{Finite distance x sub s reached}
    G -->|Yes, idealised| H[Singularity: dp/dx equals minus infinity]
    G -->|Real gas, dissipative| I[Thin shock of thickness delta equals 4 lambda]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Steady shock attached to geometry in supersonic flow]

    classDef process fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F process
    class G decision
    class H,I,J final
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shock Formation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Piston or Geometry Corner] -->|Causes compression| B[Disturbance Generation Block]
    B --> C[Finite-Amplitude Compression Waves]
    C --> D[Nonlinear Wave-Speed Block: V+a increases with compression]
    D --> E[Characteristic Convergence Block]
    E --> F[Wave Steepening Block]
    F --> G{Dissipation Active?}
    G -->|No, idealised| H[Singularity Formation at x sub s]
    G -->|Yes, real gas| I[Shock Regularisation: thickness 4 lambda]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Steady Shock Wave]

    classDef block fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F block
    class G gate
    class H,I,J endnode
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Comparison — Compression vs. Expansion Wave Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COMP [Compression Wave Branch]
        C1[Compress gas: T rises, a rises] --> C2[Crest speed V+a rises with V]
        C2 --> C3[Trailing edge catches leading edge]
        C3 --> C4[Wave steepens progressively]
        C4 --> C5[SHOCK FORMS at finite x sub s]
    end

    subgraph EXP [Expansion Wave Branch]
        E1[Expand gas: T drops, a drops] --> E2[Crest speed V+a drops with V]
        E2 --> E3[Trailing edge lags behind leading edge]
        E3 --> E4[Wave spreads out into a rarefaction fan]
        E4 --> E5[NO SHOCK FORMS: smooth fan]
    end

    classDef comp fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef exp fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 comp
    class E1,E2,E3,E4,E5 exp
```

## 4.4 Diagram: x-t Plane Showing Characteristic Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[x-axis horizontal, t-axis vertical] --> B[Family of straight-line C-plus characteristics emanating from x=0]
    B --> C[Each line slope = a sub1 plus gamma plus 1 over 2 times V sub p of tau]
    B --> D[For accelerating piston, slopes increase with emission time tau]
    D --> E[Characteristics converge to a caustic envelope]
    E --> F[Envelope curve marks x sub s: shock formation locus]
    F --> G[Beyond envelope: multi-valued region invalid for smooth flow]
    G --> H[Dissipation regularises the multivalued region into a thin shock]

    classDef ax fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef geom fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    class C,D,E,F geom

```
